docs.title: community
docs.text:
The community repository is a comprehensive collection of configurations, scripts, and tools that extend the functionality of the Talon voice control system. It provides a wide array of features, from text manipulation and mouse control to application-specific integrations and language support for programming.

The repository has several key dependencies:

  • Talon: The core application that interprets voice commands and executes actions based on those commands. The repository provides configuration, actions, and scripts that heavily rely on the Talon API.
  • Python: Many of the scripts in this repository are written in Python, which is used to implement complex logic and interact with the operating system. Python is also used to create data structures that Talon uses to perform voice actions.
  • Pytest: This is used to run unit tests to ensure that the Python code behaves as expected.
  • Cubeb: This is a Python library used for selecting microphone inputs dynamically.

The repository is organized into several key directories:

  • test: This directory contains the unit tests and the stubs for the Talon API necessary to run the tests. It allows developers to ensure code quality without requiring a full Talon environment.
  • migration_helpers: This directory provides scripts for converting CSV files to .talon-list files. This script is used by the community to migrate from older versions of the user configuration to the new .talon-list based system.
  • plugin: This directory contains a large variety of plugins that provide additional functionality to Talon, ranging from text manipulation, to gamepad control, and even features for subtitle generation.
  • apps: This directory is dedicated to application-specific configurations, providing tailored voice commands and actions for different programs and tools. This includes various IDEs, web browsers, terminal applications, document viewers, and more.
  • .github: This directory contains configuration files for GitHub Actions, including CI workflows and dependency management.
  • lang: This directory houses language-specific configurations, providing support for various programming languages, including syntax highlighting and code manipulation. This includes C, C#, CSS, Elixir, HTML, Java, Javascript, Kotlin, Lua, Markdown, PHP, Proto, Python, R, Ruby, Scala, SQL, Stata, Terraform, Typescript, and Vimscript.
  • tags: This directory contains reusable tags and actions that are used to build application and language specific functionality, such as file management, multiple cursors, and terminal support.
  • core: This directory contains core functionality and utilities, including text manipulation, mouse control, app switching, number parsing, and more. It is the central component of the user configuration.

The repository also contains various files in the root directory such as: * requirements-dev.txt: A list of dependencies used during development (e.g. pytest). * .editorconfig: Configuration for consistent code formatting across different editors. * LICENSE: The license for the project. * .gitignore: A list of files and directories to ignore in git commits. * pyproject.toml: Configuration for black, isort and pytest and pyright * CONTRIBUTING.md: Guidelines for contributing to the project. * .pre-commit-config.yaml: Configuration for pre-commit hooks that automatically format and lint code. * README.md: The primary documentation for the repository. * BREAKING_CHANGES.txt: A record of breaking changes that might affect users.

The following sections will go into more detail about each subdirectory and the most important files.

docs.title: .github
docs.text:
The community/.github directory contains configuration files for GitHub.

The workflows subdirectory contains workflow definitions for GitHub Actions. The most important file there is ci.yml, which defines the Continuous Integration (CI) workflow. This workflow is triggered on every push to the main branch and on every pull request targeting the main branch. It performs the following steps:

  • Checks out the code using actions/checkout@v4.
  • Sets up Python 3.9, using actions/setup-python@v5, and caches the pip dependencies for faster subsequent runs.
  • Installs development dependencies from requirements-dev.txt.
  • Runs pytest to execute tests and ensure the quality of the code.

This CI workflow ensures that every change to the main branch is tested, which will help prevent regressions.

The dependabot.yml file configures Dependabot to automatically update GitHub Actions dependencies daily. This helps keep the project's dependencies up to date with the latest security patches and features.

docs.title: workflows
docs.text:
The community/.github/workflows directory contains workflow definitions for GitHub Actions.

The most important file here is ci.yml, which defines the Continuous Integration (CI) workflow. This workflow is triggered on every push to the main branch and on every pull request targeting the main branch. It performs the following steps:

  • Checks out the code using actions/checkout@v4.
  • Sets up Python 3.9, using actions/setup-python@v5, and caches the pip dependencies for faster subsequent runs.
  • Installs development dependencies from requirements-dev.txt.
  • Runs pytest to execute tests and ensure the quality of the code.

This CI workflow ensures that every change to the main branch is tested, which will help prevent regressions.

docs.title: apps
docs.text:
The community/apps directory contains a collection of subdirectories, each dedicated to extending Talon's functionality for a specific application or tool. These subdirectories typically contain a combination of .talon files (for defining voice commands) and .py files (for implementing actions and defining application contexts).

The files and subdirectories within community/apps provide a wide range of functionality, from controlling web browsers and IDEs to interacting with terminal applications and window managers.

Here's a breakdown of the most important subdirectories:

  • talon: This directory contains subdirectories that provide functionality for interacting with the Talon application itself:
    • talon/talon_repl provides support for using Talon within the Talon REPL, allowing users to interact with the Talon environment using voice commands, run previous commands, and output the value of actions, lists, scopes, tags, settings and modes, as well as lists of running applications and open windows. The primary files are talon_repl.py and talon_repl.talon.
    • talon/talon_debug_window provides support for interacting with the Talon Debug window, a tool for debugging Talon scripts. It defines commands for interacting with the debug window, such as inserting actions, lists, and settings. The primary files are talon_debug_window.py and talon_debug_window.talon.
  • vscode: This directory provides comprehensive support for using Talon with VS Code, including mechanisms to communicate with VS Code, and a large set of commands for most of VS Code's major features.
    • vscode/command_client is a crucial subdirectory providing the underlying implementation for sending commands to VS Code, using a file-based RPC mechanism. The files in this directory define actions to run commands via the RPC client or the command palette, as well as a tag to enable the command client.
    • vscode/command_client/rpc_client is the subdirectory that implements the low-level file-based RPC mechanism used to communicate with VS Code. It handles the details of writing requests to temporary files, triggering the command, and reading the response.
    • vscode.py defines the core actions and context for VS Code including app matching, opening/closing tabs and windows, code editing, and actions using the command client.
    • vscode_terminal.talon defines a tag to activate the terminal commands when the VS Code terminal is focused.
    • vscode.talon defines a vast array of voice commands for controlling various features of VS Code, such as the sidebar, panels, settings, snippets, code navigation, git, testing, debugging and terminals.
  • Browser Support: Several subdirectories provide support for various web browsers, including:
    • chrome: Provides support for Google Chrome, including a tag, and custom actions for tab management and showing extensions, in chrome.py, and voice commands in chrome.talon.
    • firefox: Provides support for Mozilla Firefox, with platform-specific implementations for macOS, Windows, and Linux. The primary files are firefox.py, firefox_mac.py, firefox_win_linux.py, and firefox.talon.
    • brave: Provides support for the Brave browser, with a focus on custom actions for tab management in brave.py and voice commands in brave.talon.
    • safari: Provides support for Safari on macOS. It defines actions for getting the URL, reloading the page, managing downloads, and showing extensions. The primary files are safari.py and safari.talon.
    • edge: Provides support for Microsoft Edge, defining the application context in edge.py and enabling the browser tag in edge.talon.
    • opera: Provides support for Opera, including platform-specific implementations for macOS and Windows/Linux in opera_mac.py and opera_win_linux.py, along with the core application definition in opera.py.
    • orion: Provides support for the Orion browser on macOS, enabling tab management and other functionality. The primary files are orion.py and orion.talon.
    • vivaldi: Provides support for the Vivaldi browser, with core actions in vivaldi.py, platform specific overrides for macOS in vivaldi_mac.py and voice commands in vivaldi.talon.
  • Terminal Support: Multiple subdirectories provide support for different terminal applications, each implementing its own application specific actions:
    • apple_terminal: Provides support for the macOS Terminal application, with a focus on file management and command navigation in apple_terminal.py and voice commands in apple_terminal.talon.
    • conemu: Provides basic support for the ConEmu terminal application on Windows using conemu.talon.
    • gnome_terminal: Provides support for the Gnome Terminal application on Linux, defining the application, implementing tab management, and overriding editing actions in gnome_terminal.py and setting the application context and tags in gnome_terminal.talon.
    • guake: Provides support for the Guake terminal application on Linux, defining tab management actions in guake_linux.py.
    • iterm: Provides support for iTerm2 on macOS with a focus on tab management and terminal actions in iterm.py and application context and tags in iterm.talon.
    • kde_konsole: Provides support for the KDE Konsole terminal emulator, including tab and window management and action overrides in kde_konsole.py and activating appropriate tags in kde_konsole.talon.
    • mintty: Provides support for the mintty terminal application on Windows, focusing on file management and terminal actions in mintty_win.py.
    • termite: Provides basic support for the termite terminal application with tagging for git, kubectl, and taskwarrior. The primary file is termite.talon.
    • terminator: Provides support for the Terminator terminal application on Linux, focusing on tab and split management with overrides for editing actions in terminator_linux.py.
    • windows_terminal: Provides support for Windows Terminal, with tab management, edit actions and file manager support in windows_terminal.py and voice commands in windows_terminal.talon.
    • wsl: Provides support for the Windows Subsystem for Linux, including path conversion, WSL distro detection, and various file manager actions in wsl.py and voice commands and tags in wsl.talon.
    • windows_command_processor: Provides support for the Windows Command Processor with core actions in command_processor_win.py and some supplemental and redundant actions in command_processor_actions_win.py.
  • IDEs and Code Editors: Several subdirectories offer support for various IDEs and code editors:
    • jetbrains: Provides support for JetBrains IDEs, using a HTTP API to control the IDE. The core functionality is in jetbrains.py and voice commands are in jetbrains.talon.
    • eclipse: Provides support for the Eclipse IDE, with action implementations in eclipse_win.py and voice commands in eclipse.talon.
    • emacs: Provides extensive support for the Emacs text editor, including code for handling meta keys, and a csv file listing commands in emacs.py, emacs_commands.csv, and emacs_commands.py and voice commands in emacs.talon.
    • notepad++: Provides support for the Notepad++ text editor on Windows, implementing various actions like navigation, code editing, and find and replace in notepad++_win.py.
    • rstudio: Provides support for the RStudio IDE on macOS, with voice commands defined in rstudio_mac.talon.
    • visualstudio: Provides extensive support for Microsoft Visual Studio, including code editing, refactoring, and debugging, using visual_studio.py, visual_studio.talon, and visual_studio_win.py.
  • Document Viewers: Several directories provide support for various document viewers:
    • adobe: Provides support for Adobe Acrobat Reader DC, with platform specific implementations for Windows in adobe_acrobat_reader_dc_win.py, application definitions in adobe_acrobat_reader_dc.py and tags in adobe_acrobat_reader_dc.talon.
    • atril: Provides support for the Atril document viewer on Linux, with an implementation of page navigation actions in atril.py and enabling of the page tag in atril.talon.
    • calibre: Provides support for the Calibre e-book library manager and viewer, with app definitions in calibre.py and page/chapter navigation actions in calibre_viewer.py.
    • evince: Provides support for the Evince document viewer on Linux, using the user.pages tag to enable actions defined in evince_linux.py and enabling tags in evince.talon.
    • foxit_reader: Provides support for the Foxit Reader application, providing actions for opening tabs, navigating pages, and closing all tabs. The primary files are foxit_reader.py and foxit_reader.talon.
    • kindle: Provides support for the Kindle app on Windows, with app definitions in kindle.py, page navigation actions in kindle_win.py, and enabling tags in kindle.talon.
    • nitro_reader: Provides support for Nitro Reader 5, with actions for opening tabs and navigating pages in nitro_reader_5.py and enabling tags in nitro_reader_5.talon.
    • okular: Provides support for the Okular document viewer, with platform specific implementations for Windows and Linux, and page navigation actions in okular.py and tags in okular.talon.
    • sumatrapdf: Provides support for the SumatraPDF application, with application definitions and actions in sumatrapdf.py and tags in sumatrapdf.talon.
  • Other Applications: Numerous other directories provide support for a diverse set of applications:
    • 1password: Provides support for 1Password password manager, with actions defined in password_manager.py, implemented in a platform specific way in 1password_mac.py and 1password_win.py, and exposed with voice commands in 1password.talon and 1password_global.talon.
    • amethyst: Provides support for the Amethyst window manager on macOS, with voice commands for window management in amethyst.talon.
    • anaconda: Provides support for the Anaconda package manager via terminal commands, using anaconda.py and anaconda.talon.
    • apple_notes: Provides support for Apple Notes on macOS, with voice commands defined in apple_notes.talon and zoom actions defined in apple_notes.py.
    • arc: Provides support for the Arc browser on macOS. It defines actions and tags for Arc in arc.py, applies tags in arc_mac.talon, and adds support for little arc in little-arc_mac.talon.
    • discord: Provides support for Discord, with a common action interface defined in discord.py and platform specific keybindings in discord_win.py and discord_mac.py.
    • dock: Provides functionality for interacting with the macOS Dock, with core actions in dock.py and voice commands in dock.talon.
    • dunst: Provides support for the Dunst notification daemon on Linux, with basic notification commands in dunst.talon.
    • evernote: Provides basic support for Evernote on macOS, setting the key wait time in mac.talon.
    • finder: Provides support for the macOS Finder application, defining custom actions for file management and navigation in finder.py and defining core voice commands for Finder in finder.talon.
    • gdb: Provides support for the GNU Debugger (GDB) command line tool using gdb.py and gdb_active.talon and a global enabling command with gdb_global.talon.
    • git: Provides support for Git, defining a reusable git command in git.talon, lists of commands and arguments in git_command.talon-list and git_argument.talon-list, and adds commands for the interactive patch selection in git add --patch in git_add_patch.talon.
    • github: Provides extensive support for interacting with the GitHub website, with a focus on keybindings for navigation and performing actions on GitHub in github_web.talon.
    • gitlab: Provides support for GitLab in a web browser with most of the functionality provided by gitlab.talon which makes use of keybindings.
    • i3wm: Provides support for the i3 window manager on Linux, with commands to interact with i3 using i3wm.py and a large set of voice commands defined in i3wm.talon.
    • keepassx: Provides support for the KeePassX password manager, with commands that map to common keyboard shortcuts in keepassx_linux.talon.
    • kubectl: Provides commands to interact with the kubectl command-line tool, with lists and the kubectl tag defined in kubectl.py and voice commands in kubectl.talon.
    • meld: Provides support for the Meld diff tool, with actions for tab management in meld.py and diff navigation commands in meld.talon.
    • nautilus: Provides support for the Nautilus file manager on Linux, including actions for tab management, and file manager commands in nautilus.py and tagging in nautilus.talon.
    • notepad: Provides support for the Notepad application on Windows, with a custom action for getting the filename and setting tags in notepad.py and tags in notepad.talon.
    • obsidian: Provides basic support for the Obsidian application. It sets the app definition and default language to markdown in obsidian.py and sets the tab tag in obsidian.talon.
    • outlook: Provides support for Microsoft Outlook, with separate command sets for the Windows desktop application using outlook_win.talon and the web version using outlook_web.talon.
    • powershell: Provides support for Windows PowerShell, with file manager actions in powershell_win.py and enabling of tags in powershell_win.talon.
    • protonmail: Provides comprehensive support for the ProtonMail web application with commands for navigating the inbox, composing emails and managing contacts using keyboard shortcuts in protonmail.talon.
    • signal: Provides support for the Signal application on Linux using voice commands that map to keyboard shortcuts in signal_linux.talon.
    • slack: Provides support for the Slack application, with platform specific implementations for macOS and Windows using slack_win.py, slack_mac.py, slack_win.talon, and slack_mac.talon, and a common action override in slack.py.
    • stata: Provides support for the Stata statistical software package, with commands specific to the Stata Do-file editor in stata_do_file_editor.talon, and the application context and default language in stata.py.
    • taskwarrior: Provides support for the Taskwarrior command-line task management application using commands that map to the Taskwarrior command line tool in taskwarrior_linux.talon.
    • teams: Provides support for Microsoft Teams, with platform-specific commands defined in teams_mac.talon, teams.talon, and additional functionality in teams_linux.py, and application matching in teams.py.
    • terraform: Provides support for the Terraform CLI using terraform.py and terraform.talon.
    • thunderbird: Provides support for the Thunderbird email client with contexts for the inbox, composer, calendar, contacts, and tasks, with actions that are platform specific using the OS specific files thunderbird_linux.py, thunderbird_win.py, and thunderbird_mac.py, and a base tag and tab commands using [thunderbird.talon](https://github.com/

docs.title: 1password
docs.text:
This directory, community/apps/1password, contains the Talon files for controlling the 1Password password manager application.

The file password_manager.py defines the application itself, using the mod.apps.one_password declarations. It also defines the actions for password management in the @mod.action_class that other files can implement (e.g. password_fill). Note that the application definition uses the bundle ID for macOS and the process name for Windows, so that it can work cross platform.

The files 1password_mac.py and 1password_win.py implement the actions defined in password_manager.py for macOS and Windows respectively. They each define an action class (@ctx.action_class) with implementations of actions such as password_fill, password_show, password_new, password_duplicate, password_edit, and password_delete. These actions use the actions.key() command to send keystrokes to the application. The ctx.matches declaration restricts each file's actions to the specified operating system.

The file 1password.talon defines voice commands that are specific to the 1Password application when it is focused. For example, saying "password new" will execute user.password_new(). The file 1password_global.talon defines commands that are always available, regardless of which application is focused. Note that the commands defined in these two talon files are not fully overlapping (e.g. password edit is only defined in 1password.talon).

In summary, the files in this directory work together to define a cross-platform voice interface for 1Password. The password_manager.py file defines the application and the actions that can be performed on it. The 1password_mac.py and 1password_win.py files implement the actions using platform-specific keybindings. Finally, the 1password.talon and 1password_global.talon files expose these actions through voice commands.

docs.title: adobe
docs.text:
This directory contains files to support control of Adobe Acrobat Reader DC with Talon.

The file adobe_acrobat_reader_dc.py defines the application using Talon's module system. It specifies different ways to identify the application on Windows, using the application's name or executable file name. There is a comment indicating that Mac support is a TODO.

The file adobe_acrobat_reader_dc_win.py defines actions specific to the Windows version of Adobe Acrobat Reader DC. It implements actions for navigating tabs (tab_next, tab_previous), zooming (zoom_in, zoom_out, zoom_reset), and navigating pages (page_current, page_next, page_previous, page_jump, page_final, page_rotate_right, page_rotate_left). The zoom actions have a TODO to differentiate languages, which would likely involve checking the UI language and selecting the right shortcut based on that. The page actions use key combinations like "ctrl-shift-n" to open the page number input box, and then use other talon actions to insert the page number and press enter, allowing jumping to specific pages.

The file adobe_acrobat_reader_dc.talon sets the tags user.tabs and user.pages when the adobe_acrobat_reader_dc app is active. These tags are used by other talon files to define further actions that apply when these tags are set, enabling the actions defined in adobe_acrobat_reader_dc_win.py.

docs.title: amethyst
docs.text:
The community/apps/amethyst directory contains configurations for the Amethyst window manager.

The most important file is amethyst.talon. This file defines Talon voice commands for controlling Amethyst. It includes commands for:

  • Moving between windows (window next, window previous)
  • Maximizing a window (window full)
  • Floating a window (window float)
  • Setting window layouts (window tall, window middle)
  • Moving a window to the main area (window move main)
  • Resizing windows (window grow, window shrink)
  • Reevaluating layouts (window reevaluate)

The user.running: amethyst line indicates that these commands are enabled when the Amethyst application is running.

docs.title: anaconda
docs.text:
The community/apps/anaconda directory contains files for interacting with the Anaconda package manager using Talon.

The file anaconda.talon defines a series of voice commands for interacting with the conda command-line tool. The commands are designed to be spoken after saying "anaconda" followed by the command you wish to execute. For example, saying "anaconda list" will execute conda list. Commands include those for managing environments, installing, removing and updating packages, and other less frequently used conda commands.

The anaconda.py file defines a tag that activates the commands in anaconda.talon when the tag user.anaconda is active. The user.anaconda tag must be activated separately using other Talon configuration files or commands. Typically, it would be active whenever the user is in a terminal and working with Anaconda.

docs.title: apple_notes
docs.text:
This directory, community/apps/apple_notes, contains files that provide support for controlling the Apple Notes application using Talon.

The file apple_notes.talon defines a number of commands for the Apple Notes application. It primarily uses keybindings to trigger different actions within the application.

These actions include: * Creating new notes and folders * Toggling the visibility of folders, attachments, and the main view * Switching between list and gallery views * Finding content, printing, and attaching files * Creating links, inserting tables, and applying various text formatting options * Adjusting font size, inserting line breaks, and marking text * Moving a line up or down

The file apple_notes.py defines additional actions using Talon's Python API. It provides custom implementations of the edit.zoom_in, edit.zoom_out, and edit.zoom_reset actions, which perform the corresponding zoom functions in Apple Notes. It also provides a custom implementation of the edit.indent_less action.

docs.title: apple_terminal
docs.text:
The community/apps/apple_terminal directory contains files that add support for the macOS Terminal application. The key files here are:

  • apple_terminal.talon: This file defines the Talon commands and tags that are active when the Apple Terminal application is in focus.

    • It uses tag(): terminal to activate the core terminal functionality defined in terminal.talon.
    • It uses tag(): user.readline to enable readline keybindings for editing commands.
    • It uses tag(): user.generic_unix_shell to activate the implementation of commands and functions in terminal.talon.
    • It activates tags for common applications git, anaconda, and kubectl, making related commands available.
    • It activates user.tabs and user.file_manager tags, which enables the functionality related to managing tabs and interacting with a file system.
    • It defines suspend and resume commands, which send ctrl-z to suspend a process and then use fg and enter to resume it.

  • apple_terminal.py: This file defines the Python code that provides application-specific functionality.

    • It defines EditActions that overrides the delete_line function, mapping it to ctrl-u.
    • It defines UserActions for file management.
      • file_manager_current_path extracts the current path from the terminal window title, handling cases with " — " separators and tilde (~) characters, and allows for remapping and excluding of certain paths.
      • file_manager_open_directory inserts a cd command, adding quotes around the path, to open a directory and executes it by pressing enter.
      • file_manager_open_parent inserts a cd .. command to navigate to the parent directory and executes it by pressing enter.
      • file_manager_select_directory inserts a path to select a directory.
      • file_manager_new_folder inserts a mkdir command to create a new directory.
      • file_manager_open_file inserts a path to open a file and executes it by pressing enter.
      • file_manager_select_file inserts a path to select a file.
    • It defines app_actions that implement tab_previous and tab_next, using ctrl-shift-tab and ctrl-tab respectively for tab navigation.

These files work together to enable the user to control the Apple Terminal application using Talon. The .talon file defines the commands and tags, while the .py file implements the specific actions. The apple_terminal.py file also provides file system related commands which allows the user to navigate the file system via spoken commands.

docs.title: arc
docs.text:
The community/apps/arc directory contains files that add support for the Arc browser to Talon.

The arc.py file contains Python code that defines the application context and actions for Arc. It defines a context for when the application name is "Arc" or when the bundle ID is company.thebrowser.Browser on macOS. It provides custom actions for closing tabs using tab_close_wrapper, triggering a search using command_search, and navigating to the extensions page with show_extensions. The tab_close_wrapper includes a sleep to give the browser time to react to the action. The command_search action allows you to search directly from the address bar.

The arc_mac.talon file defines the tags that should be active when Arc is the active application on macOS. Specifically it sets the browser, user.tabs, and user.command_search tags. These tags are used to enable generic browser commands and the search functionality. This file primarily applies generic browser commands to Arc, and enables the functionality defined in arc.py.

The little-arc_mac.talon file defines commands related to the "Little Arc" feature. When the command "little arc" is spoken, it will open the little arc window and insert any text following the spoken command. This file assumes the user has not disabled Little Arc.

docs.title: atril
docs.text:
The community/apps/atril directory contains files that add functionality to the Talon voice control system for the Atril document viewer application on Linux.

The file atril.talon sets the user.pages tag when the Atril application is active. This tag is likely used to enable other Talon scripts that use pagination.

The file atril.py defines the application context and implements custom user actions specific to Atril.

The Python script does the following:

  • Defines the application context for Atril on Linux.
  • Implements the user.page_current, user.page_next, user.page_previous, user.page_jump, and user.page_final actions which allow the user to navigate the document.
  • The implementation of user.page_current relies on the user interface to show the current page number so it can be read with actions.edit.selected_text().
  • All actions are triggered with ctrl- key combinations.

docs.title: brave
docs.text:
The community/apps/brave directory contains files that provide support for the Brave browser within Talon.

The file brave.py defines the application context for the Brave browser. It defines the application matching rules for Windows, Linux, and macOS. It also contains an action override for tab_close_wrapper that adds a short delay before closing a tab. It also contains a custom action for show_extensions that opens a new tab and navigates to the extensions page.

The file brave.talon applies the browser and user.tabs tags when the application brave is active. These tags allow for the use of generic browser commands and tab-related commands.

docs.title: calibre
docs.text:
This directory contains files for the calibre application, an e-book library manager. It provides Talon scripts to control the application and its e-book viewer.

The file calibre.py defines the application contexts for the main Calibre application across Windows and Linux. This allows Talon to activate specific scripts when the Calibre application is in focus. The file calibre_viewer.py defines the application context for the Calibre e-book viewer, again across Windows and Linux. It also defines the actions for page and chapter navigation. These actions are available through the user.pages and user.chapters tags, which are enabled by calibre_viewer.talon.

Specifically, the user.pages tag provides the following actions, implemented in calibre_viewer.py: * user.page_next(): maps to pagedown which advances to the next page. * user.page_previous(): maps to pageup which goes back to the previous page. * user.page_final(): maps to ctrl-end which goes to the last page.

The user.chapters tag provides the following actions, also implemented in calibre_viewer.py: * user.chapter_next(): maps to ctrl-pagedown which advances to the next chapter. * user.chapter_previous(): maps to ctrl-pageup which goes back to the previous chapter.

These files together allow for the definition of application contexts and custom actions that can be used with voice commands within the Calibre and Calibre e-book viewer applications.

docs.title: chrome
docs.text:
The community/apps/chrome directory contains files that enable Talon to control the Google Chrome browser.

The chrome.talon file defines the core grammar for interacting with Chrome. It assigns the browser and user.tabs tags when Chrome is the active application. It also defines voice commands for switching profiles (profile switch), opening the tab search (tab search), and opening the tab search with a text argument (tab search <user.text>$).

The chrome.py file defines the application context and actions for Chrome. It defines which applications should be considered "Chrome" on Windows, macOS, and Linux. The chrome_mod action sends the specified key with the correct modifier (cmd on mac, ctrl otherwise). The tab_close_wrapper action adds a small delay before closing a tab. Finally, show_extensions opens a new tab and navigates to chrome://extensions. These actions are available to other Talon scripts when the browser tag is active.

docs.title: conemu
docs.text:
The community/apps/conemu directory contains configuration for the ConEmu terminal application.

The file conemu.talon declares that the associated application is the executable conemu64.exe (case-insensitive). When the application is active, the terminal and user.git tags are enabled. This indicates that the user is likely interacting with a terminal and may be using git, enabling appropriate commands.

docs.title: discord
docs.text:
The community/apps/discord directory contains files that enable control of the Discord application using Talon. It includes platform-specific keybindings and a common interface.

The discord.talon file defines the voice commands for controlling Discord. It uses tags to activate the commands, and defines commands for navigation, UI manipulation, and other functions. For example, saying "channel mentions next" will activate user.discord_mentions_next().

The files discord_win.py and discord_mac.py define the platform-specific implementations for the actions defined in discord.py. They both define a UserActions class which implements the actions defined in discord.py. The difference between the files are in the keybindings used to implement the actions, using ctrl on Windows and cmd on macOS. For example, discord_quick_switcher uses ctrl-k on windows and cmd-k on macOS.

The discord.py file defines the common interface used by the other files. It defines the application context, the list of discord destinations used for quick switching, and the actions that can be performed on Discord. For example, discord_mentions_last is defined as an action, and then implemented in discord_win.py and discord_mac.py as different key presses. It also defines the context that these actions are applied to, using app: discord, meaning these actions are available when the user is focused on a Discord application window.

docs.title: dock
docs.text:
The community/apps/dock directory provides functionality for interacting with the macOS Dock. It contains two files: dock.py and dock.talon.

dock.py defines Talon actions for sending notifications to the macOS Dock and activating application Exposé.

The Actions class defines two actions:

  • dock_send_notification(notification: str): Sends a notification to the macOS Dock using a low-level SPI interface.
  • dock_app_expose(app: Optional[ui.App] = None): Activates the macOS App Exposé for a given app via its Dock item. If no app is specified, it uses the frontmost app.

The UserActions class implements those actions, using the talon.mac.dock.dock_notify to implement the notification and ui.apps and AXShowExpose to implement the app expose.

dock.talon defines Talon commands that use the actions defined in dock.py.

  • The command desktop sends the com.apple.showdesktop.awake notification to the Dock.
  • The command window activates App Exposé for the current app.
  • The command launch pad sends the com.apple.launchpad.toggle notification to the Dock, toggling the launch pad.

docs.title: dunst
docs.text:
The community/apps/dunst directory contains Talon code for interacting with the Dunst notification daemon on Linux.

The file dunst.talon defines several voice commands for controlling Dunst:

  • show notifications: Activates the notifications.
  • dismiss [notifications]: Closes the currently displayed notification.
  • dismiss all [notifications]: Closes all currently displayed notifications.

The commented-out lines demonstrate how you might add commands to pause, resume, and test notifications. These commands leverage the notify-send command-line utility in combination with environment variables used internally by Dunst for controlling functionality, and could be added back in.

docs.title: eclipse
docs.text:
The community/apps/eclipse directory contains files to enable Talon voice control for the Eclipse IDE.

The core functionality is defined in eclipse_win.py. This file defines Talon actions for the Eclipse application on Windows. It uses Talon's Context and Module classes to define when these actions are active and what they do. It defines actions for app control (like closing tabs), code editing (like toggling comments), general editing (like moving lines), and user defined actions for split screen management and find and replace. These actions generally map to Eclipse keyboard shortcuts.

eclipse.talon contains Talon commands that are specific to Eclipse. These commands invoke actions defined in eclipse_win.py or directly perform keyboard shortcuts. The file also defines tags that enable groups of commands, such as user.find_and_replace or user.splits, that are implemented in eclipse_win.py.

These two files work together to provide voice control for Eclipse. The .py file defines the actions, and the .talon file defines commands to call those actions or perform keyboard shortcuts.

docs.title: edge
docs.text:
The community/apps/edge directory contains configuration for the Microsoft Edge browser.

The file edge.py defines the application context for Microsoft Edge. It specifies the conditions under which the context is active, using app.name, app.exe, and app.bundle for Windows, macOS, and Linux. It defines a custom action, show_extensions, which opens a new tab and navigates to the Edge extensions page.

The file edge.talon activates the browser and user.tabs tags when the Microsoft Edge application is active. These tags are used to enable browser-specific and tab-related commands, respectively.

docs.title: emacs
docs.text:
The community/apps/emacs directory provides a comprehensive set of tools for interacting with the Emacs text editor using Talon. It includes configurations, scripts, and data files that enable voice control of Emacs' editing, navigation, and project management features.

The core logic is implemented in emacs.py. This file defines the Talon module, settings, and actions for controlling Emacs. It includes:

  • App Matching: Defines how Talon recognizes Emacs, using app names, bundle IDs and executable names for different operating systems.
  • Meta Key Handling: Implements the emacs_meta setting, which allows the user to configure which key is used for the Emacs meta key (e.g., "esc", "alt", or "cmd"). The meta() and meta_fixup() functions handle translation of meta- prefixes into the appropriate key combinations.
  • Emacs Actions: Provides a set of Talon actions for common Emacs operations, such as running commands, inserting prefixes, and accessing help. The emacs() action is particularly important, as it attempts to use keybindings for a command if it exists, and falls back to the M-x command prompt if it does not.
  • Contextual Actions: Overrides several Talon user, edit, app, code, and win actions to provide more natural Emacs-specific behavior. For example, actions such as copy, cut, and paste are mapped to the corresponding Emacs commands, and window management is mapped to Emacs' split window functionality.
  • Text Selection: Implements a variety of text selection commands, including range selection, and actions for moving by word, line, sentence, and paragraph.
  • Dictation Peek: The dictation_peek action allows peeking at surrounding context before inserting dictated text, however this may not work in a terminal.

emacs_commands.csv is a CSV file that contains a list of Emacs commands along with their keybindings, short forms, and spoken forms. This allows the user to say the command name instead of having to use keybindings and provides a way to use the short form for M-x commands.

emacs_commands.py parses the emacs_commands.csv file and exposes the data as Talon actions. The emacs_command_keybinding action looks up the keybinding for an Emacs command, and the emacs_command_short_form action retrieves the short form. The load_commands function is decorated with @resource.watch so that changes to emacs_commands.csv are automatically loaded.

emacs.talon is the main Talon script that binds spoken commands to the actions defined in emacs.py. It defines a large number of voice commands for common Emacs tasks, including:

  • General Commands: Commands for executing Emacs functions, manipulating buffers, and running shell commands.
  • Mode Commands: Commands for toggling and enabling Emacs major and minor modes.
  • Window/Split Management: Commands for splitting and manipulating windows (splits).
  • Help Commands: Commands for accessing Emacs help documentation.
  • File and Buffer Commands: Commands for opening, closing, renaming, and navigating files and buffers.
  • Motion and Editing Commands: Commands for moving the cursor and editing text, such as selecting regions, performing searches, and inserting text.
  • Xref Support: Commands for navigating code using xref (cross-reference) functionality.
  • Project Support: Commands for managing projects and related files.
  • VC/Git Support: Commands for interacting with version control systems.
  • Mode-Specific Commands: Commands for working with specific modes, such as Python mode, smerge mode, and outline mode.

In summary, the files in this directory work together to provide a powerful and customizable way to control Emacs with voice commands. The emacs.py file handles the core logic and settings, the emacs_commands.csv file contains data about Emacs commands, the emacs_commands.py file parses the emacs_commands.csv and exposes the data, and the emacs.talon file defines the voice commands that map to the functionality defined in the Python files.

docs.title: evernote
docs.text:
The community/apps/evernote directory contains configuration files for the Evernote application on macOS.

The file mac.talon contains Talon configuration specific to Evernote on macOS. It sets a key_wait setting, likely to address issues with command timing.

docs.title: evince
docs.text:
The community/apps/evince directory contains configuration files for the Evince document viewer application on Linux.

The file evince_linux.py defines a Talon module for Evince on Linux. It starts by defining the app using a Talon module definition, associating the name "Evince" on the "linux" OS with this configuration. It then defines a context that matches when the evince application is active. Within this context, it defines an action class that implements the user.pages actions:

  • page_current: Gets the current page number by opening the location bar, copying the value, and closing the location bar.
  • page_next: Navigates to the next page.
  • page_previous: Navigates to the previous page.
  • page_jump: Jumps to the specified page number by opening the location bar, inserting the target page number, and pressing enter.
  • page_final: Navigates to the last page.

The file evince.talon is a Talon file that sets the user.pages tag when the evince application is active. This tag enables the user to use the user.pages actions defined in evince_linux.py when Evince is active.

docs.title: finder
docs.text:
The community/apps/finder directory contains files that extend Talon's functionality for the macOS Finder application. It provides commands for navigation, file management, and window manipulation within Finder.

The finder.talon file at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/apps/finder/finder.talon defines the core voice commands for the Finder application. It includes:

  • Tags: user.address, user.file_manager, user.navigation, and user.tabs. These tags enable other parts of the Talon configuration to be activated when Finder is in focus.
  • Key Bindings: Shortcuts for common Finder actions are defined, including opening preferences (cmd-,), options (cmd-j), and search (cmd-alt-f).
  • Sorting Commands: Voice commands for sorting files and folders using ctrl-alt-cmd + a number, such as "sort by name", "sort by date opened", and others.
  • View Commands: Commands for changing the view mode, including "icon view", "column view", "list view", and "gallery view".
  • Trash and Hide Commands: Commands for moving items to the trash and hiding windows.

The finder.py file at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/apps/finder/finder.py defines the Python logic for the Finder application. It defines the behavior of the user.file_manager and user.address actions and includes:

  • Context Matching: The script activates only when the focused application is "finder".
  • file_manager_open_parent: Simulates pressing cmd-up to navigate to the parent directory.
  • file_manager_current_path: Determines the current path displayed in the Finder window title and remaps some paths to more useful values (e.g. "/" to "/Volumes"), while also excluding some.
  • file_manager_terminal_here: Opens a new terminal window and changes the current directory to the directory displayed in the Finder window using AppleScript.
  • file_manager_show_properties: Shows the properties window for the selected file using cmd-i.
  • file_manager_open_directory: Opens a specific directory by using cmd-shift-g and then inserting the path.
  • file_manager_select_directory: Selects a directory by inserting the path.
  • file_manager_new_folder: Creates a new folder using cmd-shift-n and then inserting the folder name.
  • file_manager_open_file: Opens a file by inserting the path and then pressing cmd-o.
  • file_manager_select_file: Selects a file by inserting the path.
  • address_focus: Focuses the address bar by using cmd-shift-g.
  • address_copy_address: Copies the path from the address bar using alt-cmd-c.
  • address_navigate: Navigates to a specific address using user.file_manager_open_directory.

In summary, finder.talon provides the voice command definitions, while finder.py implements the underlying Python logic to perform the requested actions within the Finder application. They work together to enable voice control over macOS's Finder.

docs.title: firefox
docs.text:
The community/apps/firefox directory contains Talon scripts for controlling the Firefox web browser. It includes platform-specific Python files (firefox_mac.py, firefox_win_linux.py), a general firefox.py file, and a Talon script (firefox.talon). These files work together to provide a comprehensive set of commands for interacting with Firefox across different operating systems.

  • firefox_mac.py defines macOS-specific actions for Firefox. It includes keyboard shortcuts for opening the bookmarks sidebar (cmd-b), history sidebar (cmd-shift-h), bookmarks (cmd-shift-o), private windows (cmd-shift-p), downloads (cmd-j), and extensions (cmd-shift-a). It uses Talon's Context system to match only when the OS is macOS, the current application is Firefox, and the "browser" tag is active.

  • firefox.talon defines voice commands for interacting with Firefox. It includes voice commands for focusing the address bar and inserting text into it. It also includes voice commands to toggle the bookmarks and history sidebars. It activates the "browser" and "user.tabs" tags when Firefox is active.

  • firefox_win_linux.py provides Windows and Linux-specific actions for Firefox. It contains keyboard shortcuts for opening the bookmarks sidebar (ctrl-b), history sidebar (ctrl-h), private windows (ctrl-shift-p), downloads (ctrl-j or ctrl-shift-y on Linux), and extensions (ctrl-shift-a). It also includes a function to focus the address bar (f6 then ctrl-l) to work around a devtools issue. It uses Talon's Context system to match when the OS is Windows or Linux, the current application is Firefox, and the "browser" tag is active.

  • firefox.py defines the application matching and sets up some generic actions for Firefox. It defines different application names and bundle IDs that should be matched to the Firefox application. It also defines the firefox_bookmarks_sidebar and firefox_history_sidebar actions which are implemented in the platform specific files. It also implements focus_page which focuses the address bar, finds, then presses escape and go_home which goes to the home page. It uses Talon's Context system to match when the OS is Windows, Linux, or macOS, the current application is Firefox, and the "browser" tag is active.

The files work together using Talon's context matching to ensure the correct actions are available depending on the current operating system. The firefox.py file defines the application matching and generic actions. The firefox_mac.py and firefox_win_linux.py files provide platform specific implementations of these actions and the firefox.talon file provides voice commands that use the actions defined in the other files. This allows for a consistent user experience across different operating systems while taking into account platform-specific differences.

docs.title: foxit_reader
docs.text:
This directory contains files that add support for the Foxit Reader application to Talon.

The file foxit_reader.talon defines the application tag foxit_reader, which is used to activate this module. It also includes the tags user.tabs and user.pages which enable the functionality for navigating tabs and pages. There is also a command to close all tabs with tab close all: key(ctrl-shift-w).

The file foxit_reader.py defines the application using a regular expression matching the application's executable name and the name of the application. It then defines a context that is active when the foxit_reader app tag is active, and overrides some of the default app and user actions.

It overrides app.tab_open() to open a new tab using ctrl-o. It also overrides some actions in the app.tabs namespace with notifications that they are not supported, such as tab_reopen().

It overrides some actions in the user.tabs namespace with notifications that they are not supported, such as tab_jump(), tab_final(), and tab_duplicate().

It overrides actions in the user.pages namespace, providing more complete support for page navigation: - page_current() gets the current page by using ctrl-g, selecting the text, and returning it as a number. - page_next() advances to the next page using the right arrow key. - page_previous() goes to the previous page using the left arrow key. - page_jump() goes to a specific page using ctrl-g then inserting the page number and pressing enter. - page_final() goes to the final page using the end key. - page_rotate_right() rotates the page using shift-ctrl-keypad_plus. - page_rotate_left() rotates the page using shift-ctrl-keypad_minus.

In summary, this module adds support for Foxit Reader, providing a way to open tabs and navigate pages.

docs.title: gdb
docs.text:
The community/apps/gdb directory contains files that enable voice control for the GNU Debugger (GDB) using Talon.

The most important files are:

  • gdb_active.talon: This file contains Talon commands that are active when the user has the user.gdb tag enabled, generally when a GDB session is active in a terminal window. It provides commands for common GDB operations such as setting breakpoints, printing variables, stepping through code, and more. Many commands have variations for specific outputs or arguments.

    For example: * break [on] clipboard sets a breakpoint at a location pasted from the clipboard. * print [variable] <user.text> prints a variable, including those with names captured as <user.text>. * hex dump <number> bytes dumps a number of bytes of memory in hex format. * disable display <number_small> disables a GDB display with a specified number. * set follow (fork | forks) [mode] child sets GDB to follow the child process after a fork. * The file has commands for a variety of common actions and many related options (e.g. various print commands). * There are also commands for manipulating displays, threads, and inferior processes. * gdb.py: This Python file defines the logic for enabling and disabling the user.gdb tag. It also defines a number of actions for interacting with GDB, with many corresponding to commands in gdb_active.talon. These actions are generally more generic, and gdb_active.talon maps specific spoken commands to specific parameters of these actions.

    For example, the following actions are implemented: * debugger_step_into (mapped to stepi) * debugger_step_over (mapped to nexti) * debugger_add_sw_breakpoint (mapped to break) * debugger_clear_all_breakpoints (mapped to d br) * debugger_disable_breakpoint_id (mapped to disable br <number>) The file also includes actions for registers, starting, stopping, continuing, detaching, and more. * gdb_global.talon: This file provides global commands to enable and disable the user.gdb tag via the user.gdb_enable() and user.gdb_disable() actions defined in gdb.py. These are the primary means for activating the gdb_active.talon commands.

    For example: * enable debug mode activates GDB commands. * disable debug mode deactivates GDB commands.

These files work together to provide a comprehensive voice control interface for GDB. The gdb_global.talon file provides the means for users to turn the GDB commands on and off. The gdb_active.talon file maps spoken commands to GDB actions. And finally, the gdb.py file provides the actual implementation of those actions to interact with GDB through keyboard commands or other Talon actions.

docs.title: git
docs.text:
The community/apps/git directory provides a set of Talon scripts for interacting with Git from the command line.

The core functionality is defined in git.talon, which defines the user.git tag and several commands. The most important command is git {user.git_command} [<user.git_arguments>], which takes a git command from the user.git_command list and optional arguments from the user.git_arguments list. This is the primary way you can invoke git from Talon, for example "git status", "git commit amend", or "git add patch". There are also several convenience commands that use the clipboard or the currently highlighted text. The command git commit [<user.git_arguments>] message [<user.prose>] shows how to insert a git commit message using the <user.prose> text input. The command git stash [push] [<user.git_arguments>] message [<user.prose>] is similar, but for stashing. Finally there are some commands with the $ suffix that execute frequently used, harmless git commands. These are useful for checking the status of the repo or diffing changes.

The lists user.git_command and user.git_argument are defined in git_command.talon-list and git_argument.talon-list, respectively. These lists are used by the git.talon file to provide the arguments to the git commands.

The file git_add_patch.talon adds commands for the interactive patch selection in git add --patch. It defines the commands yank, near, quench, drum, and air, which map to the letters that git add --patch uses to interact with the patch.

Finally, git.py defines the user.git_arguments capture, which takes one or more user.git_argument list items. It also defines the git_command and git_argument lists used in the other files.

docs.title: github
docs.text:
The community/apps/github directory contains configurations for interacting with the GitHub website using Talon.

The file github_web.talon defines a variety of keyboard shortcuts for navigating and interacting with the GitHub website. The file is structured to work on any page on github.com due to browser.host: github.com.

The shortcuts are organized into several categories:

  • Site-wide shortcuts: These commands like focus search, go to notifications, go to dashboard, show keyboard shortcuts help quickly access site-wide features, and use the same single-key shortcuts used by the GitHub website itself.
  • Repositories: These commands like go to code, go to issues, go to pull requests, and similar provide shortcuts to access different sections of a repository.
  • Source code editing: This contains a shortcut to use GitHub's web editor, accessed via editor open.
  • Source code browsing: This category contains shortcuts for navigating and inspecting source code files, like find file, jump to line, switch branch, expand url, show comments, blame view open, and show annotations.
  • Issues: This section defines shortcuts for creating, searching, and interacting with issues such as create an issue, search issues, filter by labels, filter by milestones, and shortcuts for commenting, including submit comment and preview comment. It also includes a shortcut for git hub full screen which uses Ctrl+Shift+L on the Github website.
  • Browsing commit: This category provides shortcuts for navigating commits like close form, parent commit, and other parent commit.
  • Notifications: Here you will find shortcuts for managing notifications like mark as read and mute thread.
  • Issue or pull request list: This section contains shortcuts for opening and creating issues when viewing an issue or pull request list.
  • Issues and pull requests: These shortcuts allow requesting reviewers, setting milestones and assignees and labels when viewing a single issue or pull request.
  • Actions: Shortcuts for accessing GitHub actions such as go to workflow, timestamps toggle, and fullscreen toggle.

docs.title: gitlab
docs.text:
The community/apps/gitlab directory contains configuration for using Talon with GitLab in a web browser.

The most important file is gitlab.talon. It defines a large number of voice commands for interacting with GitLab in the browser.

Here's how it works:

  • It uses the browser tag to specify that these commands are used in the browser.
  • It uses browser.host: /gitlab\.com/ to scope the commands to the gitlab.com domain.
  • Many commands use key() to send keyboard shortcuts to the page, taking advantage of GitLab's existing keyboard shortcuts. These keyboard shortcuts are documented at https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/shortcuts.html. For example, the command go to projects [page]: key(shift-p) will trigger the keyboard shortcut shift-p to navigate to the projects page.
  • Other commands use insert() to type text into the page. For example, the command go [to] project home [page]: insert("gp") will type gp into the page, which is another shortcut for navigating to the project home page.
  • The commands are grouped into sections based on the area of GitLab that they relate to. For example, there are sections for "global shortcuts", "projects", "issues and merge requests", "project files", and "wiki pages".
  • Some commands are designed to move between different parts of the application. For example go to projects [page] takes you to the projects page and go to project files [list] takes you to the project files list.
  • Some commands are designed to perform actions on the page. For example edit last comment focuses on the last comment, change assignee focuses on the assignee dropdown, and open permalink focuses on the permalink.
  • Some commands are more nuanced. For example next [unresolved] discussion goes to the next unresolved discussion on a page.

docs.title: gnome_terminal
docs.text:
The community/apps/gnome_terminal directory contains configuration files for the Gnome Terminal application within the Talon voice control system. It provides a set of commands and behaviors tailored to this specific terminal emulator.

The file gnome_terminal.talon defines the application tag for Gnome Terminal, as well as a number of other tags that are enabled when Gnome Terminal is the active application. Specifically, it activates the terminal, user.tabs, user.generic_unix_shell, user.git, and user.kubectl tags. These tags enable more specific functionality defined elsewhere in the Talon configuration.

The file gnome_terminal.py defines the core logic for how Talon interacts with Gnome Terminal. This file is responsible for defining the app, matching the app context, and defining custom user and app actions.

Here's a breakdown of its key functionalities:

  • App Definition: It defines Gnome Terminal as an application by specifying the executable names for gnome-terminal-server, Gnome-terminal and Mate-terminal on Linux operating systems.
  • Context Matching: It creates a context that activates when the Gnome Terminal application is active.
  • user Actions: It implements actions within the user namespace, specifically tab_jump(number). This action allows the user to switch to a specific tab number using the keyboard shortcut alt-{number}.
  • app Actions: It implements several actions within the app namespace:
    • tab_open(): Opens a new tab (ctrl-shift-t).
    • tab_previous(): Switches to the previous tab (ctrl-pageup).
    • tab_next(): Switches to the next tab (ctrl-pagedown).
    • tab_close(): Closes the current tab (ctrl-shift-w).
    • window_open(): Opens a new window (ctrl-shift-n).
    • window_close(): Closes the current window (ctrl-shift-q).
  • edit Actions: It overrides or adds a number of actions within the edit namespace:
    • page_down(): Moves down a page (shift-pagedown).
    • page_up(): Moves up a page (shift-pageup).
    • paste(): Pastes text (ctrl-shift-v).
    • copy(): Copies text (ctrl-shift-c).
    • find(text): Opens the find dialog with optional text to insert (ctrl-shift-f).
    • delete_line(): Deletes the current line (ctrl-k) by moving to the start of the line and using the shortcut.
    • The actions extend_left, extend_right, extend_up, extend_down, extend_word_left, and extend_word_right are intentionally left unimplemented as they are not possible in Gnome Terminal.

In summary, these files provide a comprehensive set of configurations for using Gnome Terminal with Talon, including tag management, tab management, window management, and text editing. These files also define a more standard set of editing actions than are provided by default by Linux.

docs.title: guake
docs.text:
The community/apps/guake directory contains configuration for the Guake terminal application on Linux.

The file guake_linux.py defines a Talon context for the Guake terminal application on Linux. It sets the user.git, user.kubectl, user.tabs, and terminal tags, which enables other Talon scripts that use those tags to be active when Guake is in focus.

The file also defines some actions related to tabs using the app action class:

  • tab_open(): Opens a new tab using ctrl-shift-t.
  • tab_close(): Closes the current tab using ctrl-shift-w.
  • tab_next(): Navigates to the next tab using ctrl-pagedown.
  • tab_previous(): Navigates to the previous tab using ctrl-pageup.

These actions make it easier to manipulate tabs within the Guake terminal using voice commands.

docs.title: i3wm
docs.text:
The community/apps/i3wm directory contains files that provide support for the i3 window manager.

The core logic is in i3wm.py. This file defines a Talon module and context for interacting with i3. It uses the i3-msg command-line tool to send commands to the i3 window manager. The module defines settings for the i3 config path and the modifier key to use for i3 commands. It also defines a number of actions for common i3 operations, like switching workspaces, moving windows, and changing layouts.

The file i3wm.talon defines the voice commands that trigger the actions defined in i3wm.py. It includes commands for moving between workspaces (e.g., "port one", "port right"), focusing windows (e.g. "win left", "win up"), changing layouts (e.g. "win stacking", "win default"), and various other window management tasks such as floating, fullscreen, and moving to a scratchpad. Some more complex commands are also defined, such as "grow window" and "shrink window", which put i3 into resize mode and then move the window in the desired direction. This file also demonstrates using a custom user.text to provide an argument to the "launch" command.

docs.title: iterm
docs.text:
The community/apps/iterm directory contains configuration files for the iTerm2 terminal application on macOS, using the Talon voice control framework. It primarily defines actions and tags specific to iTerm2.

The directory contains the following files:

  • iterm.py: This Python file defines the core logic for interacting with iTerm2. It starts by defining the iTerm2 application context using the app.bundle: com.googlecode.iterm2. It then defines two action classes EditActions and UserActions. EditActions remaps the line_start and line_end actions to the home and end keys respectively. UserActions defines several tab related actions (tab_jump and tab_final) and terminal_clear_screen. Some commented out file_manager actions are also present in this file.
  • iterm.talon: This Talon file declares the iTerm2 context for macOS and applies several tags. Most notably, the terminal tag along with the user.generic_unix_shell, user.git, user.kubectl, user.tabs, and user.readline tags. These tags can be used by other Talon files to apply application specific commands and behavior. The commented out user.file_manager tag shows there was an attempt at implementing file manager support, which could be used in the future.

These two files work together to provide a better iTerm2 experience using Talon. The iterm.py file defines application specific actions and the iterm.talon file activates those actions, adds useful tags, and restricts the scope of these actions to iTerm2.

docs.title: jetbrains
docs.text:
The community/apps/jetbrains directory contains files that enable Talon to interact with JetBrains IDEs.

The core functionality is implemented in jetbrains.py, which defines actions to control JetBrains IDEs via their HTTP API. It determines the port for the active IDE based on the application's bundle ID or executable name, using the port_mapping dictionary. The script reads a nonce from a temporary file to authenticate with the IDE and then sends commands to the IDE's HTTP API. The file also defines Talon contexts that are activated when a JetBrains IDE is in focus. This allows the user to control the IDE with voice commands.

The file jetbrains.talon defines the voice commands for interacting with JetBrains IDEs. It uses the actions defined in jetbrains.py to execute commands. It includes commands for code completion, refactoring, navigation, search, code generation, recording macros, bookmarks, folding, clipboard operations, file creation, task management, Git integration, toggling various tool windows and settings, debugging, and more. The file also includes a wide variety of commands for moving the cursor around and selecting text, as well as more advanced commands that integrate with the JetBrains find/replace functionality, allowing users to quickly modify code.

These files work together to provide a comprehensive voice interface for JetBrains IDEs, allowing users to perform a wide range of tasks with voice commands.

docs.title: kde_konsole
docs.text:
The community/apps/kde_konsole directory contains files to enable Talon voice control for the KDE Konsole terminal emulator on Linux.

The primary file is kde_konsole.py, a Python file that defines the actions for Konsole. It uses Talon's context system to activate these actions when the "konsole" application is in focus. Here are the key features of this file: * Tab Management: It implements tab_jump, tab_open, tab_previous, tab_next, and tab_close using keyboard shortcuts specific to Konsole. Note that it uses actions.key to execute the shortcuts. It also includes an override for tab_reopen to notify the user that this function is not supported in KDE Konsole. * Window Management: The file includes an implementation for window_open using ctrl-shift-n for opening a new window. * Overrides for Edit Actions: This file overrides the default Linux edit.py actions for page_down, page_up, paste, copy and find to use the konsole-specific shortcuts. It also notes a TODO about selection issues.

The file kde_konsole.talon is a Talon script that activates the actions in the kde_konsole.py file. It also activates other useful Talon commands: * It activates the terminal tag, making commands from terminal.talon available. * It activates the user.generic_unix_shell tag, providing generic unix shell commands. * It activates tags for applications such as git and anaconda. * It activates the user.tabs tag. * It has a TODO about adding file manager support.

In summary, these two files work together to provide comprehensive voice control for the KDE Konsole application. The .py file defines the specific actions for the application, while the .talon file activates these actions and includes other useful commands.

docs.title: keepassx
docs.text:
The community/apps/keepassx directory contains Talon files for interacting with the KeePassX password manager.

The file keepassx_linux.talon defines voice commands for common KeePassX actions such as opening, saving, and closing databases, as well as actions for managing entries like adding, editing, copying user names or passwords, and finding entries. It supports basic keyboard shortcuts. For example, saying "open database" executes the ctrl-o keyboard shortcut, and "copy password" executes ctrl-c. It also supports more advanced commands like "find" to search for entries using a search term provided in the user's speech. For example, saying "find website" executes ctrl-f, inserts the text "website", and then searches for the term "website".

docs.title: kindle
docs.text:
The community/apps/kindle directory contains files that add support for the Kindle application to Talon.

The file kindle.py defines the app using the Talon Module system, specifying the app name and executable name for Windows. This allows Talon to identify when the Kindle app is active.

kindle_win.py defines the actions that are specific to the Windows version of the Kindle app. It uses the Talon context system to activate when the Kindle app is the active application on Windows. The file implements page_next, page_previous and page_jump actions that are used to navigate through the ebook using keyboard commands.

The kindle.talon file is a Talon script that activates the user.pages tag when the Kindle app is active. This tag is used by other scripts to provide page navigation functionality. In conjunction with other scripts, this enables the user to use voice commands like "next page" or "go to page 20".

docs.title: kubectl
docs.text:
The community/apps/kubectl directory provides a way to interact with the kubectl command-line tool using Talon. It defines a tag, lists, and commands that can be used in terminal environments.

The directory contains two primary files:

  • kubectl.py: This Python file defines the kubectl tag, and two lists: kubectl_action and kubectl_object. The kubectl tag is used to enable the commands defined in the kubectl.talon file. The kubectl_action list contains common kubectl actions such as "get", "delete", "describe", and "label". The kubectl_object list contains common Kubernetes objects such as "nodes", "pods", "namespaces", and "services".

  • kubectl.talon: This Talon file defines a series of voice commands for interacting with kubectl. Most commands are prefixed with "cube", followed by the desired action (e.g., "cube get" for kubectl get). The file leverages the lists defined in kubectl.py to allow for more complex commands like "cube get pods" which would translate to kubectl get pods. It also contains some helper commands like "cube shell" that will execute kubectl exec -it -- /bin/bash and "cube detach" which will execute ctrl-p followed by ctrl-q.

These files work together to provide a comprehensive set of voice commands for interacting with Kubernetes clusters using the kubectl CLI. The kubectl.py file sets up the context and definitions, while kubectl.talon defines the voice commands and integrates the lists to allow for the user to issue commands in a natural way.

docs.title: meld
docs.text:
The community/apps/meld directory contains configuration files for the Meld diff tool. It allows users to control the application with voice commands via Talon.

The meld.py file defines the application context and custom actions for Meld. It specifies that the configuration applies to windows where the application name is "Visual diff and merge tool" or the executable name is meld.exe.

The meld.py file defines the following actions:

  • tab_open: Opens a new tab (ctrl-n).
  • tab_previous: Switches to the previous tab (ctrl-alt-pageup).
  • tab_next: Switches to the next tab (ctrl-alt-pagedown).
  • tab_jump(number): Jumps to a tab by its number (using alt-number).

The meld.talon file contains the voice commands for Meld:

  • change next: triggers key(alt-down), which changes to the next change in a diff.
  • change (previous | last): triggers key(alt-up), which changes to the previous change in a diff.

Together, these files provide a basic set of voice commands for navigating and using Meld.

docs.title: mintty
docs.text:
The community/apps/mintty directory contains configuration for the mintty terminal application on Windows.

The primary file here is mintty_win.py. This file defines a Talon context for the mintty application, which allows for custom commands and actions to be used within the terminal.

Here's a breakdown of what mintty_win.py does:

  • App Definition: It defines the mintty application using mod.apps.mintty, matching windows with the name "Terminal" or "mintty.exe".
  • Context: It creates a Talon context that activates when the mintty application is in focus, applying several tags like "terminal", "user.generic_unix_shell", "user.file_manager", "user.git", and "user.kubectl". These tags allow other Talon scripts to provide further customizations when mintty is the active application.
  • Settings: The script has a setting for the path to cygpath.exe, which is used to convert Cygwin paths to Windows paths.
  • Path Conversion: The get_win_path function converts a Cygwin path to a Windows path using cygpath.exe. This is important because mintty uses Cygwin paths, but many Windows applications need the Windows path.
  • Edit Actions: It overrides the paste and copy actions to use shift-insert and ctrl-insert, respectively, as well as setting ctrl-u to delete_line.
  • User Actions: The bulk of the file focuses on defining custom user actions:
    • File Manager Actions: It provides actions for navigating directories, opening files and directories, creating new folders, selecting files and directories, and showing properties. These actions are designed to enable file management within the terminal environment. Notably, file_manager_current_path attempts to convert the Cygwin path from the window title into a Windows path.
    • Terminal Actions: It provides actions for listing directories, changing directories, clearing the screen, running the last command, and killing all processes. These actions streamline common terminal tasks.

In summary, mintty_win.py provides a comprehensive set of customizations for mintty that enhances user experience by enabling file management and common terminal actions through Talon voice commands, as well as providing a way to interpret Windows paths from Cygwin paths.

docs.title: nautilus
docs.text:
This directory contains configuration for the Nautilus file manager on Linux.

The file nautilus.talon defines the application tag nautilus and sets the user.tabs and user.file_manager tags, which are used to activate the commands defined in the corresponding user action classes.

The file nautilus.py defines the application definition for Nautilus, including supporting Caja, a fork of Nautilus. It then defines actions for the app and user namespaces.

The AppActions class implements tab_next and tab_previous using ctrl-pagedown and ctrl-pageup respectively. These commands allow the user to cycle between open tabs in Nautilus.

The UserActions class provides several useful actions: * tab_jump allows the user to jump to a specific tab using alt-number. * go_back and go_forward provide navigation between previously viewed directories. * file_manager_open_parent opens the parent directory. * file_manager_show_properties opens the properties window for the selected item. * file_manager_open_directory opens a directory at the specified path. * file_manager_new_folder creates a new folder, optionally with a given name. * file_manager_terminal_here opens a terminal in the current directory.

docs.title: nitro_reader
docs.text:
This directory contains configuration for the Nitro Reader 5 application.

The file nitro_reader_5.py defines the application and its actions. The application definition associates the name nitro_reader_five with the Nitro Reader 5 application based on its executable name. It then implements Talon actions for controlling the application. For example, app.tab_open() is implemented as ctrl-shift-o. Additionally, user.page_next(), user.page_previous(), user.page_jump(number: int), and user.page_final() are implemented using key presses or combinations.

The file nitro_reader_5.talon defines the tags that should be enabled when the nitro_reader_five application is active. This includes the user.pages and user.tabs tags, which enable generic page and tab navigation commands.

docs.title: notepad
docs.text:
The community/apps/notepad directory contains configuration files for the Notepad application within the Talon voice control system.

The file notepad.py defines a Talon module and context specifically for Notepad on Windows. It sets the application to be recognized by its executable name, notepad.exe, and then defines a custom action for retrieving the filename from the window title.
