This action splits the window title by " - " and returns the first part if it contains a ".". Otherwise, an empty string is returned.

The file notepad.talon defines the tags that are activated when the Notepad application is in focus. It activates the user.tabs and user.find_and_replace tags. This enables the use of commands associated with those tags in the notepad application.

docs.title: notepad++
docs.text:
The community/apps/notepad++ directory contains configuration for the Notepad++ application.

The file notepad++_win.py defines Talon bindings for Notepad++ on Windows. It defines the application context using mod.apps and ctx.matches which is triggered by the app name "Notepad++" or the executable name notepad++.exe. It also sets tags for find and replace, line commands, and tabs.

The file provides several action classes:

  • AppActions: Implements tab_previous and tab_next using ctrl-pageup and ctrl-pagedown respectively.
  • CodeActions: Implements toggle_comment with ctrl-q.
  • EditActions: Provides actions such as line_clone (ctrl-d), line_swap_up (ctrl-shift-up), line_swap_down (ctrl-shift-down), indent/unindent, and jump_line (ctrl-g). It also implements find_next and find_previous with enter and shift-enter respectively.
  • win_actions: Provides a filename action which extracts the file name from the window title.
  • UserActions: Includes actions such as select_next_occurrence and select_previous_occurrence, tab_jump to switch to specific tabs, and tab_final. This class also provides actions for find and replace functionality, such as find_everywhere, replace, replace_everywhere, replace_confirm, and replace_confirm_all. It also includes actions for toggling find options, like find_toggle_match_by_case, find_toggle_match_by_word, and find_toggle_match_by_regex.

This file provides a comprehensive set of actions to control Notepad++, including code editing, navigation, and find/replace functionality. The actions use standard Notepad++ keyboard shortcuts, making it easy to use for experienced users.

docs.title: obsidian
docs.text:
The community/apps/obsidian directory contains files to configure Talon for use with the Obsidian application.

The most important files are:

  • obsidian.talon: This file defines the Talon app definition for Obsidian. It simply defines the app and applies the user.tabs tag to it, which allows the use of generic tab commands within the app.

  • obsidian.py: This Python file defines the app name used by Talon to identify Obsidian. It also sets the default language for code blocks to Markdown in Obsidian. This is achieved using a context that matches on the Obsidian app, but only if the code language has not been forced by another context. If there is no other context defining a code language, then the language() action from the code action class will return markdown.

docs.title: okular
docs.text:
The community/apps/okular directory contains files to add Talon voice control for the Okular document viewer application.

The okular.talon file at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/apps/okular/okular.talon defines the application tag user.pages. This tag is activated when the Okular application is active, which enables the commands defined in the corresponding okular.py file.

The okular.py file at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/apps/okular/okular.py defines the application context for Okular on Windows and Linux (but not yet MacOS), and implements the user.pages actions using keybindings.

Specifically, the implemented user.pages actions are: * page_current(): gets the currently displayed page number using ctrl-g. * page_next(): goes to the next page using the l key. * page_previous(): goes to the previous page using the h key. * page_jump(number: int): navigates to a specific page using ctrl-g. * page_final(): navigates to the final page using ctrl-end.

These files work together to provide voice control for page navigation in the Okular application.

docs.title: opera
docs.text:
The community/apps/opera directory contains files that provide support for the Opera web browser within the Talon voice control framework.

The core application definition is in opera.py, which defines the app id for Opera on different operating systems. This allows Talon to recognize when Opera is the active application.

The files opera_mac.py and opera_win_linux.py contain platform-specific implementations of Talon actions for Opera.

opera_mac.py defines actions for macOS, including: * tab_duplicate: Duplicates the current tab by copying the current URL to the clipboard and opening a new tab with that URL. * tab_final: This is a placeholder function that will be implemented when Opera supports tab jumping, since Opera has no default shortcut for this. * tab_close_wrapper: Closes the current tab with a short delay. * tab_next and tab_previous: Navigates between tabs using cmd-alt-right and cmd-alt-left, respectively. * Various browser actions such as show_downloads, show_extensions, show_history, focus_page and reload_hard using their respective keyboard shortcuts. * go_back and go_forward: Navigates back and forward, first focusing the page content. * bookmark_tabs and go_home: These are not implemented since Opera does not support them with default keybinds.

opera_win_linux.py defines actions for Windows and Linux, including: * tab_duplicate: Duplicates the current tab by copying the current URL to the clipboard and opening a new tab with that URL. * tab_jump: Jumps to a specific tab by pressing ctrl-{number}. * tab_final: This is a placeholder function that will be implemented when Opera supports tab jumping, since Opera has no default shortcut for this. * tab_close_wrapper: Closes the current tab with a short delay. * tab_next and tab_previous: Navigates between tabs using ctrl-pagedown and ctrl-pageup, respectively. * Various browser actions such as bookmarks, show_downloads, show_extensions, focus_page and reload_hard using their respective keyboard shortcuts. * go_back and go_forward: Navigates back and forward, first focusing the page content. * bookmarks_bar, bookmark_tabs, and go_home: These are not implemented since Opera does not support them with default keybinds.

Finally, opera.talon is a Talon file that applies the browser and user.tabs tags when Opera is the active application. This enables browser-specific commands and tab-related user commands.

docs.title: orion
docs.text:
This directory contains the configuration files for the Orion browser within the Talon voice control system. The files provide a way to interact with and control the Orion browser using voice commands.

The most important files are:

  • orion.talon: This file defines the voice commands for interacting with Orion. It includes:

    • An app: orion declaration, which associates these commands with the Orion application.
    • Tag declarations tag(): browser and tag(): user.tabs, which enable other related commands or functionality.
    • A tab overview [open | close]: user.overview_tabs() command that toggles the tab overview using the user.overview_tabs() action.

  • orion.py: This Python file defines the actions that the orion.talon commands invoke. It uses the Talon API to:

    • Define the application context app: orion for the Orion app com.kagi.kagimacOS.
    • Implement the user.browser_open_address_in_new_tab() action by sending the shortcut cmd-enter.
    • Implement the browser.show_clear_cache(), browser.reload_hard(), browser.show_downloads(), and browser.show_extensions() actions via keyboard shortcuts.
    • Define the actions.user.overview_tabs() action which toggles the tab overview using the shortcut cmd-shift-\.

These files work together to provide a seamless voice control experience for Orion. The .talon file specifies the voice commands, and the .py file implements the corresponding actions that manipulate the Orion browser.

docs.title: outlook
docs.text:
The community/apps/outlook directory contains Talon scripts for interacting with Microsoft Outlook. It has two files, each providing a different set of commands based on the environment.

  • outlook_win.talon provides a set of commands for the Windows desktop application. These commands use standard keyboard shortcuts, such as ctrl-n for new email, ctrl-1 for the inbox, etc. This file provides a basic set of functionality to allow the user to navigate the application.
  • outlook_web.talon provides a much more comprehensive set of commands tailored to the web version of Outlook. These commands include actions like composing, sending, and replying to emails, as well as navigating the interface and managing the email list. It even includes the ability to move through the UI and mark messages as read, unread, or junk. There is a significant overlap between the functions provided by these two files, with outlook_web.talon being significantly more comprehensive. Both files use the key() action to map voice commands to keyboard shortcuts.

docs.title: powershell
docs.text:
The community/apps/powershell directory contains files to enable Talon to interact with the Windows Powershell application.

The most important files are:

  • powershell_win.talon: This file sets up the basic context for the PowerShell application. It activates the terminal and generic_windows_shell tags, which makes the commands defined in terminal.talon and other shell-related files available. It also enables git, anaconda, and file_manager tags. The tags determine which commands are active.

  • powershell_win.py: This Python file defines actions specific to interacting with the Windows PowerShell application. It includes actions for:

    • Refreshing the window title to show the current directory (file_manager_refresh_title).
    • Navigating to the parent directory (file_manager_open_parent).
    • Getting the current path (file_manager_current_path).
    • Opening a specified directory (file_manager_open_directory).
    • Selecting a specified directory or file (file_manager_select_directory and file_manager_select_file).
    • Creating a new folder (file_manager_new_folder).
    • Opening a file (file_manager_open_file).
    • Opening a volume/drive (file_manager_open_volume).

    It also has a setting powershell_always_refresh_title that controls if the title is updated after every directory change.

    The edit.delete_line action is overridden to send the escape key, as that cancels command input in PowerShell. * README.md: This file provides instructions for setting up the PowerShell profile to show the current directory in the window title. It explains that PowerShell does not show the current path by default and provides a PowerShell function that can be added to the user's profile to change the window title, so that Talon can use it to understand the current path. The guide also mentions that user.powershell_always_refresh_title setting can be disabled when a title-updating prompt is set.

These files work together to allow users to control Powershell using Talon voice commands. The .talon file defines when the actions are active, the .py file implements the actions, and the README.md explains some configuration needed to use the actions effectively. The Python file relies on the title being updated to get the current path from the window.

docs.title: protonmail
docs.text:
The community/apps/protonmail directory contains Talon voice commands for interacting with the ProtonMail web application.

The primary file is protonmail.talon, which defines a set of voice commands for navigating and interacting with ProtonMail.

The protonmail.talon file is structured into several sections:

  • General: Contains commands that apply across the application, such as opening the help menu (key(?)), focusing the search bar (key(/)), confirming active selections (key(enter)), closing active elements (key(escape)), and opening the command palette (key(shift-space)).
  • Composer: Includes commands for composing new messages (key(c)), sending messages (key(ctrl-enter)), and saving drafts (key(ctrl-s)).
  • Mail: This is the largest section, with commands for:
    • Jumping: Navigating to specific folders like the inbox (key(g), key(i)), drafts (key(g), key(d)), sent messages (key(g), key(s)), starred messages (key(g), key(.)), the archive (key(g), key(a)), spam folder (key(g), key(x)), and trash (key(g), key(t)).
    • Navigation: Moving between messages using arrow keys (key(up), key(down), key(left), key(right)) and j and k for older and newer messages (key(k), key(j)), respectively. Includes opening messages (key(enter)) and going back to the thread list (key(escape)).
    • Threadlist: Selecting and deselecting all messages (key(*), key(a), key(*), key(n)), selecting a specific message (key(x)), marking messages as read or unread (key(r), key(u)), starring messages (key(.)), and moving messages to inbox, trash, archive, or spam (key(i), key(t), key(a), key(s)).
    • Actions: Replying to a message (key(shift-r)), replying all (key(shift-a)), and forwarding a message (key(shift-f)).
  • Contacts: Commands for interacting with the contacts section, including:
    • Contact List: Navigating the list with arrow keys (key(up), key(down), key(right)) and deleting contacts (key(t)).
    • Contact Details: Exiting contact details view (key(left)) and saving contact changes (key(ctrl-s)).

These commands provide a comprehensive voice interface for managing a ProtonMail inbox, composing emails, and managing contacts.

docs.title: rstudio
docs.text:
The community/apps/rstudio directory contains a single file, rstudio_mac.talon. This file defines Talon voice commands for interacting with the RStudio application on macOS.

The file is a Talon script, which uses a specific syntax to bind voice commands to keyboard shortcuts. The script is organized into sections, such as "Moving around and formatting", "Folding", "Find and replace", etc., each section grouping related functionality. The script maps voice commands to keyboard shortcuts in RStudio that allow for:

  • Executing code
    • Running the current line, document, selection, or chunk
    • Running code from the top, to the end, or for a function
    • Executing all code, the knitter, or the profiler
  • Navigating the interface
    • Jumping between locations, such as tabs or source files
    • Moving to different panes, such as the console or help
    • Navigating through plots or code chunks
  • Editing code
    • Indenting or commenting lines
    • Reformatting code or comments
    • Deleting lines or words
    • Duplicating or moving lines
    • Selecting code blocks
    • Adding or moving cursors
  • Folding code
    • Folding or unfolding sections of code
  • Find and replace
    • Finding text in the current file or project
    • Navigating search results
  • Using devtools
    • Building, loading, testing, documenting, or checking R packages
  • Debugging
    • Setting and stepping through breakpoints
    • Continuing or stopping debugging
  • Git/SVN
    • Running git diff or commit
  • Other RStudio features
    • Restarting an R session
    • Opening help pages
    • Zooming or viewing all content

Overall, rstudio_mac.talon provides a comprehensive set of voice commands that allow users to control most aspects of the RStudio IDE. The file includes a comment section at the end listing additional RStudio keyboard shortcuts that could be enabled as voice commands.

docs.title: safari
docs.text:
The community/apps/safari directory contains configuration files that extend Talon's functionality for the Safari web browser on macOS. It includes two files: safari.py and safari.talon.

safari.py contains the core Python code that defines how Talon interacts with Safari. It begins by importing necessary modules from Talon. It then defines a Talon Context that is active when the application is Safari, using its bundle IDs. The file defines two action classes: UserActions and BrowserActions.

UserActions currently includes one action: * browser_open_address_in_new_tab: Opens the current address in a new tab (using cmd-enter).

BrowserActions defines several custom actions:

  • address: Retrieves the current URL from the browser window. It first tries to get the address from the AXTextField, and if that fails it falls back to an applescript call.
  • bookmark_tabs: This function is a placeholder that raises a NotImplementedError because Safari does not have a default shortcut for this functionality.
  • show_clear_cache: This function is also a placeholder, raising NotImplementedError because Safari does not directly support this action.
  • reload_hard: Reloads the current page, bypassing the cache (cmd-alt-r).
  • show_downloads: Opens the downloads window (cmd-alt-l).
  • show_extensions: Opens the extensions preferences window (cmd-, tab:8 space).

safari.talon is a simple Talon file that activates the browser and user.tabs tags when the Safari application is active. This enables a variety of default browser commands. The browser tag allows general browser interactions and the user.tabs tag enables tab related commands.

docs.title: signal
docs.text:
The community/apps/signal directory contains files that provide support for the Signal application using the Talon voice control system.

The primary file in this directory is signal_linux.talon. This Talon script defines voice commands for the Signal desktop application on Linux. It includes commands for navigation, such as moving between chats, searching, and opening preferences, as well as commands for interacting with messages, like replying, reacting, and deleting. It also has commands for the composer window, like attaching files, sending messages, and removing link previews.

This file primarily maps voice commands to keyboard shortcuts within the Signal application. It makes the app easier to use without having to use the mouse or memorize keyboard shortcuts.

docs.title: slack
docs.text:
The community/apps/slack directory contains files that provide support for using Talon with the Slack application on different operating systems.

The core functionality is split between .talon files, which define voice commands, and .py files, which define the underlying Python actions called by the voice commands. The directory contains slack.py, slack_win.py, slack_mac.py, slack_win.talon, and slack_mac.talon. The slack.py file defines the Slack application context and includes an edit.line_insert_down action for both Mac and Windows.

The files that start with slack_win are specific to Windows, and those that start with slack_mac are specific to macOS.

Here's a breakdown of the key files and their roles:

  • slack.py: This file defines the context for the Slack application using the app.name, app.bundle, and browser.host properties. It also includes an overridden edit.line_insert_down action that adds a line break after the current line, instead of before, for both macOS and Windows.
  • slack_win.talon: This file defines the voice commands for Slack on Windows and Linux. It includes commands for navigating workspaces, channels, and threads, as well as messaging actions like adding a line break, reacting to messages, inserting code, and formatting text. It also includes commands for controlling calls and other miscellaneous actions, like toggling the sidebars and accessing shortcuts.
  • slack_win.py: This file defines Python actions that are called by the voice commands in slack_win.talon. It includes actions for switching workspaces, opening the channel picker, navigating channels, marking messages as read, and uploading files.
  • slack_mac.talon: This file defines the voice commands for Slack on macOS. It includes commands similar to those in slack_win.talon, but uses macOS specific keyboard shortcuts.
  • slack_mac.py: This file defines Python actions that are called by the voice commands in slack_mac.talon. It includes actions similar to those in slack_win.py, but uses macOS specific keyboard shortcuts.

The slack_win and slack_mac files implement the same functionality but use different keyboard shortcuts that are specific to Windows/Linux and macOS, respectively.

docs.title: stata
docs.text:
The community/apps/stata directory contains files that integrate the Talon voice control system with Stata, a statistical software package.

The stata_do_file_editor.talon file, found at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/apps/stata/stata_do_file_editor.talon, provides voice commands specifically for the Stata Do-file editor. This file uses Talon's declarative syntax to define when the commands are active (when the application is Stata and the window title matches /^Do-file Editor/). It then defines several commands: * do this executes the currently selected lines of code (or current line if none selected). * do line executes the current line. * do (all | file) executes the entire contents of the Do-file editor. * do way up executes all lines from the beginning of the file to the current line. * do way down executes all lines from the current line to the end of the file.

The stata.py file, located at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/apps/stata/stata.py, is a Python module that defines the application context for Stata. This allows Talon to identify when Stata is the active application and activate the commands defined in stata_do_file_editor.talon. It also defines the code language as "stata", which enables Talon to provide better support for code in this language.

docs.title: sumatrapdf
docs.text:
This directory contains files that add support for the SumatraPDF application to Talon.

The file sumatrapdf.talon declares the application and its associated tags. This file defines the user.pages and user.tabs tags, which are used by other files to provide application-specific functionality.

The file sumatrapdf.py defines the application context, and implements custom actions specific to SumatraPDF.

The mod.apps.sumatrapdf definition specifies that the following context should be loaded when the application name is "SumatraPDF" or when the executable name matches the regex ^sumatrapdf\.exe$, and the OS is Windows.

The app_actions class implements the tab_open action, which opens a file using ctrl-o.

The EditActions class implements the zoom_in and zoom_out actions using the + and - keys, respectively.

The UserActions class implements custom actions related to pages and tabs. It implements page_current, page_next, page_previous, page_jump, page_final, page_rotate_right, and page_rotate_left, for page navigation and manipulation. It also implements tab_jump and tab_final for tab navigation.

These files work together to provide a comprehensive set of Talon commands for interacting with SumatraPDF.

docs.title: talon
docs.text:
The community/apps/talon directory contains applications that extend the functionality of Talon. It contains subdirectories for specific applications, each with its own set of files.

The talon_repl subdirectory contains files that provide support for using Talon within the Talon REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop). This allows users to interact with Talon directly, explore its API, and test code snippets.

  • talon_repl.py defines the application context for the Talon REPL and sets the default code language to "python".
  • talon_repl.talon provides core functionality for the REPL, such as Python support, history, and debug commands. The debug commands allow users to replay commands, output the value of actions, lists, scopes, tags, settings and modes, as well as lists of running applications and open windows.

The talon_debug_window subdirectory contains files for interacting with the Talon Debug window, a tool available in the Talon beta for debugging Talon scripts.

  • talon_debug_window.py defines the application tags that identify the Talon Debug window on macOS and Windows.
  • talon_debug_window.talon defines commands that are active when the Talon Debug window is focused. It allows users to dictate key combinations and insert Talon actions, lists, captures, settings, and applications into the debug window's search field.

These subdirectories provide tools that allow Talon users to interact with the Talon environment itself using voice commands. The Python files define the application context using window matching criteria, and the Talon files provide the commands and tags that are active within that context.

docs.title: talon_debug_window
docs.text:
The community/apps/talon/talon_debug_window directory contains files for interacting with the Talon Debug window, a tool available in the Talon beta for debugging Talon scripts.

The primary files in this directory are:

  • talon_debug_window.py: This Python file defines the application tags that identify the Talon Debug window. It uses mod.apps.talon_debug_window to define window matching criteria for macOS and Windows. These criteria use the application bundle on macOS and the executable name on Windows to identify the Talon Debug window.

  • talon_debug_window.talon: This Talon file defines commands that are active when the Talon Debug window is focused. It allows users to dictate key combinations and insert Talon actions, lists, captures, settings, and applications into the debug window's search field. This file also includes commented-out lines that show how to enable the user.talon_populate_lists tag which is used to access the full lists of actions, scopes, modes etc in talon.

These files work together to provide a means of interacting with the Talon Debug window using voice commands. The Python file establishes the application context, and the Talon file provides the commands and tags that are active within that context.

docs.title: talon_repl
docs.text:
The community/apps/talon/talon_repl directory contains files that provide support for using Talon within the Talon REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop). This allows users to interact with Talon directly, explore its API, and test code snippets.

The primary files are:

  • talon_repl.py: This Python file defines the application context for the Talon REPL. It declares the application using mod.apps.talon_repl, which uses regular expressions to match the title of the Talon REPL window. It also sets the default code language to "python" when the user.code_language_forced tag is not active. This ensures that code commands will function correctly in the REPL.

  • talon_repl.talon: This Talon file contains the core functionality for the REPL. It includes the user.talon_python and user.readline tags, which enable Python functionality and readline-like features such as history. It also includes a commented-out user.talon_populate_lists tag. When enabled this tag populates lists of actions, scopes and modes that can be used in debug commands (described below). There are a series of debugging commands that send various commands to the REPL:

    • test last, test <phrase>, and test numb <number_small> replay a previous command from history and run it.
    • debug action {user.talon_actions}, debug list {user.talon_lists}, and debug scope {user.talon_scopes} output the value of a Talon action, list, or scope. These rely on the user.talon_populate_lists tag and will not function when that tag is commented out.
    • debug tags, debug settings, and debug modes output the value of all Talon tags, settings, and modes.
    • debug running apps and debug all windows output a list of the running applications and all open windows.
    • debug {user.running} windows uses the user.talon_debug_app_windows action to output a list of windows for the application specified by the {user.running} grammar.

In summary, these files provide the necessary context and commands for effectively interacting with the Talon REPL. The Python file sets up the context and a default code language, while the Talon file provides useful commands for debugging and interacting with the REPL, including running commands, exploring the Talon registry, and interacting with Talon's user interface.

docs.title: taskwarrior
docs.text:
The community/apps/taskwarrior directory contains the Talon voice commands for interacting with the Taskwarrior command-line task management application.

The file taskwarrior_linux.talon provides the core voice commands for Taskwarrior. This file is specific to Linux, as indicated by the os: linux line at the beginning. The tag: terminal and tag: user.taskwarrior lines indicate that these commands are active within a terminal application when the user also has the taskwarrior tag enabled.

Here are some of the key voice commands defined in this file:

  • General Taskwarrior commands:

    • task version: Runs task --version to show the Taskwarrior version.
    • task commands: Runs task commands to list available Taskwarrior commands.
    • task help: Runs task help to display Taskwarrior help.

  • Task List commands:

    • task list: Runs task list to list all tasks.
    • task list orphans: Runs task project: list to list tasks without a project.
    • task list untagged: Runs task tags.none: list to list tasks without tags.
    • task list <user.text>: Runs task list {text} to list tasks matching a filter.
    • task list project: Runs task list project: to start filtering by project.
    • task list project <user.text>: Runs task list project:{text} to list tasks for a specific project.

  • Task Add commands:

    • task add: Runs task add to start adding a new task.
    • task add <user.text>: Runs task add {text} to add a new task with the specified description.
    • task undo: Runs task undo to undo the last command.

  • Task Next command:

    • (tasks | task next): Runs task next to show the next task.

  • Task Editing and Completion commands:

    • task <number> edit$: Runs task {number} edit to edit the task with the given number.
    • task <number> done$: Runs task {number} done to mark the task with the given number as done.
    • task <number> delete$: Runs task {number} delete to delete the task with the given number.

These commands allow users to manage their Taskwarrior tasks entirely via voice, making it a useful tool for hands-free task management.

docs.title: teams
docs.text:
The community/apps/teams directory contains files that provide support for using the Talon voice control system with Microsoft Teams. The files are organized to handle differences between operating systems.

Here's a breakdown of the files and how they work together:

  • teams.talon: This is the core file for Windows and Linux. It defines a set of voice commands that map to various Teams actions, such as opening different sections of the app (activity, chat, teams, etc.), navigating the interface, composing messages, and managing calls and meetings. It specifies app: microsoft_teams and os: windows or os: linux so that Talon only activates the commands in this file when Microsoft Teams is the active application on Windows or Linux.
  • teams_linux.py: This Python file provides additional functionality specific to Linux. Currently, it includes actions for zooming in, zooming out, and resetting the zoom level, which are not covered by the generic keyboard shortcuts in teams.talon. It specifies app: microsoft_teams and os: linux so that Talon only activates the actions in this file when Microsoft Teams is the active application on Linux.
  • teams.py: This file defines the application that is being targeted, in this case, Microsoft Teams. It specifies that the application name matches the /teams/ or /Teams/ patterns on Linux. This allows Talon to recognize the app regardless of the case of the application executable.
  • teams_mac.talon: This file is the counterpart to teams.talon for macOS. It defines similar voice commands but uses macOS-specific keyboard shortcuts (e.g., super key instead of ctrl). It also includes a few extra commands specific to macOS, like open history, and leave team meeting. It specifies app: com.microsoft.teams and os: mac so that Talon only activates the commands in this file when Microsoft Teams is the active application on macOS.

In summary, the files in this directory provide a comprehensive set of voice commands for Microsoft Teams, tailored to each operating system. The teams.talon file is the main file for Windows and Linux, while teams_linux.py adds operating system specific functionality. The teams_mac.talon file provides support for macOS, and teams.py defines how the app is matched by Talon.

docs.title: terminator
docs.text:
The community/apps/terminator directory contains configuration for the Terminator terminal application on Linux.

The file terminator_linux.py defines Talon bindings for the Terminator terminal emulator on Linux. It does this by first defining a module, then an app that matches when app.exe is terminator or app.name is Terminator under Linux, then it defines a context that matches the Terminator app, and adds the terminal, user.tabs, user.splits, user.generic_unix_shell, user.git, and user.kubectl tags to that context. Then, it defines action classes which override or implement actions within the Terminator context.

Specifically, the user_actions class implements the user.splits actions using alt and shift-ctrl keybindings. It defines actions such as split_window_right, split_window_left, split_window_down, split_window_up, split_window_vertically, split_window_horizontally, split_flip, split_maximize, split_reset, split_window, split_clear, split_clear_all, split_next, and split_last. These are common operations when using Terminator which is a terminal emulator that allows for multiple split panes.

The AppActions class overrides app.tab_open, app.tab_previous, app.tab_next, and app.tab_close actions. It also overrides app.window_open and app.window_close actions.

Finally, the EditActions class overrides edit.page_down, edit.page_up, edit.paste, edit.copy, edit.find, and edit.delete_line actions. These are common operations within terminal emulators that must be implemented using different keybindings than the defaults.

docs.title: termite
docs.text:
The community/apps/termite directory contains configuration for the termite terminal application within the Talon voice control system.

The file termite.talon configures Talon to understand when the termite application is in focus and sets up some basic commands. It also applies several tags that enable additional functionality from other parts of the Talon configuration. Here's a breakdown:

  • app: termite: This line specifies that the following configuration only applies when the active application is termite.
  • and not win.title: /VIM/: This line specifically excludes the configuration from applying to a termite window if the title contains /VIM/.
  • tag(): user.kubectl, tag(): user.git, tag(): user.taskwarrior, tag(): terminal: These lines apply tags to the application, which allow other parts of the Talon configuration to be used. For example user.git will enable all git commands while in a termite window and terminal will enable basic terminal navigation commands.
  • Selection mode commands: The file contains a few specific commands for termite, such as shell yank, shell select and shell insert. These commands make it easier to interact with the terminal and include visual selection commands such as visual line and visual line mode.

These configurations allow the user to use Talon voice commands to interact with the termite terminal application, including enabling specific features based on context.

docs.title: terraform
docs.text:
The community/apps/terraform directory provides Talon voice commands for interacting with the Terraform CLI.

The terraform.py file, located at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/apps/terraform/terraform.py, defines a Talon module and a tag named terraform_client. This tag is used to enable the Terraform commands defined in terraform.talon.

The terraform.talon file, located at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/apps/terraform/terraform.talon, contains the core voice commands for Terraform. When the terminal tag and the user.terraform_client tag are both active, these commands become available. The file defines commands for common Terraform operations such as terraform apply, terraform destroy, terraform init, and terraform plan. It also includes commands for formatting (terraform fmt), help (terraform -help), and state management (terraform state mv). These commands generally execute the corresponding Terraform CLI command by inserting the command string into the terminal.

docs.title: thunderbird
docs.text:
This directory contains the Talon files for controlling the Thunderbird email client. It includes configurations for different operating systems and different contexts within the application.

The core functionality is defined in thunderbird.talon, thunderbird.py, thunderbird_linux.py, thunderbird_win.py, and thunderbird_mac.py. The thunderbird.talon file sets the base tag and provides tab navigation commands. thunderbird.py defines the application contexts for Thunderbird, the inbox, the calendar, tasks, the composer window, and contacts. It also defines the thunderbird_mod and thunderbird_calendar_view actions, which are implemented in the OS specific Python files. The OS-specific Python files, thunderbird_linux.py, thunderbird_win.py, and thunderbird_mac.py, contain the implementations of the actions defined in thunderbird.py. They each use ctrl, alt, or cmd for keyboard shortcuts, depending on the operating system.

The files thunderbird_inbox.talon, thunderbird_composer.talon, thunderbird_calendar.talon, thunderbird_contacts.talon, and thunderbird_tasks.talon provide context-specific commands.

  • thunderbird_inbox.talon: This file contains commands for navigating, marking, and sending messages in the main inbox view, such as moving between messages, marking messages read or unread, and replying to messages.
  • thunderbird_composer.talon: This file provides commands for the message composition window, like saving, printing, sending, and pasting text. It also has commands for toggling the contacts list and navigating back to the inbox.
  • thunderbird_calendar.talon: This file defines commands for creating new events and tasks, deleting selected ones, and switching between different calendar views (day, week, month).
  • thunderbird_contacts.talon: This file contains commands for managing contacts, including creating new ones, editing, deleting, printing, and messaging contacts, as well as navigating the contact list.
  • thunderbird_tasks.talon: This file includes commands for creating new tasks and events as well as deleting selected ones.

docs.title: tmux
docs.text:
The community/apps/tmux directory contains files that enable Talon to interact with the tmux terminal multiplexer. This allows users to control tmux using voice commands.

The core logic is in tmux.py. This file defines a Talon module for tmux with several key features:

  • Application Definition: It defines an application named tmux that is active when both the terminal and user.tmux tags are active.
  • Settings: It defines a setting tmux_prefix_key that allows users to specify the prefix key used for all tmux commands, defaulting to ctrl-b.
  • Action Class: It defines a class, TmuxActions, with a set of actions to interact with tmux:
    • tmux_prefix: Sends the tmux prefix key.
    • tmux_keybind: Sends the tmux prefix followed by a specified key.
    • tmux_enter_command: Enters tmux command mode, optionally inserting a command.
    • tmux_execute_command: Executes a tmux command.
    • tmux_execute_command_with_confirmation: Executes a tmux command with a confirmation prompt.
  • Context: It defines a context that is active when the tmux application is active and then adds actions for that context for both the app and user classes:
    • App Actions: Overrides the tab_open, tab_next and tab_previous actions to execute tmux commands for opening, navigating tabs.
    • User Actions: Provides a wide range of actions to control panes (splits) and windows (tabs) within tmux:
      • tab_jump: Jumps to a specific tab by number.
      • tab_close_wrapper: Closes the current tab with confirmation.
      • split_window_right, split_window_left, split_window_down, split_window_up: Create splits in various directions.
      • split_flip: Changes the layout of the splits.
      • split_window_vertically, split_window_horizontally: Split the window.
      • split_maximize, split_reset: Maximizes or resets the size of the current pane.
      • split_window: Splits the window horizontally by default.
      • split_clear: Closes the current pane with confirmation.
      • split_next: Selects the next pane.
      • split_last: Selects the last pane.
      • split_number: Selects a pane by its index.

tmux_linux.talon provides basic voice commands for tmux on Linux:

  • Tags: It declares the user.tmux tag.
  • Prefix: It defines a command mux that can be used to invoke many tmux commands.
  • Session Management: Provides commands for creating, listing, naming and killing tmux sessions
  • Window Management: Provides commands for creating, selecting, renaming and closing windows (tabs).
  • Pane Management: Provides commands for splitting the screen, navigating between panes, and closing panes.
  • Pane Numbers: Provides a command to show the numbers of all panes on the screen.

tmux.talon sets up the basic tags and commands for use with tmux:

  • Tags: It declares the user.splits and user.tabs tags, which are used in other files to provide consistent command for splits and tabs across different applications.
  • Pane Navigation: It defines commands for navigating between splits using arrow keys and a command for showing the pane numbers.
  • Note: It includes a note stating that the user needs to add a configuration to match the tmux app and activate the user.tmux tag, as it is not active by default.

These files work together to provide a comprehensive way to control tmux using Talon. The tmux.py file defines the actions that can be performed, tmux_linux.talon and tmux.talon provide the voice commands that trigger those actions. Users need to ensure that they have a configuration that activates the user.tmux tag when tmux is active, as described in the tmux.talon file.

docs.title: twitter
docs.text:
The community/apps/twitter directory contains configuration files for controlling the Twitter web application using Talon voice commands.

The file twitter.talon defines a set of voice commands for navigating and interacting with Twitter in a web browser. It uses the browser tag to activate the commands when the active browser is displaying a twitter.com page.

The file defines commands for common actions, such as: * Navigating the Twitter feed using next tweet and previous tweet. * Loading new tweets or jumping to the top of the page. * Accessing specific Twitter pages via shortcuts, such as go home, go explore, and go notifications. * Performing actions on tweets, such as new tweet, send tweet, like message, reply message, and re tweet. * Other tweet actions like share tweet, bookmark, mute account, and block account are also available. * Opening a tweet for details using open details or expanding a photo with expand photo.

docs.title: visualstudio
docs.text:
The community/apps/visualstudio directory contains files that provide Talon voice control for Microsoft Visual Studio on Windows.

The most important files are:

  • visual_studio.py: This file defines the core functionality for Visual Studio support in Talon. It includes:
    • App identification for Visual Studio 2019 and 2022 via mod.apps
    • Context matching for the Visual Studio application via ctx.matches
    • Action implementations for app, code, edit, win, and user namespaces. These include standard functions like tab management, code commenting, indentation, saving files, line manipulation, and find/replace functionality. It also includes support for the MultiCaretBooster extension which adds multi cursor functionality to Visual Studio.
    • The WinActions.filename() is of particular interest. It attempts to extract the current file name from the window title by stripping off the suffix. It relies on a Visual Studio extension to ensure consistent window titles.
  • visual_studio.talon: This file contains the Talon commands for interacting with Visual Studio. It includes:
    • Tag activation for user.tabs, user.line_commands, user.find_and_replace, and user.multiple_cursors.
    • Commands for opening and closing various panels, such as the solution explorer, properties, output, class, errors, and breakpoints.
    • Commands for showing settings, toggling fullscreen and wrapping, and performing file operations, such as creating, renaming, revealing and opening files.
    • Commands for code navigation, including hints, definitions, references, and formatting.
    • Commands for refactoring code, such as renaming fields, interfaces, and methods.
    • Commands for code navigation such as "go to" declaration, type, and members.
    • Commands for using bookmarks, such as toggling, going to next, and going to last.
    • Commands for folding code, such as toggling folds and collapsing definitions.
    • Commands for debugging, such as setting breakpoints and stepping through code.
  • visual_studio_win.py: This file contains additional platform specific actions for Visual Studio on Windows. It includes implementations for app, code, and user namespaces, and specifically includes the multiple cursor functionality which requires the MultiCaretBooster extension.

These files work together to enable a comprehensive voice control experience for Visual Studio, including application context, keyboard shortcuts, code editing, refactoring, navigation, and debugging.

docs.title: vivaldi
docs.text:
The community/apps/vivaldi directory contains files that provide Talon voice control support for the Vivaldi web browser.

The core functionality is provided by the following files:

  • vivaldi.talon: This file defines the Talon app tag for Vivaldi, which is browser, user.tabs, and user.command_search, and provides voice commands for opening different panels in Vivaldi. These commands call actions defined in the Python files.

  • vivaldi.py: This file contains the bulk of the Vivaldi support. It defines the application context for Vivaldi across all supported operating systems, and it contains action implementations for common browser commands, such as tab_close_wrapper, tab_jump, command_search, show_extensions, focus_address, focus_page, bookmarks, show_downloads, and go, as well as actions specific to Vivaldi such as toggling the history, bookmarks, downloads, and notes panels.

  • vivaldi_mac.py: This file provides platform-specific overrides for macOS, including different keyboard shortcuts for some actions. These overrides include actions for toggling the history, downloads, and notes panels, and also includes overrides for tab navigation, extensions, bookmarks, and focusing the address bar.

These files work together to provide a comprehensive voice control experience for Vivaldi. The .talon file provides the basic context and commands, while the Python files define the actions that those commands trigger. The vivaldi_mac.py file ensures that the correct actions are performed on macOS.

docs.title: vscode
docs.text:
The community/apps/vscode directory provides support for using Talon with VS Code. It includes a variety of files and subdirectories that handle different aspects of the integration, such as sending commands to VS Code, managing UI elements, and providing actions for common tasks.

The community/apps/vscode directory contains two important subdirectories:

  • command_client: This directory contains the implementation for sending commands to VS Code and other applications using a file-based RPC mechanism. It provides a way for Talon to interact with applications that have a server-side component to handle commands. See its README.md for a detailed explanation of how it works.
  • command_client/rpc_client: This subdirectory contains the low-level implementation of the file-based RPC mechanism used by the command client. It handles the details of writing requests to files, triggering command execution, and reading responses. See its README.md for details.

Here's a breakdown of the important files in the community/apps/vscode directory:

  • vscode.py: This file defines the core actions and context for VS Code. It includes application matching logic for different operating systems, as well as action implementations for common operations like opening/closing tabs and windows, toggling comments, and indenting code. It also provides actions that use the command client to send commands, and fall back to the command palette if the command server is not running. It also includes actions for controlling splits, multiple cursors, tabs, and find/replace. It exposes some functionality that is provided directly by VS Code and not by any plugins or extensions, while also providing actions for some common Talon user actions that are not supported directly by VS Code itself.

  • vscode_terminal.talon: This file defines a tag to activate the terminal commands when the VS Code terminal is focused. It looks for a special string in the window title that indicates that the terminal has focus. The string, which is set by configuring VS Code's window.title setting, is focus:[Terminal]. It enables the terminal tag when this is detected.

  • README.md: This README provides general information about using Talon with VS Code. It recommends installing the VS Code Talon extension pack for advanced commands, and includes instructions for setting up the window title setting for the terminal integration. It also provides a brief description of the Cursorless integration.

  • vscode.talon: This file contains a large set of Talon commands for interacting with VS Code. It includes commands for controlling the sidebar, panels, settings, snippets, display, files, language features, code navigation, git, testing, debugging, terminals, and more. Many of these commands use the user.vscode action, which sends commands to VS Code through the command client. It also uses the user.vscode_with_plugin which is only used by tab_jump, because tab_jump has to fall back to a plugin implementation when trying to jump to a tab greater than 9 on non-macOS platforms. This is a good example of how the module is extended using actions.

In summary, these files provide a comprehensive set of tools for interacting with VS Code from Talon. The command client provides a robust way to send commands, while the Talon scripts provide actions for common tasks and UI elements. The vscode.talon file provides a rich set of commands for most of VS Code's major features, and the vscode.py file implements the core logic for each of those actions.

docs.title: command_client
docs.text:
The community/apps/vscode/command_client directory contains the Talon client-side implementation for communicating with command servers in applications like VS Code and Visual Studio using a file-based RPC mechanism.

The most important part of this directory is the rpc_client subdirectory. See its README.md for a detailed explanation of how it works. In summary, it provides a robust way for Talon to send commands to external applications, using temporary files for communication, and it's designed to avoid race conditions.

The files in the root of the command_client directory provide actions for users to easily send commands to applications, using either this file-based RPC mechanism, or, if not available, falling back to the command palette.

Here's a summary of the key files and how they interact:

  • command_client.py: This file defines the main user-facing actions to run commands via the RPC client. It contains actions like run_rpc_command, run_rpc_command_and_wait, and run_rpc_command_get, which can be called from Talon scripts to send commands to applications. It also defines actions to get the command server directory and to trigger the command execution on the server-side (using a keyboard shortcut). It also handles a "pre-phrase signal" which is used by other parts of Talon to indicate the start of a spoken phrase.

  • vscode.py: This file provides the application-specific configuration and actions for VS Code. It defines the command server directory (vscode-command-server). It includes actions like vscode and vscode_and_wait, which use the command client to send commands, and fall back to the command palette if the command server is not running. It also provides backwards-compatible actions that call through to actions in command_client.py. It also handles the platform-specific keyboard shortcut for triggering the command execution.

  • visual_studio.py: This file provides the application-specific configuration for Visual Studio, similar to vscode.py. It defines the command server directory (visual-studio-command-server).

  • command_client_tag.py: This file defines the command_client tag, which is used to enable the command client functionality in contexts. It also defines the command_server_directory action, which is the core requirement for any application to use the command client.

  • README.md: This README provides a brief overview of the directory and instructions on how to contribute.

The main workflow is: 1. An application (like VS Code) must have a server-side component that implements the file-based RPC using temporary files. 2. A Talon script will call actions.user.vscode(...) or actions.user.visual_studio(...). 3. The corresponding file (e.g. vscode.py) defines the command_server_directory for its application, and calls the command_server_or_client_fallback action. 4. The command_server_or_client_fallback will in turn call the run_command function in command_client.py. 5. run_command uses the rpc_client_run_command action in rpc_client/rpc_client.py to send the command to the application, which communicates via temporary files. 6. The appropriate application receives the command, executes it, and returns a result, which is returned by the run_command action. 7. Finally, the vscode and visual_studio actions return, or, if the command server is not available, they fall back to the command palette.

In summary, this directory provides a flexible mechanism for Talon to communicate with external applications, with specific support for VS Code and Visual Studio, but is designed to be extensible to other applications by defining the command_server_directory and optionally overriding the command trigger keystroke.

docs.title: rpc_client
docs.text:
This directory contains the client-side implementation of an RPC (Remote Procedure Call) mechanism used by Talon to communicate with external applications, specifically VS Code. It facilitates sending commands to the application and optionally receiving responses.

The core idea is that Talon and the external application communicate through temporary files. Talon writes a request to a file, then triggers the application to execute the command. The application writes the response to another file that Talon reads. This mechanism avoids the complexities of directly managing sockets, pipes, or other communication channels. The system is designed to be robust against multiple Talon instances trying to communicate with the same application at once.

Here's a breakdown of the most important files:

  • get_communication_dir_path.py: This file provides a function to determine the path to the temporary directory used for communication. It generates a unique path based on the given application name and the user ID to avoid clashes between different user accounts.

  • types.py: Defines data structures for the RPC communication, specifically the Request dataclass, which holds information about the command to be executed (command ID, arguments, whether to wait for completion, etc.). It also defines a custom exception NoFileServerException.

  • write_request.py: This file is responsible for writing the request data to a file in a robust way. It handles cases where a file already exists, checking if the file is stale (older than STALE_TIMEOUT_MS) and deleting it if it is. This ensures that only one Talon process can write to the file at any time, preventing race conditions.

  • read_json_with_timeout.py: This file contains the logic to read the response from the temporary file. It repeatedly attempts to read the JSON response with a timeout. It waits for a trailing newline character in the file to confirm that the write operation is complete. It also uses an exponential backoff to avoid spinning in a tight loop.

  • robust_unlink.py: This file provides a function to robustly delete files. On Windows, if the file is in use, it's moved to a "graveyard" directory rather than deleted to avoid errors.

  • rpc_client.py: The main entry point for the RPC client. It exposes an action (rpc_client_run_command) that sends a command to the server. This function does the following:

    1. Calculates the communication directory path.
    2. Creates the Request object.
    3. Writes the request data to the request file using write_request.py.
    4. Triggers command execution in the external application by calling the trigger_command_execution function.
    5. Reads the response from the response file using read_json_with_timeout.py.
    6. Deletes both the request and response files using robust_unlink.py.
    7. Performs error and sanity checks by checking the UUID and if there are any errors reported by the server.
    8. Returns the command output.

The files in this directory work together to provide a simple file-based RPC mechanism, allowing Talon to send commands to external applications. This system provides a level of reliability and avoids issues with concurrent access from multiple Talon processes.

docs.title: warp
docs.text:
The community/apps/warp directory defines support for the Warp terminal application.

The file warp.py declares the application warp for use in Talon scripts. It specifies that this app definition applies to macOS and to the application with the bundle identifier dev.warp.Warp-Stable.

The file warp.talon tags the warp application with several tags:

  • terminal: Indicates this is a terminal application.
  • user.generic_unix_shell: Indicates this application provides a generic unix shell.
  • user.git: Indicates this application supports git.
  • user.kubectl: Indicates this application supports kubectl.
  • user.tabs: Indicates this application supports tabs.
  • user.file_manager: Indicates this application can be used as a file manager.
  • user.readline: Indicates this application supports readline.

This allows Talon scripts to use these tags to provide functionality specific to the Warp application. For example, if the user says "git status", a script tagged with user.git could run the command git status in the current Warp terminal.

docs.title: windbg
docs.text:
The community/apps/windbg directory contains files that add support for the WinDbg debugger to Talon. It provides a tag, user.windbg, which is used to activate WinDbg-specific commands and actions.

The primary files are:

  • windbg.talon: This file contains the Talon script that defines voice commands for interacting with WinDbg. It includes commands for:
    • Managing symbols (add microsoft symbols, force reload symbols, reload symbols)
    • Displaying information (loaded modules, display pointers)
    • Dereferencing pointers (dereference pointer)
    • Showing the WinDbg version (show version)
    • Switching between different WinDbg views (view command, view watch, view locals, etc.)
    • Dumping function parameters (dump function params)
    • Inserting the name of a windows DLL (lib <user.windows_dlls>)
    • Registering actions for the user.debugger tag
  • windbg.py: This Python file defines the logic and actions that are triggered by the voice commands in windbg.talon. It:
    • Defines the user.windbg tag.
    • Sets up a context (ctx_windbg_enabled) that is only active when the user.windbg tag is enabled.
    • Defines a list of Windows DLLs (self.windows_dlls) that can be inserted with a voice command.
    • Implements actions for:
      • Generic debugger functionality such as stepping, continuing, setting breakpoints, and inspecting memory. These are activated when the user.debugger tag is active.
      • WinDbg-specific commands like clearing, enabling, and disabling breakpoints by ID.

These files work together to provide voice control for WinDbg. The windbg.talon file defines the voice commands, and windbg.py implements the actions that are triggered by those commands. The user.windbg tag controls when the WinDbg-specific commands are active. The user.debugger tag (defined elsewhere) enables generic debugger functionality.

docs.title: windows_command_processor
docs.text:
The community/apps/windows_command_processor directory contains files that provide functionality for interacting with the Windows Command Processor (cmd.exe) and Windows Terminal. This includes navigation, file management, and other terminal-related actions.

The most important files are:

  • command_processor_win.py: This file defines the core actions for interacting with the Windows Command Processor. It sets up a Talon context that activates when the user is in a Command Prompt or Windows Terminal window with a title that includes "Command Prompt". The context is tagged with user.file_manager, user.git, user.kubectl, and terminal, allowing other parts of the Talon configuration to provide additional functionality.
    • It defines actions for file management, such as navigating to parent directories (file_manager_open_parent), opening specific directories (file_manager_open_directory), creating new folders (file_manager_new_folder), and opening files (file_manager_open_file). It uses cd to change directories and mkdir to create new folders. The file_manager_current_path action reads the current path from the window title, removing the "Administrator:" and "Command Prompt:" prefixes.
    • It provides actions to list directories (terminal_list_directories, terminal_list_all_directories), change directories (terminal_change_directory, terminal_change_directory_root), clear the screen (terminal_clear_screen), run the last command (terminal_run_last), and kill all processes (terminal_kill_all).
    • It also includes actions to refresh the window title to display the current path.
  • command_processor_actions_win.py: This file provides some additional actions for the Windows Command Processor. It has a similar context and tags as command_processor_win.py.
    • It duplicates the file_manager_refresh_title and file_manager_open_parent actions defined in command_processor_win.py. It also includes a duplicate delete_line action from the edit context. It's unclear why these actions are duplicated between these files.
    • It also provides the same delete_line action from the edit context.

These two files work together to provide a comprehensive set of actions for interacting with the Windows command processor. command_processor_win.py contains most of the logic while command_processor_actions_win.py provides some supplemental and redundant functionality.

docs.title: windows_explorer
docs.text:
The community/apps/windows_explorer directory provides Talon scripts for interacting with Windows Explorer and file dialogs.

The core functionality is split between two files:

  • windows_explorer.talon: This file defines the Talon commands for this application. It assigns the user.address, user.file_manager, and user.navigation tags when the application is in focus. It also defines some basic commands for navigating to common locations, such as:

    • go <user.letter>$: Opens the drive specified by the letter (e.g., "go c" opens C:).
    • go app data: Opens the %AppData% directory.
    • go program files: Opens the %programfiles% directory.

  • windows_explorer.py: This file contains the Python code that defines the application contexts and implements the actions for interacting with Windows Explorer and file dialogs. It defines two app contexts: windows_explorer and windows_file_browser. The windows_explorer context matches the main Windows Explorer application. The windows_file_browser context matches any application with a title that includes "Save", "Open", "Browse", or "Select", which covers most file dialogs. It defines a UserActions class that implements actions for file management, such as opening directories, selecting files, showing properties, and opening a terminal in the current directory. It also handles remapping of common directories like "Desktop," "Documents," and "Pictures," especially when OneDrive is used.

These two files work together to provide a comprehensive set of commands for interacting with Windows Explorer and file dialogs. The .talon file defines the spoken commands and triggers the actions, while the .py file handles the application context, mapping of directories, and the underlying logic.

docs.title: windows_terminal
docs.text:
The community/apps/windows_terminal directory contains files that enable Talon voice control for the Windows Terminal application.

The core logic is implemented in windows_terminal.py, which defines actions for interacting with the Windows Terminal. It uses Talon's Context system to activate these actions specifically when the Windows Terminal application is in focus.

Here's a breakdown of the key actions defined in windows_terminal.py:

  • Tab Management: tab_close (Ctrl+Shift+W), tab_open (Ctrl+Shift+T), and tab_jump (Ctrl+Alt+[number]) for navigating tabs, and tab_final (Ctrl+Alt+9) to jump to the last tab.
  • Edit Actions: paste (Ctrl+Shift+V), copy (Ctrl+Shift+C), and find (Ctrl+Shift+F) with optional text input.
  • File Manager Actions: The file_manager_* actions are designed to interact with the terminal as a basic file manager. This includes actions for opening and selecting files and directories (file_manager_open_directory, file_manager_select_directory, file_manager_open_file, file_manager_select_file), creating new folders (file_manager_new_folder), and opening volumes (file_manager_open_volume). The file_manager_current_path action attempts to extract the current path from the terminal title, handling variations in prompt text.
  • Split Window Actions: This includes actions to split the window vertically (split_window_vertically, Shift+Alt+Plus) and horizontally (split_window_horizontally, Shift+Alt+Minus). The other split actions (split_window_right, split_window_left, split_window_down, split_window_up, split_flip, split_clear, split_clear_all, split_next, split_last, split_number) are not fully supported by default in Windows Terminal and will trigger a notification. split_window is an alias for split_window_vertically.

The file windows_terminal.talon acts as the Talon script that activates the actions defined in windows_terminal.py. It also defines voice commands to execute those actions. Here's a breakdown:

  • App Matching: app: windows_terminal indicates that this script is active when the Windows Terminal application is in focus.
  • Tag Activation: tag(): terminal makes commands from terminal.talon available. tag(): user.generic_windows_shell activates the implementations of commands/functions in terminal.talon. The tag() commands with user.git, user.anaconda, user.tabs and user.splits make commands for those functionalities available when in a windows terminal.
  • Custom Commands: Specific voice commands are defined such as settings open which maps to key(ctrl-,), focus left which maps to key(ctrl-alt-shift-left), focus right which maps to key(ctrl-alt-shift-right), focus up which maps to key(ctrl-alt-shift-up) and focus down which maps to key(ctrl-alt-shift-down). Additionally, term menu maps to key(ctrl-shift-f1).
  • Find Command: find it is mapped to edit.find(). And a voice command find it <phrase>$: is mapped to edit.find("{phrase}\n") which first escapes out of any previous find and then does a find for the specified text, with an implicit enter.

In summary, these files provide a comprehensive set of voice commands for interacting with the Windows Terminal, including tab and window management, basic file operations and text manipulation.

docs.title: wsl
docs.text: The community/apps/wsl directory contains files that enable Talon to interact with the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL). This allows for actions such as navigating directories, running commands, and using file manager actions within WSL. The core functionality is in wsl.py, with wsl.talon providing the voice commands that trigger the actions.

docs.title: wsl.py
docs.text:
wsl.py is the main Python module that provides the logic for WSL integration. Here's a breakdown of its key components:

  • Context Matching: It defines a Talon context that activates when the active application is windows_terminal and the user.wsl tag is enabled. This context is designed to be more specific than the one defined in apps/win/windows_terminal/windows_terminal.py to ensure that WSL takes precedence.
  • Registry Monitoring: The module monitors the Windows registry for changes in the list of installed WSL distributions, this ensures that Talon is aware of which WSL distros are available.
  • Window Title Parsing: The script parses the active window's title to extract the WSL distro and the current path. It uses a regular expression (wsl_title_regex) to match the expected format of the window title (e.g., WSL:<distro> user@host: <path>). It extracts the distro and the path. If the title does not match it attempts to extract the path by getting the text following the last :.
  • Path Conversion: The module includes functions to convert between Windows paths and WSL paths.
    • get_win_path(wsl_path, distro): Converts a WSL path to its Windows equivalent using wslpath -w.
    • get_usr_path(distro): Gets the absolute path of the WSL user's home directory.
    • get_wsl_path(win_path, distro): Converts a Windows path to its WSL equivalent using wslpath -u.
  • Path Remapping: It remaps certain directories like Desktop, Documents, and Downloads to their appropriate Windows paths, especially when using OneDrive.
  • Error Handling: The code includes retry logic and error handling for wslpath and wsl commands. It logs and notifies the user of errors. Specifically, it checks for a known error where WSL instances terminate and disables path detection until the user manually resets it.
  • Action Class (UserActions): This class implements Talon actions that allow users to interact with WSL. This includes actions like:
    • file_manager_refresh_title(): Skips refreshing the title as this is handled elsewhere.
    • file_manager_open_parent(): Navigates to the parent directory using cd ...
    • file_manager_current_path(): Gets the current WSL path, converts it to a Windows path, and remaps it if necessary.
    • file_manager_open_directory(path): Opens a directory in WSL using cd.
    • file_manager_select_directory(path): Inserts the directory path.
    • file_manager_new_folder(name): Creates a new folder using mkdir.
    • file_manager_open_file(path): Inserts the file path.
    • file_manager_select_file(path): Inserts the file path.
    • file_manager_open_volume(volume): Opens a volume in WSL using cd.
    • terminal_list_directories(): Lists files in the current directory with ls.
    • terminal_list_all_directories(): Lists all files in the current directory with ls -a.
    • terminal_change_directory(path): Changes the current directory in WSL using cd.
    • terminal_change_directory_root(): Navigates to the root directory in WSL using cd /.
    • terminal_clear_screen(): Clears the terminal using ctrl-l.
    • terminal_run_last(): Runs the last command in the terminal.
    • terminal_kill_all(): Kills all processes.
  • Action Class (Actions): This class includes:
    • wsl_reset_path_detection(): Resets the flag that disables WSL path detection after a WSL error.
    • wsl_speak(): A debugging action that causes each WSL distribution to output a string to the log to help verify WSL is running.

docs.title: wsl.talon
docs.text:
wsl.talon defines the voice commands and tags that activate the functionality in wsl.py. * It sets the user.wsl tag, which is required for the context in wsl.py to activate. * It includes the terminal, user.file_manager, user.generic_unix_shell, user.git, and user.kubectl tags so that the user can perform terminal and file manager operations in WSL. * It defines a command go <user.letter> for navigating to a specific mount point (e.g. go c would navigate to /mnt/c). * It defines the command wsl reset path detection which calls the user.wsl_reset_path_detection() action to reset the WSL path detection and the command wsl speak which calls the user.wsl_speak() action.

In summary, this directory enables users to interact with their WSL environment using voice commands, converting between windows and wsl paths as needed. The wsl.py file provides the core functionality, and the wsl.talon file provides the voice commands.

docs.title: core
docs.text: The community/core directory is the heart of the Talon Community project, containing core functionality and utilities. It's organized into subdirectories, each focused on a specific area of functionality. Here's an overview:

docs.title: Subdirectories
docs.text:
• text: Provides text input, formatting, and manipulation tools. It defines lists for currency, phrase enders, prose modifiers, and prose snippets. It also includes core logic for text formatting and a phrase history, as well as Talon bindings to use these features. The files in this directory enable robust text input and manipulation.
  • menu_choose: Contains functionality to choose items from a menu or list using voice commands. It defines actions to simulate pressing arrow keys and enter. The associated Talon file binds these actions to commands such as "choose [number]" and "choose up [number]".
  • snippets: Manages code snippets, providing a way to quickly insert complex code blocks using voice commands. The snippets subdirectory contains the .snippet files themselves, and the rest of the directory contains the Python logic and Talon bindings to use them. The files in this directory allow users to quickly insert complex code structures.
  • vocabulary: Manages custom vocabulary for Talon. It allows users to add new words, remap existing words, and handle pluralization and possessives. This is implemented with a combination of a Talon list, a Python module, and a Talon file for binding the commands. The files in this directory enable robust custom vocabularies.
  • edit: Provides a comprehensive set of tools for editing text. It includes platform-specific implementations, text manipulation utilities, and a command system for combining actions and modifiers. The files in this directory provide flexible and powerful text editing capabilities.
  • file_extension: Defines a list of file extensions that can be used in Talon commands, allowing users to say "dot py" instead of ".py". This is implemented with a python module and a simple Talon file that uses the created list.
  • websites_and_search_engines: Provides tools for opening websites and performing searches using voice commands. This directory defines lists of search engines and websites, and includes logic for opening URLs, performing searches, and binding commands to these actions.
  • edit_text_file: Provides a mechanism for opening frequently edited text files using voice commands. This is implemented using platform-specific actions, a python module to hold the logic, and a Talon file to bind the voice commands.
  • keys: Contains configurations for mapping spoken commands to key presses. It defines lists of keys and provides a way to use them in Talon scripts. It contains platform-specific lists, as well as lists that are common to all operating systems. This is one of the core elements of using Talon.
    • keys/win: Contains Windows and Linux specific key mappings.
    • keys/mac: Contains macOS specific key mappings.
  • windows_and_tabs: Manages windows and tabs across different operating systems. It provides actions for cycling through windows, snapping windows, and managing tabs. This directory contains core logic, OS-specific implementations, and Talon bindings.
  • mouse_grid: Implements a mouse grid system, allowing users to quickly move the mouse cursor to specific locations on the screen using a numbered grid overlay. This directory contains the core grid logic and associated Talon bindings.
  • formatters: Contains code and configuration for formatting text in Talon. It defines text transformations, making them available via voice commands, including a variety of ways to change casing. This is implemented with a Python module, a series of list files to bind formatters, and a Talon file to provide commands.
  • app_switcher: Implements an application switcher, allowing users to switch between or launch applications using voice commands. This directory contains a Python module that handles application searching, a UI to explore the results, and platform-specific mappings to simplify application names.
  • homophones: Provides functionality for selecting homophones using Talon. This is implemented with a Python module, a CSV containing a list of homophones, and Talon files to enable the functionality. This is a powerful feature for ensuring the correct word is inserted when dictating.
  • modes: Defines various modes of operation for Talon, including command, dictation, and sleep modes. It also includes handling for different speech engines, and for setting languages for code. The various modes are implemented using multiple Talon files, and a Python module to hold the core logic.
  • numbers: Defines how numbers are parsed and spoken in Talon, including ordinal numbers. This is implemented using several Python modules, and Talon files.
  • screens: Provides functionality for working with multiple screens in Talon, including showing numbers on the screens and moving windows between them. The logic is implemented in the Python module, and the display of the numbers is triggered with a Talon command.
  • help: Implements a help system for Talon, allowing users to view available commands, contexts, and other information. This is implemented using a python module and multiple Talon files.
  • navigation: Defines and implements navigation actions, such as going back and forward in a browser or other applications. This is implemented with a combination of a Python module to hold the core logic, and a Talon file to bind the voice commands.
  • abbreviate: Contains code for expanding abbreviations in Talon. This is implemented with a Python module and CSV file that defines the abbreviations, and a Talon module that binds the abbreviation to a voice command.
  • snippets/snippets: Contains the .snippet files used by the snippets directory. This subdirectory provides a large number of snippets for generating commonly used code structures in a variety of languages.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• noise.py: Maps noises (like "pop" and "hiss") to actions, enabling contextual behavior based on detected noises.
  • delayed_speech_off.py: Implements a "temporary speech" mode that can be disabled lazily after a phrase finishes.
  • create_spoken_forms.py: This file creates spoken forms for various inputs, which are used for application names, website names, and more.
  • system_command.py: Provides actions to execute system commands.
