  • README.md: Provides a high-level overview of the core directory.
  • dragon_engine.py: Provides actions to control the Dragon speech engine.
  • app_running.py: This module provides a scope for running application names.
  • user_settings.py: Implements functionality for reading and writing user-defined settings from CSV files.
  • system_paths.py: Defines a list of common system paths and creates a Talon list for user customization.
  • tags.py: Defines tags used throughout the repository.
  • application_matches.py: Defines common application matches to be used throughout the repository.
  • deprecations.py: Provides helpers for deprecating voice commands, actions, and captures, notifying users before removing functionality.

docs.title: How They Work Together
docs.text: The subdirectories and files in community/core work together to provide the fundamental building blocks for voice control in Talon. The subdirectories are organized by feature, and each subdirectory contains a combination of Python files for logic, Talon list files for voice commands, and Talon files that bind the voice commands to actions. This architecture promotes modularity, organization, and reusability, making the Talon Community project a powerful and flexible platform for voice control.

docs.title: abbreviate
docs.text:
The community/core/abbreviate directory contains code for expanding abbreviations in Talon.

The key file is abbreviate.py. This file defines a Talon module that provides a list of abbreviations and a capture rule for using them. The core functionality is to allow the user to say "brief" followed by a spoken form of an abbreviation (e.g. "brief app") and have it output the abbreviation (e.g. "app"). This file also defines a list of common abbreviations, and their long-form spoken form.

Here's a breakdown of how it works:

  • Module and Context: The file sets up a Talon module named abbreviate and a context.
  • Abbreviation List: It defines a list of abbreviations with a default dictionary abbreviations. For example, "application" is mapped to "app".
  • CSV Tracking: The @track_csv_list decorator loads the values from abbreviations.csv into the abbreviations_list variable. The abbreviations.csv file allows users to add or change the abbreviations, and have these changes be picked up automatically, if the user has the user_settings.py module enabled.
  • Capture Rule: It defines a capture rule user.abbreviation that allows the user to say "brief" followed by an abbreviation's spoken form.
  • Context List: The ctx.lists["user.abbreviation"] is populated with the short form mapped to the short form as well as the long form mapped to the short form. This allows a user to say "brief app" or just "app" and get "app" out. This also enables the user to expand the list of abbreviations by editing the abbreviations.csv file.

In summary, abbreviate.py provides a flexible mechanism for expanding abbreviations using Talon, using both a static list of abbreviations and a user-customizable CSV file.

docs.title: app_switcher
docs.text:
The community/core/app_switcher directory contains the code for an application switcher. It allows the user to switch between or launch applications using voice commands.

The core functionality is implemented in app_switcher.py. This file defines Talon lists for running and launchable applications. It also defines actions for focusing, launching, and managing applications. The script dynamically generates spoken forms for application names, allowing the user to speak simplified versions of app names to focus or launch them.

The application switcher uses CSV files for platform-specific app name overrides and exclusions. These files are:

  • app_name_overrides.mac.csv
  • app_name_overrides.windows.csv
  • app_name_overrides.linux.csv

Each of these CSV files defines a mapping between a "spoken form" and an application name or executable. It also allows applications to be excluded from the running list by listing their names without a spoken form. This allows users to use simpler names for applications or exclude applications that they don't want to appear in the switcher. For example, the following line in app_name_overrides.mac.csv would allow the user to say "term" to focus on the iTerm2 application.

term, iTerm2

The app_switcher.py script loads these overrides, and uses them to generate spoken forms for both running and launchable applications. The script uses different strategies to find applications based on the operating system, using platform-specific APIs. For example, on Windows it reads the shell:AppsFolder to find both traditional and "modern" apps.

The app_switcher.py script also provides a graphical interface for displaying currently running applications with their associated spoken forms, accessible via the switcher_toggle_running() action. This menu can be helpful for debugging or exploring the current state of the application switcher.

docs.title: edit
docs.text:
The community/core/edit directory provides a comprehensive set of tools for editing text using Talon. It includes platform-specific implementations, text manipulation utilities, and a command system for combining actions and modifiers.

Here's a breakdown of the key components:

docs.title: Platform-Specific Actions
docs.text:
• edit_mac.py: This file defines the edit actions for macOS. It uses keyboard shortcuts like cmd-c for copy, cmd-x for cut, etc. These actions are contextual and only apply when the operating system is macOS, as defined in the ctx.matches string.
  • edit_win.py: Similarly, this file defines the edit actions for Windows, using shortcuts like ctrl-c, ctrl-x. The actions are contextual to Windows.
  • edit_linux.py: This file defines the edit actions for Linux, using shortcuts like ctrl-c, ctrl-x. The actions are contextual to Linux.

These files provide the basic building blocks for editing actions by mapping them to platform-specific keyboard shortcuts.

docs.title: Text Manipulation Utilities
docs.text:
• insert_between.py: This file provides the insert_between action, which inserts a string before and after the current cursor position, and then moves the cursor between the inserted strings.
  • edit.py: This file contains a collection of more complex actions like selected_text, line_insert_down, selection_clone, and select_word. These functions involve more advanced text manipulation. For example, select_word handles edge cases where actions.edit.extend_right() doesn't select a character. It also defines actions to cut and copy words, as well as actions to cut and copy lines.
  • edit_paragraph.py: This file implements actions related to paragraph manipulation such as paragraph_start, paragraph_end, select_paragraph, delete_paragraph, cut_paragraph, copy_paragraph, and paste_paragraph. It also includes helper functions such as is_line_empty, extend_paragraph_start_with_success, and extend_paragraph_end_with_success.
  • delimiter_pair.py: This file provides actions for inserting and wrapping text with delimiter pairs, such as parentheses, brackets, or quotes. It defines a delimiter_pair capture and the actions delimiter_pair_insert and delimiter_pair_wrap_selection

docs.title: Command System
docs.text:
• edit_command_modifiers.py: Defines the edit_modifier capture and a dictionary of callbacks to be performed on a given modifier, such as line, word, or document. The capture is used in the rule defined in edit.talon.
  • edit_command_actions.py: Defines the edit_action capture and a dictionary of callbacks to be performed on a given action, such as select, copy, or delete. It uses dataclasses to represent the possible actions, which can be either simple actions, insert actions, wrap actions, or format actions. The capture is used in the rule defined in edit.talon.
  • edit_command.py: This file combines EditAction and EditModifier to perform complex actions. It contains a dictionary of compound actions for common action/modifier combinations, and then the fallback is to perform the action after applying the modifier.
  • edit_navigation_steps.py: This file defines a NavigationStep data class to represent navigation actions and a capture to parse speech into this data class. It also defines an action to perform a series of navigation steps.
  • edit.talon: This file provides the user-facing commands for editing text. It uses the edit_action and edit_modifier lists to create commands like "select line" or "clear word". It also defines commands for zooming, searching, navigation, indentation, copy, cut, paste, and other useful actions.

docs.title: List Files
docs.text:
• delimiter_pair.talon-list: Defines the delimiter_pair list, mapping spoken forms like "round" to their corresponding delimiter pairs like "( )".
  • edit_command_modifiers.talon-list: Defines the edit_modifier list, mapping spoken forms like "line" to their corresponding modifier types, such as line or fileEnd.
  • edit_command_actions.talon-list: Defines the edit_action list, mapping spoken forms like copy to their corresponding action types, such as copyToClipboard.

docs.title: How They Work Together
docs.text: The platform-specific edit_*.py files provide the basic text editing actions. The text manipulation files enhance these actions with more complex operations. The command system files provide a way to combine actions and modifiers into more powerful commands. The list files provide the vocabulary to be used in the commands. Finally, edit.talon provides a user-friendly interface, by using the captures defined in the python files to enable powerful and flexible text editing capabilities.

docs.title: edit_text_file
docs.text:
The community/core/edit_text_file directory provides a mechanism for opening frequently edited text files using voice commands. This functionality is implemented across multiple operating systems (Windows, macOS, and Linux) with appropriate handling for each.

The core functionality is in edit_text_file.py. This file defines a Talon module that creates a list named edit_text_file, which is intended to hold paths to frequently edited files. It also defines a user action edit_text_file(file: str) that attempts to open the provided file using the system's default text editor. The file also contains platform-specific implementations of the action in classes WinActions, MacActions, and LinuxActions. These classes use the os.startfile command in Windows, the open command on macOS, and xdg-open on Linux, to open a file, with additional error handling. The get_full_path function resolves the provided file path to its absolute path.

The file edit_text_file.talon creates a voice command edit text file that triggers the user.edit_text_file action, passing edit_text_file as an argument, and sleeps for 500ms. Finally, it calls edit.file_end(). The sleep may allow for the text editor to open before the file is advanced to the end, depending on the editor.

The file edit_text_file.talon-list defines the user.edit_text_file list, along with other lists that are used throughout the repository.

docs.title: file_extension
docs.text:
The community/core/file_extension directory defines a list of file extensions that can be used in Talon commands.

The file_extension.py file is the main logic for this feature. It defines a Talon module that creates a list named "file_extension". It uses a dictionary _file_extensions_defaults to define a set of common file extensions and their spoken forms. It also uses a decorator @track_csv_list to load additional file extensions from the file_extensions.csv file and merge them with the defaults. The on_update function updates the Talon list self.file_extension with the merged file extensions.

The file_extension.talon file defines a simple grammar rule that allows the user to insert any file extension from the self.file_extension list in their commands. The rule matches a spoken file extension such as dot pie and outputs the corresponding text representation such as .py.

docs.title: formatters
docs.text:
The community/core/formatters directory contains the code and configuration for formatting text in Talon. It defines a variety of text transformations (e.g. capitalization, case changes, quoting, etc.) and makes them available to Talon users via voice commands.

The core functionality is implemented in formatters.py. It defines several Formatter classes, each implementing format and unformat methods to transform text. The format method applies the transformation, and unformat attempts to reverse it, to return the text to its original form.

Here is a summary of the Formatter classes:

  • Formatter: Abstract base class for all formatters.
  • CustomFormatter: A formatter defined by a custom formatting function.
  • CodeFormatter: A formatter for code, which splits text by non-alphanumeric characters and applies formatting based on the position of the word (first word, subsequent word).
  • TitleFormatter: Formats text in title case, with some words in lower case (e.g. "a", "the").
  • CapitalizeFormatter: Capitalizes only the first word of the text.
  • SentenceFormatter: Capitalizes the first word of a sentence.

The file also includes functions for: * capitalize_first: Capitalizes the first character of the first word. * capitalize: Capitalizes the first character of a string. * lower: Changes all characters to lower case. * unformat_upper: Changes to lower case if the input is in upper case. * remove_code_formatting: Removes formatting conventions like snake, camel, and kebab case and returns the text to a human-readable format. * de_camel: Splits camel case words into separate words. * format_phrase: Formats a phrase according to a list of formatters. This is the primary function that users interact with. * format_text_without_adding_to_history: Formats text according to a list of formatters, without adding to Talon's history. This is a helper function that is used by format_phrase

The file also defines:

  • formatter_list: A list of all available formatters, instantiated as objects of the classes above.
  • formatters_dict: A dictionary mapping formatter IDs to their corresponding formatter object.
  • Talon Module declarations to create the lists that the user can call from their .talon files.
  • Talon Captures to make it possible to format text via voice commands.
  • Talon Actions to expose the formatting functions to the user.

The other files in this directory are Talon list files which define the voice commands the user can use to invoke the formatters:

  • reformatter.talon-list defines a list of reformatters that can be used to unformat, then reformat text.
  • prose_formatter.talon-list defines a list of formatters intended to be used with prose (e.g. "sentence", "title").
  • code_formatter.talon-list defines a list of formatters typically used in code (e.g. "camel", "snake", "constant").
  • word_formatter.talon-list defines a list of formatters that can be used on individual words (e.g. "word", "trot", "proud", "leap").

These files work together to provide a powerful and flexible text formatting system in Talon. The Python code defines the formatting logic, and the Talon list files expose that logic to the user via voice commands.

docs.title: help
docs.text:
The community/core/help directory contains files that implement a help system for Talon. This system allows users to view available commands, contexts, and other information.

The core functionality is provided by help.py, which defines actions for displaying help information and manages the state of the help system. It uses the imgui library to display interactive GUIs for showing different types of help.

help.talon contains the voice commands for accessing the help system. For example, saying "help alphabet" will show the alphabet list and saying "help context" will show the enabled contexts.

help_open.talon provides voice commands for navigating the help GUIs, such as "help next", "help previous", and "help close". It uses the user.help_open tag to limit these commands to when the help system is active.

The help system is comprised of three interactive GUIs:

  • gui_context_help: This GUI, defined within help.py, displays available Talon contexts, and commands. It supports pagination, search, and selection of individual contexts. This is the main help window, and is shown with the commands "help context" and "help active".
  • gui_formatters: This GUI displays a list of formatters and reformatters. This is shown with the commands "help formatters" and "help re formatters".
  • gui_list_help: This GUI displays a list of available Talon lists, such as "user.letter". This is shown with commands like "help alphabet", "help symbols" or "help snip".

The help_scope.py file defines a GUI for showing the current Talon scope, including active modes, tags, and other data. This is activated with the command "help scope". The help_scope_open.talon file contains voice commands specific to the scope GUI, such as "scope hide" to close it.

Here's how the files work together:

  • help.talon defines the initial commands to open the help system, including commands to show lists, context commands, and formatters.
  • When a user speaks a command from help.talon, help.py opens the relevant imgui GUI to display the requested information.
  • The help_open.talon file provides commands for navigating the GUIs.
  • help_scope.py provides a separate GUI for showing the current scope, and help_scope_open.talon provides navigation commands for it.
  • The help.py file uses functions such as refresh_context_command_map, get_sorted_display_keys, and refresh_rule_word_map to generate the data displayed in the GUIs. It also stores state information including current_context_page, selected_context, and search_phrase.

docs.title: homophones
docs.text:
The community/core/homophones directory provides functionality for selecting homophones (words that sound alike but have different spellings and meanings) using Talon. It consists of a Python module, a CSV file containing a list of homophones, and a few Talon files to enable the functionality.

The most important files are:

  • homophones.py: This Python file implements the core logic for the homophone functionality.
    • It reads the homophone pairs from homophones.csv and stores them in a dictionary.
    • It uses the talon library to create a GUI to display the list of available homophones.
    • It defines actions to show, hide, and select a homophone.
    • The raise_homophones function is the main function that determines what homophones to present for selection based on the word.
    • The update_homophones function is used to reload the homophones when the CSV is updated.
    • The gui function defines how the homophone selection dialog is displayed using ImGui.
  • homophones.csv: This CSV file contains the list of homophones, with each line representing a group of homophones separated by commas. For example, there,their,they're is a line in the CSV.
  • homophones.talon: This Talon file defines voice commands for using the homophone functionality.
    • The phones <user.homophones_canonical> command will display a selection dialog of homophones for the word spoken.
    • The phones that command will display a selection dialog of homophones for the currently selected word, or the word under the cursor if there is no selection.
    • The phones force <user.homophones_canonical> command is the same as phones except it will bypass a quick replace if there's only two homophones.
    • The phones force command will display the homophone selection dialog for the current selection with the bypass.
    • The phones hide command will close the dialog.
    • The phones word command will select the word under the cursor and show the selection dialog.
    • The phones [<user.ordinals>] word left command will move the cursor to the left some number of words, select a word, and show the selection dialog.
    • The phones [<user.ordinals>] word right command will move the cursor to the right some number of words, select a word, and show the selection dialog.
  • homophones_open.talon: This Talon file provides commands that are only active while the homophones selection GUI is open.
    • The choose <user.formatters> <number_small> command will select the homophone at the given number in the list, using the given formatter (like capitalization or upper case).

The files work together as follows: The user speaks a command like "phones there" which activates the homophones.talon command phones <user.homophones_canonical>. This uses the homophones_canonical capture defined in homophones.py and passes it to the user.homophones_show action. The homophones.py file then reads the homophones from homophones.csv, raises the ImGui, and displays the homophones for "there", which are "there", "their", and "they're". The user can then select the correct homophone with choose <number_small>, or by using the number directly via the ImGui.

docs.title: keys
docs.text:
The community/core/keys directory contains configuration files for mapping spoken commands to key presses in Talon. It defines lists of keys and provides a way to use them in Talon scripts.

The community/core/keys directory contains two subdirectories, mac and win, that contain platform-specific key mappings. These mappings are used to provide a consistent way to refer to keys across operating systems.

The community/core/keys directory also contains a number of files that define common lists and functionality.

  • number_key.talon-list defines the user.number_key list which maps spoken words to number keys ("zero" to "0", "one" to "1", etc.).

  • keypad_key.talon-list defines the user.keypad_key list which maps spoken words to keypad keys ("key pad zero" to "keypad_0", "key pad plus" to "keypad_plus", etc.).

  • keys.py defines Talon modules and captures for the various key lists. This file defines lists and captures for the other list files including letter, symbol_key, arrow_key, number_key, modifier_key, function_key, special_key, and keypad_key, as well as composite captures for modifiers, arrow_keys, key, and keys. It also defines punctuation_words and symbol_key_words which are used to map spoken forms to punctuation symbols. These lists are used in the keys.talon file to define the speech commands.

  • function_key.talon-list defines the user.function_key list which maps spoken words to function keys ("f one" to "f1", "f twelve" to "f12", etc.).

  • letter.talon-list defines the user.letter list which maps spoken words to letter keys ("air" to "a", "bat" to "b", etc.).

  • keys.talon uses the lists defined in the other files to map spoken commands to key presses. This file contains Talon commands for inserting letters (optionally uppercase), symbols, function keys, special keys, keypad keys, and key combinations with and without modifiers.

  • arrow_key.talon-list defines the user.arrow_key list which maps spoken words to arrow keys ("down" to "down", "left" to "left", etc.).

These files work together to provide a comprehensive system for mapping spoken commands to key presses, making it easier to control a computer using voice. The .talon-list files define the available keys, the keys.py file defines the captures and lists used in the grammar, and the keys.talon file defines the speech commands that use these lists and captures.

docs.title: mac
docs.text:
The community/core/keys/mac directory contains configuration files specific to macOS for mapping spoken commands to key presses. These files primarily define lists used in Talon scripts.

The file special_key.talon-list defines the user.special_key list for macOS. This list maps spoken words to various non-alphanumeric keys, such as end, home, minus, return, page down, page up, escape, space, tab, insert, and delete. Note that wipe and delete are both mapped to backspace, while forward delete is mapped to delete.

The file modifier_key.talon-list defines the user.modifier_key list for macOS. This file maps spoken words to modifier keys, such as alt, control, shift, super (mapped to command), command (also mapped to command), option (mapped to alt), function (mapped to fn), and globe (also mapped to fn). These modifier keys are often used in combination with other keys to perform shortcuts.

docs.title: win
docs.text:
The community/core/keys/win directory contains lists of keys used in Talon. These lists are used to map spoken commands to keyboard keys. The files in this directory are specific to Windows and Linux, although some keys are used in both operating systems.

  • special_key.talon-list defines a list of special keys, such as end, home, enter, and escape. It maps spoken forms to their respective keyboard keys. For example, saying "end" will send the "End" key press. The wipe and delete commands are both mapped to backspace and the command forward delete is mapped to the delete key.
  • modifier_key.talon-list defines a list of modifier keys such as alt, control, and shift. The super key, which is the Windows key, is also included. The command is mapped to the ctrl key on Windows and Linux, and the option key is mapped to the alt key.

docs.title: menu_choose
docs.text:
The community/core/menu_choose directory contains files for choosing items in a menu or list using voice commands.

The core functionality is implemented in menu_choose.py, which defines Talon actions choose and choose_up. The choose action takes a number as input and simulates pressing the down arrow key a given number of times minus one, then presses enter. Similarly, the choose_up action presses the up arrow key a number of times, then enter.

The menu_choose.talon file binds voice commands to these actions. The command "choose [number]" executes the choose action, and "choose up [number]" executes the choose_up action. For example, saying "choose 3" will simulate pressing the down arrow twice then enter.

docs.title: modes
docs.text:
The files in community/core/modes define various modes of operation for Talon, including command, dictation, and sleep modes, and how they interact with different speech engines like Dragon. They also handle language-specific settings for code.

  • language_modes.talon and language_modes.py work together to allow users to set and clear a forced language mode for code.
    • The Talon file defines voice commands to force a language mode, show the currently forced language, and clear the forced language mode.
    • The Python file defines the logic for these commands. It uses a list of code languages and their associated file extensions from code_languages.py to automatically determine the current language from a filename and extension. This file also provides the user.code_set_language_mode() action, which sets the active language, and disables extension matching, and user.code_clear_language_mode(), which clears the active language and re-enables extension matching.
    • The code.language action (via Context) will return the forced language if set. If it is not set, it will attempt to determine the language from the filename and extension by using a mapping of extensions to language ids, or from a mapping of special file names.
  • modes_not_dragon.talon provides commands to enable and disable Talon speech recognition (wake and sleep) when not using the Dragon speech engine. It also includes a command to put Talon to sleep that hides various UI elements.
  • command_and_dictation_mode.talon defines commands to switch between command and dictation modes. It also clears any forced language mode when switching to dictation mode and disables the gdb mode.
  • dictation_mode.talon provides commands for dictation mode, including:
    • Inserting text with user.dictation_insert() to handle auto-capitalization and spacing.
    • Formatting text using commands like "cap", "no cap", and "no space".
    • Navigating and selecting text using commands like "go up", "select left", and "clear right".
    • Formatting selections using user-defined formatters.
    • Correcting speech with commands such as "nope that", "select that", and "spell that."
  • modes_dragon.talon contains commands to enable and disable Talon when using the Dragon speech engine. It also includes a command to put Talon to sleep that hides various UI elements and puts the Dragon engine to sleep.
  • sleep_mode_pop_twice_to_wake.py implements a feature that enables Talon to wake from sleep mode with a double "pop" noise trigger, useful when using the noise module to control the mouse. It uses settings to determine the minimum and maximum times to accept a second pop.
  • dragon_mode.talon defines commands to enable and disable Dragon's command mode, and when to enable and disable talon speech commands, in conjunction with the modes.py file.
  • sleep_mode.talon configures settings for sleep mode, disabling some mouse functionality, and defines the "welcome back" command for waking up Talon.
  • modes.py defines the talon_mode and dragon_mode actions for use by the dragon_mode.talon file to switch between modes. This file also sets up contexts for sleep and awake modes and defines actions to be performed when enabling and disabling speech in those contexts.
  • sleep_mode_wav2letter.talon is a special case for the wav2letter speech engine to prevent Talon from waking up too easily during sleep mode.
  • code_languages.py defines the supported code languages and their associated file extensions and spoken forms. It also includes a mapping for special filenames not associated with an extension to the language, such as CMakeLists.txt which is associated with cmake. This is used by the language_modes.py to determine the active language.

docs.title: mouse_grid
docs.text:
The community/core/mouse_grid directory contains files that implement a mouse grid system for Talon. This system allows users to quickly move the mouse cursor to a specific location on the screen by using a numbered grid overlay. Here's how the key files work together:

  • mouse_grid.py: This Python file contains the core logic of the mouse grid.

    • The MouseSnapNine class manages the grid's state, including its position, size, and display. It handles narrowing the grid based on user input and drawing the grid on the screen using a Talon canvas.
    • The setup method initializes or resets the grid, and can optionally target a specific screen or window.
    • The show method makes the grid visible and registers its draw method.
    • The close method hides the grid and cleans up.
    • The draw method uses the Talon canvas API to draw the grid lines, numbers, and a zoomed view of the target area.
    • The narrow method shrinks the grid to a specific subsection, effectively zooming in on the desired area.
    • The go_back method restores the grid to its previous state.
    • The GridActions class defines the actions available to the Talon user, such as grid_activate, grid_narrow, grid_close, and others.
    • The module settings grid_narrow_expansion and grids_put_one_bottom_left control the grid's behavior and display.

  • mouse_grid_open.talon: This Talon file defines the grammar for when the mouse grid is active.

    • The user.mouse_grid_showing tag activates this grammar.
    • It provides commands for selecting a grid position using number keys (<user.number_key>), closing the grid (grid off, grid close, grid hide), resetting the grid (grid reset), and going back to the previous grid state (grid back).

  • mouse_grid.talon: This file contains a deprecated command for activating the mouse grid. The user.mouse_grid_enabled tag activates this grammar. It is recommended to use the mouse_grid_always.talon grammar instead.

  • mouse_grid_always.talon: This Talon file contains the primary grammar for activating and controlling the mouse grid.

    • It defines voice commands for activating the grid (mouse grid), placing the grid on the active window (grid win), narrowing the grid using a sequence of numbers (grid <user.number_key>+), and selecting a screen (grid screen [<number>]).

In summary, the mouse_grid.py file provides the core functionality of the mouse grid, while the Talon files define the voice commands and grammars for using the grid. The mouse_grid_always.talon file contains the primary user-facing voice commands. The system allows users to quickly narrow down a target area using number keys and then close or reset the grid as needed.

docs.title: navigation
docs.text:
The community/core/navigation directory contains files that define and implement navigation actions, such as going back and forward in a browser or other applications.

The key files are:

  • navigation.py: This Python file defines the core logic for navigation using Talon. It defines a navigation tag and implements go_back and go_forward actions. It uses contexts to handle different navigation methods based on the application (browser) or operating system (mac).

    • The BrowserActions class uses actions.browser.go_back() and actions.browser.go_forward() to handle navigation in browsers.
    • The MacActions class uses actions.key("cmd-[") and actions.key("cmd-]") to handle navigation on macOS.
    • The Actions class defines fallback actions for other cases with actions.key("alt-left") and actions.key("alt-right").

  • navigation.talon: This Talon file creates a user.navigation tag and links voice commands "go back" and "go forward" to the corresponding user.go_back() and user.go_forward() actions defined in navigation.py. This enables voice control for navigation.

These files work together to provide a flexible and cross-platform navigation system. The navigation.py file defines the core logic and provides contextual overrides, while navigation.talon makes the navigation available to voice commands.

docs.title: numbers
docs.text:
The community/core/numbers directory contains files that define how numbers are parsed and spoken in Talon.

The core logic for number parsing is in numbers.py. It defines a series of maps (digits_map, teens_map, tens_map, scales_map, and numbers_map) that translate spoken number words into their numeric values. The parse_number function uses these maps to convert a list of spoken number words into a string of digits. This is accomplished via a few helper functions: scan_small_numbers which converts number words less than 100 to their integer representation, parse_scale which handles numbers that use scales like "hundred", "thousand", "million", etc., and split_list which splits a list by a given value. The file also defines several Talon captures: - digit_string captures a sequence of digit words (e.g. "one two three"). - digits converts a digit_string into an integer. - number_string captures a more complex number phrase (e.g. "one hundred and twenty three"). - number converts a number_string into an integer. - number_signed_string captures a number phrase that may be negative, returning it as a string. - number_signed converts a number_signed_string to an integer. - number_prose_with_dot, number_prose_with_comma, and number_prose_with_colon capture number strings that use a dot, comma, or colon to separate number groups, respectively. - number_prose_unprefixed captures a number prose string, whether it's signed, dotted, commad, or colon separated. - number_prose_prefixed captures a number prose string that's prefixed with the word "numb" or "numeral". - number_small captures a small number from 0 to 99, based on a user list with various ways to speak them. - number_signed_small captures a small number that may be negative.

The file numbers_unprefixed.talon (numbers_unprefixed.talon) defines a tag, user.unprefixed_numbers, which allows using number_prose_unprefixed without a prefix. This tag and capture are used by other parts of the Talon configuration to enable natural spoken numbers. This means you can just say "one hundred twenty three" instead of "numb one hundred twenty three".

The file ordinals.py defines how ordinal numbers (first, second, third, etc.) are parsed and spoken. Like numbers.py, it contains several maps for converting spoken words into numeric values. ordinal_numbers maps all ordinals from 1 to 99 and ordinal_small maps ordinals from 1 to 20. It defines the following Talon captures: - ordinals captures any of the ordinal numbers up to 99 and returns the integer value. - ordinals_small captures any of the ordinal numbers up to 20 and returns the integer value.

The file numbers_prefixed.talon defines a capture for number_prose_prefixed. This makes it possible to use prefixed numbers by saying "numb one twenty three".

docs.title: screens
docs.text:
The community/core/screens directory provides functionality for working with multiple screens in Talon.

The primary files are:

  • screens.talon: This file contains Talon commands to show the screen numbers. The command user.screens_show_numbering() triggers the display of screen numbers.

  • screens.py: This Python file implements the logic for screen numbering and navigation.

    It defines several actions: * screens_show_numbering(): Displays a number on each screen. It uses ui.screens() to get all screens, then calls show_screen_number to render the number. A cron.after timer is used to automatically close the canvas after 3 seconds. * screens_get_by_number(screen_number: int) -> ui.Screen: Retrieves a screen object by its number. It ensures the screen number is valid, raising an exception if not. * screens_get_previous(screen: ui.Screen) -> ui.Screen: Retrieves the screen before the provided screen in the screen list. * screens_get_next(screen: ui.Screen) -> ui.Screen: Retrieves the screen after the provided screen in the screen list.

    The file also defines helper functions: * get_screen_by_offset(screen: ui.Screen, offset: int) -> ui.Screen: Retrieves a screen by an offset from a given screen, wrapping around when necessary. * show_screen_number(screen: ui.Screen, number: int): Creates a Canvas on a given screen to draw a number.

docs.title: snippets
docs.text:
The community/core/snippets directory contains the code that manages code snippets in Talon. The snippets themselves are located in the snippets subdirectory, while the rest of the files in this directory implement the functionality to read and use the snippets.

The snippets subdirectory contains .snippet files which define the snippets that can be inserted. See the snippets/README.md for details on the format of these files. The snippets provide a way to quickly insert code blocks by voice command. They support variables with insertion formatters, wrapper snippets for use with Cursorless, and multiple languages. Some examples of what can be created with snippets includes class declarations, comments, function and method definitions, conditional statements, try/catch blocks, loops, switch statements, lambda expressions, print statements, format strings, new instances of classes, and exception throwing statements.

The snippets are used in conjunction with the snippets.talon file, which defines the voice commands that trigger the snippets, and the code.talon file, which defines the voice commands that trigger the code actions.

Here's how the files in the community/core/snippets directory work together:

  • snippets_insert_raw_text.py provides the insert_snippet_raw_text function, which inserts the raw text of a snippet into the editor, handling tab stops and variables. It also contains the helper functions to parse snippets, move the cursor, and determine the correct tab order.
  • snippets.py is the main file that loads and manages snippets. It defines Talon lists for snippets, reads .snippet files, and creates contexts for each defined language. This file also defines the actions get_snippet, get_insertion_snippet, and get_wrapper_snippet to access a snippet by name or type. The update_snippets function reads the snippets and creates the lists that are used by the snippets.talon file.
  • snippet_types.py defines the data structures for snippets, including SnippetVariable, Snippet, InsertionSnippet, and WrapperSnippet.
  • snippets_parser.py is responsible for parsing the .snippet files. It defines functions to create snippet objects from the files, and to validate the snippet definitions to catch errors early.
  • README.md documents the snippet file format, features, and examples.
  • snippets.talon defines the voice commands that trigger the snippets. It uses the user.snippet and user.snippet_with_phrase lists defined in snippets.py.
  • snippets_insert.py implements the insert_snippet, insert_snippet_by_name, and insert_snippet_by_name_with_phrase actions. The insert_snippet action uses the insert_snippet_raw_text from snippets_insert_raw_text.py. The other two actions retrieve snippets by name and then insert them, optionally substituting variables.

In summary, this directory provides a robust system for managing code snippets, allowing users to quickly insert complex code structures using voice commands. It includes a custom file format, a parser, and integration with the Talon voice control system.

docs.title: snippets
docs.text:
The community/core/snippets/snippets directory contains a collection of code snippets used by the Talon voice control system. These snippets are designed to generate commonly used code structures in various programming languages. Each snippet is defined in its own file and includes a name, a phrase that triggers the snippet, and the code to be inserted. Some snippets have additional properties such as insertionScope and formatters for the inserted text.

Here's an overview of some of the most interesting snippets and how they work together:

  • The classDeclaration.snippet provides a way to quickly create class declarations in JavaScript, Java, and Python. It includes formatters to ensure the class name is in PUBLIC_CAMEL_CASE.
  • The commentLine.snippet and commentBlock.snippet files provide a way to create single-line and multi-line comments in several languages, including C, JavaScript, Java, Python, HTML, and others. These snippets help to keep code well-documented.
  • The functionDeclaration.snippet and methodDeclaration.snippet allow users to generate function and method definitions in JavaScript, Java, and Python. The function declaration is used to create free functions, while the method declaration is used to create class methods.
  • The ifStatement.snippet, elseIfStatement.snippet, and elseStatement.snippet files are used to construct conditional statements with support for if, else if, and else clauses in JavaScript, Java, Python, and Lua. These snippets work together to build full conditional logic blocks.
  • The tryStatement.snippet, catchStatement.snippet, and tryCatchStatement.snippet files provide a way to create try, catch, and try-catch blocks in JavaScript, Java, and Python. These snippets handle exception handling in different languages.
  • The forLoopStatement.snippet, forEachStatement.snippet, forInLoopStatement.snippet, and doWhileLoopStatement.snippet snippets generate different kinds of loops with slightly different syntax across JavaScript, Java, and Python. The forLoopStatement.snippet generates a traditional for loop, the forEachStatement.snippet iterates over a collection, the forInLoopStatement.snippet iterates over the properties of an object, and doWhileLoopStatement.snippet generates a do-while loop.
  • The switchStatement.snippet and caseStatement.snippet files are used to create switch statements with case clauses for JavaScript, Java, and Python. The defaultStatement.snippet creates the default case for switch statements.
  • The lambdaExpression.snippet provides a way to create lambda expressions or anonymous functions in JavaScript, Java, and Python.
  • The printStatement.snippet file is used to create print statements for JavaScript, Java, and Python.
  • The formatString.snippet file is used to generate format strings for JavaScript, Java, and Python.
  • The newInstance.snippet file is used to create new instances of a class in JavaScript, Java, and Python.
  • The throwException.snippet file is used to create statements for throwing exceptions in Javascript, Python, and Java.
  • The functionCall.snippet file generates function calls for C, JavaScript, Java, Python, and Talon.
  • The html.snippet file contains snippets for creating HTML elements, attributes, tables and lists. The element snippet creates a pair of matching tags, the attribute snippet creates an attribute with a name and value, the table creates a basic HTML table, and the unorderedList creates a basic unordered list.
  • The markdown.snippet file contains snippets for creating markdown links.

These snippets provide a foundation for generating commonly used code blocks in a variety of languages with simple voice commands. They are combined with other features of Talon to provide a powerful way to create code using voice.

There are also some language-specific snippets, such as: * The talon.snippet provides snippets specific to the Talon language for defining voice commands. * The python.snippet provides snippets specific to Python and the Talon API such as module declarations, context matchers, context lists, and action classes. * The lua.snippet provides snippets specific to Lua for creating for-in loops. * The javascript.snippet provides snippets specific to JavaScript for creating imports, anonymous functions, and react hooks. * The typescript.snippet provides snippets specific to TypeScript for creating interfaces.

These snippets are all used in conjunction with the code.talon file, which defines the voice commands that trigger the snippets.

docs.title: text
docs.text:
The community/core/text directory contains files related to text input, formatting, and manipulation in Talon. It provides a foundation for more natural and efficient dictation and text editing.

The files in this directory can be grouped as follows:

  • Lists:
    • currency.talon-list: Defines a list of currency names and symbols (e.g., "dollar", "$", "euro", "€"). These are used by the prose_currency capture in text_and_dictation.py.
    • phrase_ender.talon-list: Defines a list of words that can be used to end a phrase. Currently, this list only contains "over". This list is used in text.talon as an optional ending to text phrases.
    • prose_modifiers.talon-list: Defines a list of modifiers that can be used within prose, such as "cap", "no cap", and "no space". These map to methods of the DictationFormat class in text_and_dictation.py and are used in the prose capture to control capitalization and spacing.
    • prose_snippets.talon-list: Defines a list of snippets that can be inserted into prose, such as "spacebar", "new line", and "open quote." These are used in the prose capture in text_and_dictation.py.
  • Core Logic:
    • phrase_history.py: Implements a phrase history, allowing users to recall, retype, or copy recently spoken phrases using commands such as "recent repeat <number>" and "recent copy <number>". It also provides actions for clearing, selecting, and moving before the last phrase. The phrase_history variable stores recent phrases, and the Actions class defines methods for manipulating this history. An ImGui interface is also defined for displaying the history.
    • text_and_dictation.py: Contains core logic for text formatting, auto-spacing, and auto-capitalization during dictation. It defines the DictationFormat class, which handles formatting based on the context and modifier commands, and implements the text, prose, and raw_prose captures that are the basis for formatted text input. This file also defines several useful captures such as prose_percent, prose_currency, and prose_time that are used by prose.
  • Talon Bindings:
    • text.talon: Binds spoken commands to actions defined in phrase_history.py and text_and_dictation.py. It provides commands for inserting text, applying formatters, accessing the phrase history, and more. It uses the captures in text_and_dictation.py, such as text and prose, and supports the phrase_ender list.

In summary, this directory provides the necessary components for robust text input and manipulation in Talon. The lists define vocabulary, phrase_history.py provides the ability to recall previous phrases, text_and_dictation.py handles complex text formatting and spacing logic, and text.talon ties it all together with user-friendly commands. These files work together to enable natural language interaction with the computer.

docs.title: vocabulary
docs.text:
The community/core/vocabulary directory contains files that manage custom vocabulary for Talon. This includes adding new words, remapping existing words, and handling pluralization and possessives.

The most important files in this directory are:

  • vocabulary.talon-list: This file contains a list of custom vocabulary words and phrases, which can be used in dictation and commands. Each line is either a single word to be added to the vocabulary or a spoken form: written form pairing, where the spoken form is what you say and the written form is what is written by Talon. For example: N map: nmap under documented: under-documented

    The custom vocabulary is loaded by the vocabulary.py file, and it is used for both dictation and command modes.

  • edit_vocabulary.talon: This file contains Talon commands for adding words to the vocabulary. It defines commands like "copy to vocab", "copy name to vocab", and "copy noun to vocab". These commands allow users to quickly add selected text to the vocabulary, with the option to automatically generate plural and possessive forms. It also has similar commands for adding words to the words_to_replace.csv file, which is used for remapping spoken words to written words.

  • vocabulary.py: This is the core Python file for managing custom vocabulary. It defines:

    • A vocabulary list which can be referenced in other Talon files.
    • A PhraseReplacer class which is used to replace spoken phrases with written phrases. This is used to implement the dictate.replace_words action, which replaces words in dictation. This replacer supports multi-word phrases, unlike the default dictate.word_map in Talon.
    • The words_to_replace.csv file, which can be edited to remap words.
    • The add_selection_to_vocabulary action that allows adding selected text to vocabulary.talon-list.
    • The add_selection_to_words_to_replace action that allows adding selected text to words_to_replace.csv.
    • A helper function to generate possessive and plural forms when adding to the vocabulary.

These files work together to provide a robust system for managing custom vocabulary in Talon. The vocabulary.talon-list file defines the actual vocabulary, the edit_vocabulary.talon file provides commands for adding to the vocabulary, and the vocabulary.py file provides the logic for loading the vocabulary, remapping words, and providing the actions for editing the vocabulary.

docs.title: websites_and_search_engines
docs.text:
This directory provides a set of tools for opening websites and performing searches using voice commands.

The file search_engine.talon-list defines a list of search engines. Each entry in the list provides the base URL for a search engine, where the %s is a placeholder for the user's query. For example, the google entry is https://www.google.com/search?q=%s.

The file websites_and_search_engines.talon contains the Talon commands to use these lists. The command open {user.website} uses user.open_url() to open a URL from the user.website list. The commands open that and open paste open the selected text or clipboard content as a URL. The commands {user.search_engine} hunt <user.text>$, {user.search_engine} (that | this), and {user.search_engine} paste perform searches using the specified search engine from the user.search_engine list and the provided text, selected text, or clipboard content.

The file websites_and_search_engines.py implements the actions used by the Talon commands. The open_url action uses the webbrowser module to open a URL. The search_with_search_engine action replaces the %s placeholder in a search engine template with the user's search query (URL-encoded via quote_plus) and then opens the resulting URL in a web browser. This file also defines the website and search_engine lists and defines a browser context. The address capture captures the website for the browser context.

The file website.talon-list provides a list of website URLs, which are used by the open command in websites_and_search_engines.talon.

docs.title: windows_and_tabs
docs.text:
The community/core/windows_and_tabs directory contains files for managing windows and tabs across different operating systems.

The core functionality is in windows_and_tabs.py, which defines actions for cycling through windows of the current application. It also contains a helper function is_window_valid to filter out invalid windows, such as those with no title or tiny windows that are not actual windows. The cycle_windows function is used by window_previous and window_next.

The directory also contains OS-specific files that define default application actions for Windows, Linux, and macOS. These files implement actions like closing, opening, and navigating tabs and windows using OS-specific keyboard shortcuts. * windows_and_tabs_win.py defines actions for Windows. * windows_and_tabs_linux.py defines actions for Linux. * windows_and_tabs_mac.py defines actions for macOS.

The directory also includes files for window snapping and tab management. * window_snap.py defines actions for snapping windows to predefined positions on the screen or moving them between screens. It includes functions for calculating and setting window positions, as well as a setting for how windows should be sized and positioned when moving between screens with different resolutions or orientations. It also defines lists for window_snap_positions and window_split_positions. * tabs.talon provides voice commands for tab management such as opening, closing, and navigating between tabs. * tabs.py defines action classes for tab jumping, closing, and duplicating, which are used by tabs.talon.

Finally, window_management.talon defines voice commands for window management, such as opening, closing, and focusing windows, snapping windows to screen positions and moving windows between screens. It makes heavy use of user.running_applications and user.launch_applications which are defined elsewhere.

In summary, these files work together to provide a comprehensive system for managing windows and tabs across different operating systems. They provide core functions and OS-specific actions for common window and tab operations as well as more complex features like window snapping and application switching.

docs.title: lang
docs.text:
The community/lang directory contains subdirectories that provide language-specific support for Talon. Each subdirectory contains a combination of .talon files, which define the voice commands and context, and .py files, which contain the supporting Python code that implements actions and manages data. These files enable users to write code in different programming languages using voice control. Here's an overview of the most important subdirectories and files:

  • talon: This directory provides support for the Talon language itself. It includes:

    • talon.talon: Sets the code language to talon and enables tags for math operators, assignment, comments, and common functions.
    • talon_common.talon: Defines commands common to both Talon and Python files with the user.talon_python tag active.
    • talon-list.talon: Defines lists for use in Talon files and sets the code language to talonlist.
    • talon_context.talon: Provides commands for inserting context requirements into Talon files.
    • talon.py: Sets up the logic for Talon language support, defines actions for inserting operators, comments, and function calls, and defines lists for actions, lists, captures, apps, tags, modes, settings, and scopes.
    • talon_code_common_function.talon-list: Defines a list of common functions used in code.
    • talon_python_activator.talon: Activates the user.talon_python tag, enabling Talon-specific Python commands. These files provide a comprehensive set of tools for developing Talon code, using context-aware commands, custom lists, and Python actions to streamline the process of writing and editing Talon files.

  • vimscript: This directory enables voice control for the Vimscript language.

    • vimscript.py: Defines the core logic for vimscript support including contexts and lists of common vimscript functions and scope identifiers, as well as actions for inserting operators, comments, and keywords.
    • vimscript.talon: Defines voice commands and tags for vimscript, including commands for inserting common vimscript constructs.

  • markdown: This directory provides support for editing Markdown files.

    • markdown.py: Defines a context for Markdown and a list of languages for code blocks.
    • markdown.talon: Provides commands for inserting common Markdown elements.

  • kotlin: This directory enables voice control for the Kotlin language.

    • kotlin.py: Defines the context, actions for inserting common Kotlin syntax elements, such as keywords, operators, control flow statements, and class and function declarations.
    • kotlin.talon: Configures the Kotlin language support, including settings for formatting and tags for imperative and object-oriented code.

  • elixir: This directory contains support for the Elixir programming language.

    • elixir.talon: Defines the Elixir language tag, formatting settings, and various Elixir-specific states and keywords.
    • elixir.py: Defines the core logic for Elixir, including contexts, lists of keywords, and actions for common code constructs.

  • html: This directory defines language support for HTML, JavaScript React, and TypeScript React.

    • html.talon: Declares the language associations for the supported languages.

  • ruby: This directory provides support for the Ruby programming language.

    • ruby.py: Defines actions for common Ruby code constructs, such as operators, keywords, and control flow statements.
    • ruby.talon: Defines the voice commands for Ruby, including settings and tags for imperative and object-oriented code, as well as some additional commands.

  • python: This directory contains support for the Python programming language.

    • python.talon: Defines the Talon grammar for Python, including tags, formatting settings, and rules for common syntax constructs.
    • python.py: Implements the logic behind the grammar defined in python.talon, defining lists of common elements, actions for inserting and formatting code, and enabling autocompletion.

  • sql: This directory enables voice control for SQL.

    • sql.py: Defines the Talon context, common functions, and action implementations.
    • sql.talon: Defines voice commands for SQL, including tags for operators, comments, and common functions, as well as commands for common SQL keywords and patterns.

  • stata: This directory adds support for the Stata language.

    • stata.talon: Defines the Talon language mode and sets up the necessary tags for Stata-specific functionality and voice commands.
    • stata.py: Contains the Python code that implements actions and lists used in stata.talon.

  • r: This directory provides support for the R programming language.

    • r.py: Defines a context for R and actions for inserting common R code constructs such as operators, control flow, and comments.
    • r.talon: Defines voice commands and tags for R, including commands for inserting R-specific code elements.

  • batch: This directory adds support for the batch scripting language.

    • batch.talon: Defines the basic grammar for batch scripting and commands for inserting commonly used batch keywords.
    • batch.py: Defines the code_comment_line_prefix() action.

  • java: This directory provides support for the Java programming language.

    • java.py: Defines contexts, lists of Java keywords, and actions for inserting various code constructs, and formatted function insertions.
    • java.talon: Defines the voice commands that trigger the actions defined in java.py, and tags to enable code-related features, including modifiers, types, and operators.

  • csharp: This directory enables voice control for C#.

    • csharp.talon: Defines the context for C# code, tags for general coding commands, and settings for formatting functions and variables.
    • csharp.py: Defines actions for inserting C# code snippets, such as operators, keywords, and formatted functions.

  • scala: This directory adds support for the Scala programming language.

    • scala.py: Defines core functionality for Scala including contexts, lists of types, modifiers and keywords, and actions for inserting operators, control flow, comments, and function declarations.
    • scala.talon: Defines voice commands that trigger the actions defined in scala.py and tags that define code style.

  • proto: This directory enables voice coding in protobuf.

    • proto.py: Defines a Talon context and a list of protobuf data types.
    • proto.talon: Defines commands for protobuf keywords and syntax and leverages the user.code_type list defined in proto.py.

  • javascript: This directory provides support for JavaScript, TypeScript, and React-based languages.

    • javascript.py: Defines contexts, lists, and actions specific to JavaScript and related languages.
    • javascript.talon: Sets up Talon commands and tags for JavaScript support and enables various voice commands.

  • lua: This directory provides support for the Lua programming language.

    • lua.py: Defines a context for Lua, the lua_version setting, lists of functions and libraries, and actions for inserting code constructs such as operators, comments, and control flow statements.
    • lua.talon: Defines Talon commands for Lua and various tags, settings, and voice commands for Lua syntax and related concepts.
    • stylua.talon: Defines commands for interacting with the stylua Lua code formatter.

  • c: This directory provides support for the C programming language.

    • c.talon: Defines Talon commands for working with C code, such as preprocessor directives and keywords.
    • c.py: Contains Python code that supports the commands in c.talon, including context activation, lists of C keywords, types, and library functions, and action implementations for inserting operators, control flow, and function calls.

  • tags: This directory contains .talon and .py files that define tags, actions, and rules for common programming constructs, serving as building blocks for specific language grammars.

    • comment_block.talon & comment_block.py: Implements block comment functionality.
    • comment_line.talon & comment_line.py: Implements line comment functionality.
    • data_bool.talon & data_bool.py: Implements commands for inserting boolean values.
    • functions_common.talon, functions_common.py, & functions_common_gui_active.talon: Implements mechanisms for inserting common functions, potentially with a GUI for selection.
    • operators_math.py & operators_math.talon: Implements commands for inserting math operators.
    • libraries.talon & libraries.py: Implements commands for library imports.
    • operators_pointer.talon & operators_pointer.py: Implements commands for inserting pointer operators.
    • imperative.py & imperative.talon: Implements commands for inserting common imperative programming constructs.
    • functions.talon & functions.py: Implements commands for inserting and modifying function declarations.
    • comment_documentation.py & comment_documentation.talon: Implements commands for inserting documentation comments.
    • operators_assignment.py & operators_assignment.talon: Implements commands for inserting assignment operators.
    • operators_lambda.talon & operators_lambda.py: Implements commands for inserting lambda functions.
    • operators_array.py & operators_array.talon: Implements commands for inserting array subscript operators.
    • data_null.py & data_null.talon: Implements commands for inserting null values.
    • object_oriented.py & object_oriented.talon: Implements commands related to object oriented programming such as object accessors and class declarations.
    • operators_bitwise.py & operators_bitwise.talon: Implements commands for inserting bitwise operators.
    • keywords.py & keywords.talon: Implements commands for inserting keywords.

  • css: This directory contains files that add support for CSS and SCSS.

    • css.py: Defines lists of CSS keywords, units, and functions, and some basic code actions.
    • css.talon: Defines voice commands for CSS and SCSS, using tags, settings, and commands for common CSS constructs.

  • php: This directory provides support for the PHP programming language.

    • php.py: Defines a Talon context and actions for common PHP constructs, such as keywords, operators, control flow statements, and function definitions.
    • php.talon: Sets up the context, provides formatting settings, and adds shorthands for some commands.

  • terraform: This directory contains files for supporting the Terraform language.

    • terraform.py: Defines the core logic for Terraform, including contexts, lists of types and properties, and actions for inserting module blocks, resources, and data sources.
    • terraform.talon: Defines voice commands and tags for Terraform.

  • typescript: This directory contains files to support TypeScript.

    • typescript.talon: Defines voice commands for inserting common TypeScript syntax, such as union and intersection types.
    • typescript.py: Defines the context and actions for TypeScript, including lists for user defined types and insertion of function declarations with access modifiers and type annotations.

  • rust: This directory contains files that provide support for the Rust programming language.

    • rust.py: Defines a Talon module with actions, lists, and captures for working with Rust code, including code manipulation actions and support for operators and keywords.
    • rust.talon: Defines voice commands for Rust, using the definitions from rust.py and adds commands for rust-specific constructs.

In summary, the community/lang directory is a well-structured collection of language-specific support files for Talon. Each subdirectory contains a combination of .talon files, which define the voice commands and context, and .py files, which implement actions and data. The subdirectories provide a wide range of features that streamline the process of voice coding in many different programming languages. The tags directory is an exception, providing common code constructs that are reused by the language specific files.

docs.title: batch
docs.text:
The community/lang/batch directory contains files to add support for the batch scripting language in Talon.

The file batch.talon defines the basic grammar for batch scripting. It sets the code.language tag to batch, which allows Talon to activate this grammar when coding in batch. It defines a tag user.code_comment_line which can be used in other grammar files to provide generic comment functionality. It provides commands to insert commonly used batch keywords. For example, saying "echo" will insert echo, saying "go to" will insert goto, and saying "arg 1" will insert %1. It also provides commands to insert exit commands, both soft exit which exits the current batch file or subroutine, and hard exit which exits the command shell.

The file batch.py defines Python code that will be used to define actions. It sets the context to be active only when code.language is set to batch. It defines the code_comment_line_prefix() action, which inserts REM. This action is used when the user says the "comment line" command.

docs.title: c
docs.text:
The community/lang/c directory contains files that provide support for the C programming language in Talon.

The primary file is c.talon. This file defines the Talon commands for working with C code. It includes:

  • Tags that activate when the user is working in C code. For example, user.code_imperative and user.code_block_c_like.
  • Settings for how variables and function names are formatted. All are set to SNAKE_CASE by default.
  • State commands for inserting common C preprocessor directives like #include, #define, #ifdef, #if, #error, and #pragma.
  • A command push braces that inserts a pair of braces and puts the cursor inside the braces.
  • Commands that insert variable declarations, casting syntax, and keywords. These commands use the lists defined in c.py to insert correct syntax.
  • A command toggle includes that is mapped to user.code_toggle_libraries() action, which will insert a standard library include statement.

The file c.py contains the Python code that supports the commands in c.talon. It includes:

  • Context activation when the code language is C.
  • Lists of C keywords, types, and common standard library functions, and common standard library includes. These are referenced in c.talon to build up complex commands.
  • Captures for combining the lists together, for example, c_cast for casting syntax, which combines the c_signed, c_types, and c_pointers lists.
  • Action implementations for common C code constructs like operators, control flow statements, and functions. These are referenced by commands in c.talon.

Together, c.talon and c.py provide a comprehensive set of tools for writing C code with Talon, including inserting common C syntax, common C library includes, and common C code structures.

docs.title: csharp
docs.text:
The community/lang/csharp directory contains files to enable Talon voice control for the C# programming language.

The file csharp.talon is a Talon script that defines the context for C# code. It uses code.language: csharp to specify that the rules within the file should only be active when the active language is C#. It also specifies a number of tags that are used by other talon scripts to provide generic coding commands. For example, the user.code_imperative tag enables commands for imperative programming, and the user.code_comment_line tag enables commands for inserting line comments. Finally, it specifies settings for formatting functions and variables in C# using the CamelCase naming convention for private, protected, and public members.

The file csharp.py defines a Python class UserActions that contains a set of actions which are used to insert C# code snippets. This includes common operators, state keywords, and actions for inserting functions with a variety of access modifiers. For example, code_operator_indirection() inserts a * character, and code_state_if() inserts if(). The code_insert_function() action is used as the basis for the various function insertion actions, and it uses the settings from csharp.talon to format the text according to naming conventions. This file also defines a list of common functions to be used by other talon scripts.

docs.title: css
docs.text:
The community/lang/css directory contains files that add support for CSS and SCSS to Talon.

The core logic is in css.py, which defines lists of CSS keywords, units, and functions. It also defines some basic code actions.

  • css_unit: A list of CSS units like "em", "px", "deg", and "s".
  • css_at_rule: A list of CSS at-rules like "media", "import", and "keyframes".
  • css_global_value: A list of CSS global values like "initial", "inherit", and "unset".
  • code_common_function: A list of CSS functions like "rgb", "calc", "linear-gradient", and "translate".

These lists are used in css.talon, which defines the voice commands for CSS and SCSS. This file provides:

  • Tags: user.code_block_c_like, user.code_comment_block_c_like, user.code_functions_common, user.code_libraries, and user.code_operators_math. These tags enable common functionality for C-like languages, and provide an interface for libraries and math.
  • settings(): user.code_public_variable_formatter = "DASH_SEPARATED": This setting ensures that public variable names will be formatted with dashes.
  • Commands for inserting CSS:
    • block: Inserts a code block.
    • attribute [<user.text>]: Inserts a CSS attribute within brackets.
    • prop <user.text>: Inserts a CSS property with a colon and semicolon.
    • rule <user.text>: Inserts a CSS rule with a colon.
    • value <user.number_string> [{user.css_unit}]: Inserts a number with an optional unit.
    • value <user.number_string> point <digit_string> [{user.css_unit}]: Inserts a floating point number with an optional unit.
    • (value | state) {user.css_global_value}: Inserts a global value like initial.
    • value <user.text>: Inserts a value with dash-separated formatting.
    • variable <user.text>: Inserts a CSS variable.
    • op var: Inserts var(--) for a CSS variable.
    • at {user.css_at_rule}: Inserts an at-rule.
    • unit {user.css_unit}: Inserts a unit.
    • [value] current color: Inserts the currentColor value.
    • op important: Inserts !important.

Together, these files enable the user to speak CSS and SCSS code using voice commands. The Python file defines the underlying data and some logic, while the Talon file defines the voice commands using that data.

docs.title: elixir
docs.text:
This directory contains support for the Elixir programming language.

The file elixir.talon is the primary Talon file for Elixir. It defines the language tag, enabling Elixir-specific features. It also sets code formatting settings (like SNAKE_CASE for functions and variables) and defines various Elixir-specific states or keywords, such as:

  • def, defp, if, else, case, cond, try, rescue, after, and end. These are common keywords in Elixir for defining functions and control flow.
  • op pipe which inserts the pipe operator |>.
  • raise and rescue keywords that include an exception type.

The elixir.py file defines the core logic for interacting with Elixir code via Talon. It defines a context that is active when the code.language is Elixir. This allows for Elixir-specific commands. It defines lists of Elixir keywords, and provides implementations for common code actions, including:

  • Inserting comments, operators, and boolean/null values.
  • Inserting conditional statements (if, else if, else, case, for, while).
  • Defining functions (both private and public).
  • Importing, aliasing, requiring, and using modules.

docs.title: html
docs.text:
The community/lang/html directory defines Talon language support for HTML, JavaScript React, and TypeScript React.

The file html.talon uses code.language tags to declare these language associations. This allows Talon to provide appropriate code-specific functionality, such as syntax highlighting, auto-completion, and custom commands, when editing files with these languages.

docs.title: java
docs.text:
The community/lang/java directory contains files to support Java development in Talon.

The java.py file defines the core logic for Java support. It defines Talon contexts, lists of Java keywords, and actions for inserting various Java code constructs.

Here are some of the key features of java.py:

  • Context and Module: It sets up a Talon context that activates when the code language is Java (code.language: java). It also defines a module, mod, for managing lists.
  • Type Lists: It defines several lists:
    • java_primitive_types: Primitive Java types like int, boolean, etc.
    • java_boxed_types: Boxed versions of primitive types like Integer, Boolean, etc.
    • java_common_classes: Common Java classes like String and Object.
    • java_generic_data_structures: Common generic data structures like List, Set, and Map.
    • java_modifiers: Java modifiers like public, private, static, etc.
  • Operator Actions: It provides a comprehensive set of actions for inserting Java operators such as ->, =, +, *, ==, &&, ||, etc.
  • Code Insertion Actions: Defines functions for inserting various code structures like: if/else statements, switch/case statements, for/while loops, return statements, comments, and class and method definitions.
  • Formatted Function Insertion: Defines methods for inserting function declarations for private, protected, and public functions with configurable formatting.

The java.talon file defines the voice commands that trigger the actions defined in java.py.

Here's how java.talon works:

  • Tags: It applies several tags to indicate the kind of code it represents (e.g., user.code_imperative, user.code_object_oriented, user.code_block_c_like).
  • Settings: It configures formatters for inserting functions and variables. These formatters are used by java.py when inserting function declarations.
  • Type Commands:
    • "boxed [type] {user.java_boxed_type}": inserts a boxed type followed by a space (e.g. "boxed integer" inserts Integer).
    • "generic [type] {user.java_generic_data_structure}": inserts a generic type with angle brackets (e.g. "generic list" inserts List<>).
  • Array Commands
    • "type {user.code_type} array": inserts a type followed by square brackets for array declaration (e.g. "type int array" inserts int[]).
  • Modifier Command
    • "[state] {user.java_modifier}": inserts a Java modifier followed by a space (e.g. "state public" inserts public).
  • Operator Commands:
    • "op array": inserts square brackets using user.code_operator_subscript.
    • "op new": inserts new

docs.title: javascript
docs.text:
The community/lang/javascript directory contains files that provide support for JavaScript, TypeScript, and React-based languages within the Talon voice control system.

The core logic for this support is in javascript.py. This file defines Talon contexts, lists, and actions specific to JavaScript and related languages. The file starts by defining a context that activates when the code language is set to Javascript, Typescript, JavascriptReact, or TypescriptReact. It then goes on to define a variety of lists used by the voice commands:

  • user.code_common_function: Contains common JavaScript functions like Math.abs, console.log, and Object.keys.
  • user.code_common_member_function: Contains methods frequently used in dotted chains, such as map, filter, and join.
  • user.code_keyword: Defines common JavaScript keywords such as async, await, class, and function.

This file also defines a large number of actions within the UserActions class which are used by the voice commands to generate code. Some examples include actions for inserting if, else, for, and while statements, operators such as +, -, ==, and &&, and keywords such as break, continue, return, and import. The file also contains actions to generate function declarations, with options for private, protected and public scope.

javascript.talon sets up the Talon commands and tags for this language support, enabling the voice commands defined in the python file. It sets a number of tags that are common to code, such as user.code_imperative, user.code_block_c_like, user.code_functions, and user.code_operators_assignment. This enables those generic commands to work in JavaScript contexts, as well as specific JavaScript commands. It also sets settings to be used when formatting function declarations.

This file defines several voice commands. For example, "state const" will insert const, and "dot map" will insert .map(). It also includes deprecated commands for "state map", "state filter", and "state reduce" which display an error notification that directs the user to use the "dot" versions instead.

docs.title: kotlin
docs.text:
