The community/lang/kotlin directory contains files that provide support for the Kotlin programming language within the Talon voice control system.

The file kotlin.py defines a Talon context that activates when the user specifies the code language as Kotlin. It then defines a number of actions for inserting common Kotlin syntax elements, such as:

  • Keywords: var, val, lateinit, access modifiers (public, private, protected), companion object, synchronized, volatile, transient, abstract, interface, final, and return.
  • Operators: lambda (->), subscript ([]), assignment (=), arithmetic (+, -, *, /, %), comparison (==, !=, >, >=, <, <=), logical (&&, ||), and bitwise (&, |, ^, <<, >>) operators.
  • Other common code constructs: null (null), is null (== null), is not null (!= null), this.
  • Control flow statements: if, else if, else, when (switch), for, and while loops.
  • Class and function declarations.
  • Function insertion with formatting.

The file kotlin.talon configures the Kotlin language support. It assigns tags that indicate that Kotlin is an imperative, object-oriented language. It also enables support for features like line and block comments, boolean and null data types, functions, libraries, array operators, assignment operators, bitwise operators, lambda operators, math operators, and keywords. Finally, it configures settings for formatting functions and variables.

docs.title: lua
docs.text:
The community/lang/lua directory contains files that provide support for the Lua programming language in Talon.

The file lua.py is the main Python file for the Lua language support. It defines a Talon context that activates when the code language is set to Lua. It also defines a setting lua_version to specify which version of Lua is being used, which affects the available bitwise operators, and a stylua tag used by the stylua.talon file.

The file defines lists of common functions, libraries, and actions for working with the Lua programming language. It defines a capture lua_functions for common functions. It also defines the following user actions:

  • code_state_*: For inserting control flow keywords like if, else, for, while, etc.
  • code_comment_*: For inserting single-line and multi-line comments.
  • code_insert_*: For inserting booleans (true, false), null (nil), functions, and libraries.
  • code_operator_*: For inserting operators, including assignment, math, comparison, and bitwise operators. Bitwise operators are version-dependent, using either the bit library or the built-in bitwise operators, as indicated by the lua_version setting.

The file lua.talon defines the Talon commands for the Lua language. It defines several tags, including code_imperative, code_comment_line, code_comment_block, code_data_bool, code_data_null, code_functions, code_functions_common, code_libraries, code_operators_array, code_operators_assignment, code_operators_bitwise, code_operators_math, and code_operators_pointer. It also defines settings for formatting code using SNAKE_CASE for various code elements like private functions, public functions, private variables, and public variables.

The lua.talon file provides voice commands for inserting:

  • Keywords, such as local, end, then, repeat, and until.
  • return statements with various values (null, true, false, or a table).
  • String appends ...
  • Labels using :: for use with goto.
  • require statements for libraries.
  • Variable declarations using the code_public_variable_formatter or code_private_variable_formatter.
  • Object method calls using :.
  • self. reference.
  • Index access using [].

The file stylua.talon defines the Talon commands for interacting with the stylua Lua code formatter. It defines a user.stylua tag that can be used to enable the linting commands, and provides commands for inserting stylua ignore statements: lint ignore, lint ignore start, and lint ignore end.

docs.title: markdown
docs.text:
The community/lang/markdown directory provides support for editing Markdown files in Talon.

The file markdown.py defines a Talon context that is active when the code language is set to markdown. It also defines a list of languages that can be used in Markdown code blocks (user.markdown_code_block_language).

The file markdown.talon provides commands for inserting common Markdown elements, such as headings, lists, and code blocks. For example, saying "heading one" will insert a level one heading, "list two" will insert a level two list item, and saying "typescript block" will insert a code block with the language set to Typescript. The command "link" inserts the text []() and moves the cursor to the correct place to insert a link.

docs.title: php
docs.text:
The community/lang/php directory contains files that provide support for the PHP programming language in Talon.

The core functionality is defined in php.py. This file defines a Talon context that activates when the code language is set to PHP. It provides several actions to ease writing PHP code including:

  • Inserting common keywords and operators such as class, use, ->, true, false, null, is_null(), isset(), =, +, -, *, /, %, ==, ===, !=, !==, >, <, >=, <=, &&, and ||.
  • Inserting common control flow statements like if, elseif, else, while, for, foreach, switch, case, do, goto, return, break, and continue.
  • Defining and inserting different types of functions including public, protected, private and their static variants.
  • Inserting a return type declaration such as : int or : string.

These actions are all defined in a class called UserActions, which inherits from Talon's user action class.

The file php.talon is a Talon script that sets up the context and provides a few additional features.

  • It activates the user.code_imperative, user.code_object_oriented, and user.code_libraries tags, indicating that PHP is an imperative and object-oriented language with libraries.
  • It also activates user.code_block_c_like, user.code_comment_line, user.code_comment_block, user.code_comment_documentation, user.code_data_bool, user.code_data_null, user.code_operators_assignment, user.code_operators_math, and user.code_functions, indicating various code styles and features are supported in this context.
  • It sets several user settings to format function and variable names to PRIVATE_CAMEL_CASE.
  • It provides commands for loosely equals == and loosely not equals !=.
  • It provides try and catch snippets.
  • Finally, it provides a command for inserting variables, using the PRIVATE_CAMEL_CASE formatting.

Together, these two files provide a comprehensive set of tools for writing PHP code using Talon. php.py defines the actions, and php.talon sets the context, tags, formatting and adds some convenient shorthand.

docs.title: proto
docs.text:
The community/lang/proto directory contains files to enable voice coding in protobuf.

The file proto.py defines a Talon context that activates when the code language is set to protobuf. It also defines a list named user.code_type that maps spoken words to protobuf data types such as "string", "int32", and "double".

The file proto.talon defines Talon commands for protobuf. It adds the user.code_block_c_like tag, which is used by other Talon files to add code editing commands. It defines voice commands for the keywords and syntax used in protobuf files, including "message", "package", "enum", "import", "option", and "repeated". It also leverages the user.code_type list defined in proto.py to construct data type declarations via voice. For example, "type string" will output "string", and "repeated type int32" will output "repeated int32".

docs.title: python
docs.text:
The community/lang/python directory contains files that provide support for the Python programming language in Talon. It defines custom commands, lists, and actions that make coding in Python easier using voice.

The core functionality is split between two files:

  • python.talon: This file defines the Talon grammar for Python. It includes:

    • Tags that enable Python-specific functionality like user.code_imperative and user.code_object_oriented.
    • Settings for formatting variable and function names. For example, user.code_private_function_formatter = "SNAKE_CASE" will format private functions in snake case.
    • Grammar rules for common Python syntax constructs, such as def (defined as "state def"), try, except, and raise. These rules provide shortcuts to generate frequently used syntax. For example, saying "state try" will insert "try:\n".
    • Rules to insert documentation strings with docstring fields, for example, "dock string" and "dock type {user.code_type}". These rules leverage the user defined lists in python.py.
    • Commands for importing libraries using import <user.code_libraries>.

  • python.py: This file contains the Python code that implements the logic behind the grammar defined in python.talon. It includes:

    • A context that matches when the code language is Python.
    • Lists of common functions (user.code_common_function), docstring fields (user.python_docstring_fields), types (user.code_type), keywords (user.code_keyword), and exceptions (user.python_exception). These are used to provide autocompletion and voice commands for the corresponding elements.
    • Action implementations that handle various code insertion tasks. The actions include:
      • Inserting operators using commands like "code operator lambda" which will insert lambda :
      • Inserting code states like "if", "else if", "else", "for", and "while".
      • Inserting keywords and boolean values.
      • Inserting and formatting functions, both private and public. For example, saying "public function foo bar" will insert def foo_bar(): with the cursor in the parenthesis.
      • Inserting type annotations and return types.
      • Inserting language specific comments with "code comment line prefix" and docstrings with "code comment documentation".
      • Implementing functions such as code_break and code_next

These two files work together to create a powerful and intuitive voice coding experience for Python. The python.talon file defines the commands, while python.py provides the actions and lists of words that those commands use.

docs.title: r
docs.text:
The community/lang/r directory contains files that provide support for the R programming language in Talon.

The core functionality is in r.py, which defines a Talon context that is active when the current code language is R. This file is responsible for:

  • Defining lists of common R functions, libraries, and parameter names, such as "as data frame", "ggplot2", and "colour". These lists are used by other Talon scripts (e.g. r.talon) to provide voice commands for inserting code.
  • Defining Talon actions for common R code constructs, such as assignment (<-), operators (+, -, *, /), control flow (if, for, while), and comments (#). It also defines actions for inserting NULL, TRUE, and FALSE.
  • Defining actions for inserting functions, libraries, and named arguments. The code_private_function action formats the given text as a function name using snake case, inserts the basic function definition and positions the cursor for typing the parameters.

r.talon defines the voice commands for R. It includes:

  • Tags for enabling various code-related features such as imperative programming, C-like block structures, line comments, boolean data, null values, function-related commands, library management, assignment operators, bitwise operators, and math operators.
  • Settings for how private, protected, and public functions and variables should be formatted when inserted by Talon. In this case, all are set to snake case.
  • Voice commands for inserting R-specific code elements, such as the pipe operator (%>%) and NA.
  • A command to insert library statements.
  • A command to define a private function, which calls the code_private_function action in r.py.
  • A command to insert named arguments based on the user.code_parameter_name list defined in r.py.

In summary, r.py defines the underlying logic and lists of terms for R support in Talon, while r.talon provides the voice commands that users can use to interact with R code. Together they provide a robust set of voice commands for interacting with R.

docs.title: ruby
docs.text:
The community/lang/ruby directory contains files that provide support for the Ruby programming language within the Talon voice control system.

The file ruby.py defines a Talon context that activates when the current code language is Ruby. It then defines a set of user actions for common Ruby code constructs, such as operators, keywords, and control flow statements. These actions use actions.auto_insert and actions.insert to write code and actions.key to move the cursor. For instance, code_operator_lambda inserts ->, code_state_if inserts if, and code_insert_null inserts nil. It also includes some more complex actions such as code_default_function, which uses a formatter setting to insert a function definition with the correct casing, defined by settings.get("user.code_public_function_formatter").

The file ruby.talon provides the Talon voice commands that trigger the actions defined in ruby.py. It sets tags such as user.code_imperative, user.code_object_oriented, user.code_comment_line, and user.code_operators_math to enable other Talon scripts that depend on these tags, for example code navigation and editing features. It also defines settings such as the function and variable formatters used by actions in ruby.py. Finally, it provides some additional voice commands such as: * args pipe: user.insert_between("|", "|") for inserting pipes as arguments to a function or a block. * dock string: user.code_comment_documentation() for inserting a comment block, even though documentation comments are not actually strings in Ruby. * state end: "end" for inserting the end keyword. * state begin: "begin" for inserting the begin keyword. * state rescue: "rescue " for inserting the rescue keyword. * state module: "module " for inserting the module keyword. * ^instance <user.text>$: which inserts @ followed by a formatted variable name.

These two files work together to provide a comprehensive set of voice commands for writing Ruby code. The .py file defines the actions and the .talon file defines the voice commands that trigger them, as well as enabling tags for other general code scripts.

docs.title: rust
docs.text:
The community/lang/rust directory contains files that provide support for the Rust programming language in Talon.

The most important file in this directory is rust.py. This file defines a Talon module that provides a wide range of actions, lists, and captures for working with Rust code. It includes:

  • Lists of code elements: rust.py defines lists such as code_type (for Rust types like i32, String, Vec), code_macros (for macros like println!, vec!), and code_trait (for traits like Iterator, From). These lists are used in rust.talon to allow for easy insertion of these elements via voice commands.
  • Actions for code manipulation: It provides actions for inserting code blocks, such as code_state_implements which inserts an impl block, code_state_unsafe which inserts an unsafe block, and actions for inserting macros, documentation comments, and various other common code constructs. Many of these actions use actions.auto_insert or actions.user.insert_between to insert text and adjust the cursor position.
  • Context-specific settings: The context ctx is configured to only be active when the current language is Rust (code.language: rust). This ensures that these commands only activate when working in a Rust file.
  • Operator and keyword support: The file also defines actions for inserting various operators, such as assignment, arithmetic, and bitwise operators, as well as keywords such as if, for, and while.

rust.talon is the Talon file that uses the definitions from rust.py to bind spoken commands to actions. Here's a breakdown of its contents:

  • Tagging: It assigns several tags (user.code_comment_line, user.code_block_c_like, user.code_imperative, etc.) to the context. These tags activate general Talon commands that are appropriate for working with code.
  • Settings: It sets formatting options for private, protected, and public functions and variables, which are used by other code actions.
  • Rust-specific grammars: It defines voice commands for Rust-specific keywords and constructs, such as:
    • state unsafe: Inserts the unsafe keyword.
    • unsafe block: Calls user.code_state_unsafe() to insert an unsafe block.
    • state struct <user.text>: Inserts a struct declaration with a formatted name.
    • state enum <user.text>: Inserts an enum declaration with a formatted name.
    • borrow and borrow mutable: Inserts & and &mut, respectively.
    • state pub, state pub crate, state dyn, state const, state func, state imp, state let, state mute, state mod, state ref, state trait, state match, state some, state static, self taught, and state use which insert their corresponding keywords and symbols.
    • use <user.code_libraries>: Inserts a use statement with a library from the user.code_libraries list defined in rust.py.
    • state if let some, state if let (ok | okay), and state if let error which insert their corresponding if let statements.
    • macro {user.code_macros}: Inserts a macro from the user.code_macros list.
    • macro wrap {user.code_macros}: Inserts a macro from the user.code_macros list, wrapping the current selection.
  • Rust-specific comments: Adds commands for inserting block, inner, and inner block documentation comments.

In summary, rust.py defines the logic and data structures for interacting with Rust code, while rust.talon translates spoken commands into actions using the definitions from rust.py. Together, these files enable users to write and navigate Rust code using voice commands.

docs.title: scala
docs.text:
The community/lang/scala directory contains files that add support for the Scala programming language to Talon.

The file scala.py defines the core functionality for the Scala language within Talon. It sets up a context that activates when the user specifies the code language as Scala. It defines lists of common Scala types, modifiers, and keywords and makes them available via Talon's user. namespace so they can be easily referenced in voice commands. It also defines a large number of actions, such as inserting operators, control flow statements, and comments, as well as providing methods for defining different types of functions and variables. These actions are available in the user namespace and are used in the .talon files.

The file scala.talon uses the actions and lists defined in scala.py to bind specific voice commands to actions. For example, the command "state match" will insert a switch statement via the action defined in scala.py. This file also adds tags to the Scala context that define the code style, such as "imperative", "object oriented", and others. It also sets formatting options used by the actions defined in scala.py when creating functions. This file also defines a few additional commands specific to scala, such as inserting the arrow operator or a multiline string.

docs.title: sql
docs.text:
The community/lang/sql directory contains files to enable Talon voice control for SQL.

The file sql.py defines a Talon context for SQL, which activates when the code language is set to SQL. It defines common functions for the language, and provides action implementations.

The UserActions class contains implementations for code operators such as +, -, =, <, >, IN, AND, and OR, and also includes actions for inserting null values, comments, and generic function calls. These actions are used by the commands defined in sql.talon.

The file sql.talon defines a set of voice commands for SQL. It includes tags for common code operations like math operators, commenting, null data, and common functions. It also includes commands for common SQL keywords, such as SELECT, FROM, WHERE, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, JOIN, DESC, and ASC. There are also commands for common SQL patterns such as WITH clauses, and inserting new columns, the count() function, and inserting dates.

docs.title: stata
docs.text:
The community/lang/stata directory contains files that add support for the Stata language to Talon.

The stata.talon file, located at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/lang/stata/stata.talon, defines the Talon language mode for Stata. This file primarily sets up the necessary tags to enable Stata-specific functionality and defines some basic voice commands. * It sets the code.language to stata. * It adds the user.code_imperative, user.code_comment_block_c_like, user.code_comment_block, user.code_comment_line, user.code_functions, user.code_functions_common, user.code_libraries, user.code_operators_array, and user.code_operators_assignment tags, which activate code-related voice commands. * It configures the user.code_private_function_formatter setting to "SNAKE_CASE". * It defines commands such as arg {user.code_parameter_name} to insert a named argument, state for val to insert a for loop, and s s c install to insert an "ssc install" command.

The stata.py file, located at https://github.com/talonhub/community/blob/main/lang/stata/stata.py, contains Python code that provides the implementation of the actions and lists used in stata.talon.

  • It sets up a Talon context that matches when the code.language is set to stata.
  • It defines lists, such as user.code_parameter_name, user.code_common_function, and user.code_libraries. These are used to provide the voice commands in stata.talon with a set of available options.
  • It defines an action class UserActions that implements various code-related actions for Stata. These actions include inserting comments, defining functions, handling conditional and loop statements, importing libraries, inserting operators, and more.
  • The functions in UserActions use Talon actions to insert text, manipulate the cursor, and format code.

In summary, stata.talon provides the voice commands and stata.py defines the corresponding python code, together providing a user with voice control over a Stata code editor.

docs.title: tags
docs.text:
The community/lang/tags directory contains a collection of .talon and .py files that define tags, actions, and rules for common programming language constructs. These files provide building blocks for more specific language grammars.

Here's a breakdown of the key files and their functionalities:

  • comment_block.talon, comment_line.talon, comment_block.py, and comment_line.py: These files work together to provide commands for inserting comments into code.

    • comment_block.talon and comment_line.talon define the voice commands like "block comment", "comment line", and variations for inserting comments. They use actions defined in the corresponding .py files.
    • comment_block.py defines actions for inserting C-style block comments (/* ... */) and includes a tag (user.code_comment_block_c_like) that can be used to activate specific comment actions for C-like languages. It also provides the actions code_comment_block_prefix and code_comment_block_suffix which are used by comment_block.talon to implement features like "open block comment" and "close block comment".
    • comment_line.py defines an action code_comment_line_prefix for inserting a line comment prefix.

  • data_bool.talon and data_bool.py: These provide commands for inserting boolean values.

    • data_bool.talon defines commands like "state true" and "state false" that invoke actions from data_bool.py.
    • data_bool.py defines the actions code_insert_true and code_insert_false.

  • functions_common.talon, functions_common.py, and functions_common_gui_active.talon: These files provide a mechanism for inserting common functions, potentially with a GUI for selection.

    • functions_common.talon defines commands for toggling function lists and inserting or wrapping functions, such as "toggle funk", "funk name", and "funk wrap name".
    • functions_common.py defines actions for displaying a function selection GUI, selecting a function from the list, and inserting the chosen function. It also defines the code_common_function capture that is used in other files like functions.talon. The GUI is implemented using Talon's imgui module.
    • functions_common_gui_active.talon provides the "toggle funk" command when the GUI is active.

  • operators_math.py and operators_math.talon: These files define commands for inserting math operators.

    • operators_math.py defines actions like code_operator_addition, code_operator_subtraction, etc. for common math operations.
    • operators_math.talon provides voice commands like "op plus", "op minus", etc., that invoke the actions in operators_math.py.

  • libraries.talon and libraries.py: These enable commands related to library imports.

    • libraries.talon defines the command "state import" that uses the action code_import defined in libraries.py.
    • libraries.py provides the action for inserting library import statements. It also defines the code_libraries capture for use in language specific files.

  • operators_pointer.talon and operators_pointer.py: These files define commands for inserting pointer operators.

    • operators_pointer.talon provides commands like "op dereference", "op address of", and "op arrow".
    • operators_pointer.py defines the actions that correspond to those commands, such as code_operator_indirection, code_operator_address_of, and code_operator_structure_dereference.

  • imperative.py and imperative.talon: These provide commands for inserting common imperative programming constructs.

    • imperative.py defines actions for inserting code blocks, loops, conditional statements, and other imperative constructs such as code_block, code_state_if, and code_state_while. It also includes a tag code_block_c_like which is used by imperative.talon in C-like languages.
    • imperative.talon provides voice commands like "block", "state if", and "state while" that trigger the actions in imperative.py.

  • functions.talon and functions.py: These files define commands for inserting and modifying function declarations.

    • functions.talon defines the user.code_function_modifier capture, and the commands for inserting functions, such as "funky name", "private funky name", and also includes commands for annotating function parameters with types.
    • functions.py defines actions for handling function modifiers (private, protected, public, static) when declaring a function. It also defines actions for formatting function names, inserting type annotations, and inserting return types.

  • comment_documentation.py and comment_documentation.talon: These files enable inserting documentation comments.

    • comment_documentation.py defines the code_comment_documentation action, which inserts a documentation comment.
    • comment_documentation.talon defines the "dock comment" command that triggers the code_comment_documentation action.

  • operators_assignment.py and operators_assignment.talon: These provide commands for inserting assignment operators.

    • operators_assignment.py defines actions such as code_operator_assignment, code_operator_addition_assignment, etc.
    • operators_assignment.talon defines the voice commands like "op equals", "op plus equals", etc.

  • operators_lambda.talon and operators_lambda.py: These files enable commands for inserting lambda (anonymous) functions.

    • operators_lambda.talon provides the "op lambda" command.
    • operators_lambda.py defines the corresponding action.

  • operators_array.py and operators_array.talon: These files provide the action and voice command for array subscript operations.

    • operators_array.py defines code_operator_subscript action.
    • operators_array.talon uses this action to provide a voice command, "op subscript".

  • data_null.py and data_null.talon: These files enable commands related to inserting null values.

    • data_null.py defines actions such as code_insert_null, code_insert_is_null, and code_insert_is_not_null.
    • data_null.talon provides voice commands like "state null", "is null", and "is not null" that trigger the actions defined in data_null.py.

  • object_oriented.py and object_oriented.talon: These enable commands related to object-oriented programming.

    • object_oriented.py defines actions for object access, inserting self references, and defining classes, such as code_operator_object_accessor, code_self, and code_define_class.
    • object_oriented.talon provides voice commands such as "self dot", "state self", and "state class" for those actions.

  • operators_bitwise.py and operators_bitwise.talon: These provide commands for inserting bitwise operators.

    • operators_bitwise.py defines the actions for various bitwise operators such as code_operator_bitwise_and, code_operator_bitwise_or, etc.
    • operators_bitwise.talon provides the corresponding voice commands such as "op bitwise and" and "op bitwise or".

  • keywords.py and keywords.talon: These enable commands for inserting language keywords.

    • keywords.py defines the action code_keyword which inserts a list of keywords.
    • keywords.talon provides the command "keyword keyword" which calls the code_keyword action, and uses the code_keyword capture.

In summary, the files in the community/lang/tags directory provide a collection of general-purpose building blocks for working with code. They define tags that can be used to enable specific code-related features, actions that perform common operations, and voice commands for triggering those actions. These files lay the groundwork for language-specific grammars that can be built on top of them, allowing developers to write code more efficiently using voice.

docs.title: talon
docs.text:
The community/lang/talon directory contains files that provide support for writing and editing Talon code within Talon itself, as well as related Python code. These files primarily focus on enabling specific commands, lists, and contexts for Talon language development.

Here's a breakdown of the key files and how they work together:

  • talon.talon: This file sets the code.language to talon and tags the context with user.code_operators_math, user.code_operators_assignment, user.code_comment_line, and user.code_functions_common. These tags likely activate a set of commands for math operators, assignment, comments, and common functions. It also defines a setting block command that inserts the text "settings():\n\t". This file also has a commented-out user.talon_populate_lists tag that when uncommented, activates talon-specific lists of actions, scopes, modes, etcetera.

  • talon_common.talon: This file defines commands common to both Talon and Python files when the user.talon_python tag is active. It uses the user.talon_tags tag to enable other tags, and it sets up lists and captures using user.talon_lists and user.talon_captures. It defines commands to insert key combinations key <user.keys> over: "{keys}" and key <user.modifiers> over: "{modifiers}". It also enables the action {user.talon_actions} command, which uses the user.talon_code_insert_action_call action to insert a Talon action call. This file makes use of the user.talon_populate_lists tag, which is disabled by default.

  • talon-list.talon: This file sets the code.language to talonlist and defines a list of user.talon_lists using the list [require] {user.talon_lists} command. It also defines a list [require] command that will insert the text "list: ". Like talon_common.talon, this file uses the user.talon_populate_lists tag.

  • talon_context.talon: This file provides commands for inserting context requirements into Talon files. It includes requirements for operating system (win require, mac require, linux require), window titles (title require), applications (application [require] [{user.talon_apps}]), modes (mode require [{user.talon_modes}]), tags (tag require [{user.talon_tags}]), and hostnames (host require).

  • talon.py: This Python file sets up the underlying logic for the Talon language support. It defines contexts for Talon (ctx_talon), Python within Talon (ctx_talon_python), and lists (ctx_talon_lists). It uses the user.talon_populate_lists tag to conditionally enable talon lists. The file defines lists for actions, lists, captures, apps, tags, modes, settings, and scopes as well as functions for updating those lists. It implements the talon_code_insert_action_call, talon_code_enable_tag, and talon_code_enable_setting actions. The TalonActions class handles actions when in Talon files, the TalonPythonActions class when in Python files and the UserActions class when in either, which provide actions for inserting operators, comments, and function calls.

  • talon_code_common_function.talon-list: This file defines a list of common functions used in code: insert, key, print, and repeat. This list is likely used to populate the user.code_functions_common tag in talon.talon.

  • talon_python_activator.talon: This file activates the user.talon_python tag, which enables talon-specific Python commands. It provides examples for activating the tag based on the application or window title. This can be used to activate python commands when editing specific files.

In summary, these files provide a comprehensive set of tools for developing Talon code. They define context-aware commands, custom lists, and Python actions that streamline the process of writing and editing Talon files and related python. The user.talon_populate_lists tag controls the activation of many of these features.

docs.title: terraform
docs.text:
The community/lang/terraform directory contains the Talon files for supporting the Terraform language.

The file terraform.py defines the core logic for interacting with Terraform code. It sets up a Talon context that is active when the code language is set to Terraform. It defines lists for common Terraform types (string, number, bool, etc.) and properties (name, type, description, etc.) as well as module blocks (variable, output, provider, and module). The file also defines Talon actions for inserting module blocks, resource blocks, and data source blocks, and overrides the built-in Talon actions for operators, boolean values, and null values. Finally, it defines a code_state_for action for inserting a for loop.

The file terraform.talon defines the voice commands for the terraform language. It activates a number of tags to inherit common functionality such as c-like blocks and comments, bools, null, operators and lambda. It then defines a set of commands for the Terraform language. * block: uses the inherited user.code_block() to wrap the selected text in curly braces. * state {user.terraform_module_block}: inserts the given module block using the code_terraform_module_block action. * resource <user.text>: inserts a resource block with the given name using the code_terraform_resource action. * data [source] <user.text>: inserts a data source block with the given name using the code_terraform_data_source action. * [state] prop {user.terraform_common_property}: inserts a common Terraform property and an assignment operator. * type {user.code_type}: inserts the given type.

Together, these files provide a comprehensive set of tools for interacting with Terraform code using Talon. The terraform.py file provides the underlying logic and actions, while the terraform.talon file provides the voice commands for using those actions.

docs.title: typescript
docs.text:
The community/lang/typescript directory contains files to support the TypeScript language in Talon.

The typescript.talon file defines basic voice commands for inserting common TypeScript syntax. This includes:

  • type union [<user.code_type>]: Inserts a union type (|) using the user defined code_type.
  • type intersect [<user.code_type>]: Inserts an intersection type (&) using the user defined code_type.
  • state type: Inserts type =.
  • as const: Inserts as const.

The typescript.py file defines the context and actions for TypeScript.

  • It defines the user.code_type list, which is used by the typescript.talon file to insert type annotations.
  • It defines actions to insert function declarations with different access modifiers. These actions utilize the formatting settings defined in user settings for public, protected, and private functions.
  • It defines actions to insert type annotations and return types.

The typescript.talon file uses the user.code_type list and other actions defined in the typescript.py file. Together, these two files provide a basic set of voice commands for writing TypeScript code.

docs.title: vimscript
docs.text:
This directory, community/lang/vimscript, contains files to enable Talon voice control for the Vimscript language.

The file vimscript.py defines the core logic for vimscript support. It defines a Talon context that activates when the code language is set to "vimscript". It defines two lists, self.vimscript_functions and self.vimscript_scope, which contain common vimscript functions and scope identifiers, respectively. These lists are then exposed as captures that can be used in other Talon files. Finally, it defines a set of actions for inserting common operators, comments, and state keywords used in vimscript.

The file vimscript.talon defines the voice commands and tags for using vimscript with Talon. It includes the user.code_imperative, user.code_operators_assignment, user.code_operators_math and user.code_comment_line tags, which provide general programming support. It sets a series of formatters for private, protected, and public functions and variables to SNAKE_CASE. The file defines a series of voice commands for inserting vimscript constructs, such as "assign" for inserting a variable assignment, or directly inserting a function from the vimscript_functions list defined in vimscript.py. It also defines several state keywords such as "state command" for "command! ", "state end if" for "endif", etc.

These two files work together to provide a comprehensive voice control experience for vimscript. The python file provides the backend with lists of useful constructs and functions as well as actions for adding in common syntax, while the talon file allows a user to trigger the insertion of those constructs using voice commands.

docs.title: migration_helpers
docs.text:
The community/migration_helpers directory contains the migration_helpers.py script, which is used to convert CSV files to .talon-list files. This conversion is essential because Talon uses .talon-list files to define lists of words and phrases that can be used in voice commands.

The script provides a way to automatically convert many of the CSV files in the repository. It also provides actions that can be used to convert custom CSV files.

Here's how it works:

  • migration_helpers.py: This script defines the logic for converting CSV files to .talon-list files.
    • It defines a CSVData dataclass to store the configuration for each CSV file to convert including the path to the csv file, the path to the output .talon-list file, whether the first line is a header, and whether the spoken form is the first column.
    • It defines a list of supported files to convert as an array of CSVData named supported_csv_files including things like user.git_argument, user.vocabulary, user.letter, user.system_paths and various emoji related files like user.emoji, user.emoticon, and user.kaomoji.
    • The convert_csv_to_talonlist function takes a CSV reader and a CSVData object as input and generates the content of a .talon-list file as a string. It handles cases with one or two columns, skipping comments and blank lines, and correctly handling custom conversions.
    • The convert_files function iterates through all the .csv files and compares them against supported_csv_files to convert them to their corresponding .talon-list files. It also renames the converted CSV files to .csv-converted-to-talon-list, backing up existing .talon-list files.
    • The migrate_known_csv_files action automatically converts all the supported CSV files.
    • The migrate_custom_csv action allows the conversion of a custom CSV file given the path, new path, list name, and flags for header and spoken form.
    • The on_ready function attempts to automatically execute migrate_known_csv_files on startup. It includes a fallback for an identified bug where the action may not be available.

In summary, this script automates the conversion of various CSV files used in the Talon configuration to .talon-list files. The conversion is done according to the defined CSVData configurations and provides a mechanism for custom conversions. It is useful for maintaining the Talon configuration.

docs.title: plugin
docs.text:
The community/plugin directory contains a collection of subdirectories, each representing a distinct plugin that adds functionality to Talon. These plugins enhance Talon's capabilities by providing features like text manipulation, mouse control, macro recording, and more.

The paste_to_insert.py file modifies the default insert behavior. It introduces a setting paste_to_insert_threshold. If the inserted text is longer than this threshold, the text is pasted using the OS clipboard instead of being inserted character by character. This is primarily useful to improve performance when pasting large amounts of text.

The README.md file in the community/plugin directory serves as an index to the various subdirectories. It provides a brief description of the functionality each plugin provides, making it easier for users to navigate the repository and find the plugins that meet their specific needs.

The eye_tracking_settings.py file is commented out and contains platform specific settings for the zoom mouse functionality of eye tracking. It is intended to be used to control the zoom level and zoom window size for eye tracking on macOS and Windows.

Here's a breakdown of the most important subdirectories:

  • symbols: Provides commands for inserting and wrapping text with symbols and delimiters. It includes a deprecated file that will eventually be replaced by a newer implementation.
  • datetimeinsert: Allows users to insert the current date and time in various formats using voice commands. It provides options for local and UTC time with varying levels of precision.
  • macro: Enables users to record, save, and replay macros using voice commands. It includes a GUI for displaying saved macros, and actions to copy macros to the clipboard as Talon commands.
  • repeater: Contains commands to repeat commands and phrases, including a feature to repeat the last command using a double "pop" sound.
  • gamepad: Implements gamepad support in Talon. It maps gamepad inputs to Talon actions, enabling mouse control and other functionality, and provides a gamepad tester to ensure correct bindings.
  • listening_timeout: Automatically disables speech recognition after a period of inactivity, potentially saving system resources.
  • mode_indicator: Displays a visual indicator showing the current Talon mode (command, dictation, etc.), along with microphone mute status.
  • subtitles: Implements custom subtitles that display spoken text on the screen.
  • are_you_sure: Adds a confirmation dialog before executing potentially destructive commands, helping prevent accidental data loss.
  • mouse: Provides a comprehensive suite of mouse control features including zoom mouse, dragging, scrolling, and cursor visibility toggling.
  • draft_editor: Enables users to use a text editor as a temporary "draft editor" within Talon. This allows you to quickly capture and edit text using a separate application and then paste the result back into the original application.
  • dropdown: Contains deprecated commands to trigger and navigate dropdown menus.
  • desktops: Provides cross-platform support for controlling virtual desktops with voice commands, with OS-specific implementations.
  • screenshot: Allows users to take screenshots of different areas of the screen or specific windows.
  • microphone_selection: Provides a GUI to select the active microphone from a list, with dynamic updating of available devices.
  • talon_helpers: Includes actions and commands that are useful for Talon development and debugging.
  • text_navigation: Provides powerful text navigation and manipulation using regular expressions and natural language commands.
  • command_history: Displays a history of recently spoken commands.
  • cancel: Implements a command cancellation feature using a special phrase or when waking from sleep.
  • talon_draft_window: Provides a dedicated window for editing text via voice commands using an anchor based system for navigation.
  • media: Contains commands for controlling media playback using voice commands.
  • then: Contains a single command, then, used to separate chained commands for improved speech recognition accuracy.

These plugins enhance the core functionality of Talon, providing a more flexible and user-friendly experience, and can be mixed and matched to suit individual workflows and preferences.

docs.title: are_you_sure
docs.text:
The are_you_sure plugin provides a mechanism to require confirmation before executing potentially destructive actions. It displays a simple dialog with a message and "Yes I am sure" and "Cancel" buttons.

The core logic resides in are_you_sure.py. It defines a ConfirmationState class that manages the current confirmation request and handles the confirmation or cancellation of the action. The request_confirmation method takes a message, a confirmation callback, and an optional cancellation callback. It displays a simple GUI using imgui with the message and buttons and sets a tag "user.are_you_sure" to activate the Talon commands defined in are_you_sure.talon. It also defines actions are_you_sure_confirm, are_you_sure_cancel and are_you_sure_set_on_confirmation_action which are used to confirm, cancel and set the action to be confirmed.

The are_you_sure.talon file provides the Talon commands used to interact with the confirmation dialog. Saying "yes I am sure" triggers the user.are_you_sure_confirm() action, and saying "cancel" triggers user.are_you_sure_cancel(). These actions are defined in the Actions class in are_you_sure.py.

The README.md provides a basic overview of the plugin and how to use it. It includes an example showing how to use the user.are_you_sure_set_on_confirmation_action action, including defining the callbacks for confirmation and cancellation.

In summary, this plugin provides a way to add confirmation prompts to Talon commands, making it safer to use potentially destructive commands.

docs.title: cancel
docs.text:
The community/plugin/cancel directory contains files that implement a command cancellation feature for Talon. This allows the user to prevent a command from executing after it has been spoken.

The core logic is implemented in cancel.py. It registers a pre:phrase callback with the Talon speech system. This callback is executed before a spoken phrase is processed by Talon. The callback checks two conditions:

  • Wake-up Threshold: If Talon has just woken up, the callback checks if the start of the current phrase is before the wake-up time. This prevents spurious commands from being executed if the user made noise or said something before waking up Talon.
  • Cancellation Phrase: If the spoken phrase ends with the cancellation phrase ("cancel cancel" by default), the callback cancels the phrase and displays a notification.

The file cancel.talon defines the Talon grammar for the cancellation functionality. It defines the cancel cancel phrase to call the skip() action which does nothing and thus prevents any further execution of the phrase. It also has an ignore phrase which will simply display a notification. This is useful when you wish to speak but do not want Talon to process the command.

These two files work together: cancel.talon defines the grammar that triggers cancellation, while cancel.py implements the cancellation logic. The Python code is responsible for inspecting the phrase before it gets processed by any Talon commands and deciding whether to cancel it. This feature is useful for preventing unwanted command execution.

docs.title: command_history
docs.text:
The command_history directory contains files for a Talon plugin that displays a history of recently spoken commands.

The core logic is within command_history.py. This file defines:

  • Settings command_history_size and command_history_display which control the maximum size of the history and the number of lines displayed by default, respectively.
  • A history list to store the command history.
  • An on_phrase callback that appends new phrases to the history. This function also calls actions.user.history_transform_phrase_text which transforms the phrase before it is appended to the history. By default it just joins the words with spaces but can be overridden to filter phrases or to add additional context.
  • An imgui based GUI that displays the history and a close button. The number of lines displayed varies depending on the hist_more global variable.
  • A set of Talon actions for interacting with the history including history_toggle, history_enable, history_disable, history_clear, history_more, history_less and history_get.

command_history.talon provides voice commands for interacting with the command history, calling the actions defined in command_history.py. For example, saying "command history" will toggle the history window.

docs.title: datetimeinsert
docs.text:
The community/plugin/datetimeinsert directory contains files for a Talon plugin that inserts the current date and time into the active text field.

The core functionality is implemented in datetimeinsert.py. This file defines a Talon module with two actions:

  • time_format(fmt: str = None) -> str: This action returns the current local time, formatted according to the provided format string (fmt). If no format is provided, it defaults to ISO format. It utilizes Python's datetime module to get the current time and format it.
  • time_format_utc(fmt: str = None) -> str: This action is similar to time_format but returns the current UTC time instead of the local time.

The datetimeinsert.talon file defines Talon commands that invoke the actions defined in datetimeinsert.py. These commands include:

  • date insert: Inserts the current date in YYYY-MM-DD format.
  • date insert UTC: Inserts the current UTC date in YYYY-MM-DD format.
  • timestamp insert: Inserts the current date and time in YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS format.
  • timestamp insert high resolution: Inserts the current date and time in YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS.ffffff format (including microseconds).
  • timestamp insert UTC: Inserts the current UTC date and time in YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS format.
  • timestamp insert UTC high resolution: Inserts the current UTC date and time in YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS.ffffff format (including microseconds).

In summary, the datetimeinsert plugin provides a convenient way to insert formatted date and time strings using Talon commands, with options for local time, UTC time, and varying levels of precision.

docs.title: desktops
docs.text:
The desktops directory contains files that allow the user to control virtual desktops using Talon. The core functionality is defined in desktops.py, which provides a set of actions that are then implemented in OS-specific files.

Here's a breakdown:

  • desktops.py: This file defines the base action class Actions with methods for switching, showing, and moving windows between desktops. These methods use app.notify to indicate that they are not supported by the base implementation. This file sets the structure for all operating systems and is overridden with OS-specific implementations.

  • desktops_linux.py: This file provides the Linux implementation of the actions defined in desktops.py. It uses the ui.switch_workspace function to change desktops, ui.active_workspace to determine the active desktop, and modifies the workspace property of windows to move them. It also includes an action to show the current desktops with the "super" key.

  • desktops_mac.py: This file contains the macOS implementation, using actions.key to send keyboard shortcuts for desktop switching (e.g., "ctrl-1" for desktop 1). It also implements window movement by simulating a drag action on the fullscreen button in the UI, then changing desktops while dragging, and then dropping it, and provides special handling for the Amethyst window manager if it is installed.

  • desktops_win.py: The Windows implementation uses actions.key to simulate the "super-ctrl-right/left" keyboard shortcuts for switching desktops, and shows the current desktops with "super-tab". Note that there is no implementation of desktop(number: int) or window moving as the OS implementations of those features are quite complex.

  • desktops.talon: This Talon file defines the voice commands that trigger the actions defined in the python files. For example, "desk 1" will call user.desktop(1), and "window move desk left" will call user.window_move_desktop_left().

In summary, this directory provides a cross-platform way to control virtual desktops in Talon, using a base implementation that is overridden by OS-specific implementations to provide a consistent user experience, using a unified set of voice commands.

docs.title: draft_editor
docs.text:
The community/plugin/draft_editor directory implements a system for using a text editor as a temporary "draft editor" within Talon. This allows you to quickly capture thoughts or code snippets, edit them in a full-fledged editor, and then paste the result back into the original application.

The key files and their roles are:

  • draft_editor.talon: This Talon file defines the core commands for initiating the draft editor. It leverages a user.draft_editor_app_running tag to determine if the configured editor app is running and a user.draft_editor_app_focused tag to determine if the configured editor app has focus. The commands are:

    • draft this: Opens the draft editor with the current selection.
    • draft all: Opens the draft editor with all the text in the current document.
    • draft line: Opens the draft editor with the text on the current line.
    • draft top: Opens the draft editor with the text from the beginning of the document to the current cursor position.
    • draft bottom: Opens the draft editor with the text from the current cursor position to the end of the document.
    • draft submit: Pastes the last submitted text from the draft editor.

  • draft_editor_open.talon: This file defines the commands used within the draft editor. It uses the user.draft_editor_active and user.draft_editor_app_focused tags to determine when these commands are active:

    • draft submit: Submits the content from the draft editor and pastes it back into the original application.
    • draft discard: Closes the draft editor without pasting its content back to the original application.

  • draft_editor.py: This Python file contains the core logic for the draft editor.

    • It defines three tags: user.draft_editor_active, user.draft_editor_app_running, and user.draft_editor_app_focused.
    • It introduces the user.draft_editor setting which lets you specify a comma-separated list of application names to use for your draft editor.
    • It manages application lifecycle events, setting the user.draft_editor_app_running and user.draft_editor_app_focused tags.
    • The draft_editor_open action opens a new tab in the configured editor, pastes the selected text (if any), and sets the user.draft_editor_active tag.
    • The draft_editor_submit action saves the text in the draft editor to a variable named last_draft, closes the tab, and pastes the last draft to the original window. It uses a user.switcher_focus_window to return to the original application. If this fails, the user is notified to manually refocus on the destination.
    • The draft_editor_discard action closes the draft editor tab without saving, and focuses back on the original window. If this fails, the user is notified.
    • The draft_editor_paste_last action pastes the last submitted text, making it easy to re-use or paste content in multiple locations.

In summary, this system allows you to:

  1. Use voice commands to open a designated text editor.
  2. Automatically paste the currently selected text or the entire document into the editor.
  3. Edit the text in the editor.
  4. Submit (paste the result back to the original app) or discard the text with voice commands.

The draft editor is an efficient way to edit text in a separate application, and it uses Talon tags to manage the state of the draft editor and its related commands.

docs.title: dropdown
docs.text:
The community/plugin/dropdown directory contains a single file:

  • dropdown.talon: This file is marked as deprecated and contains Talon commands to trigger dropdown menus using a number. It also contains deprecated commands to move the selection up in the menu using a number. This file is likely being phased out in favor of another approach.

docs.title: gamepad
docs.text:
The community/plugin/gamepad directory contains files that enable gamepad support in Talon. The most important files are:

  • gamepad.py: This file defines the core logic for the gamepad bindings. It maps gamepad inputs to Talon actions, such as mouse clicks, scrolling, and navigation. It also includes actions for toggling slow scroll and mouse movement modes.
  • gamepad.talon: This Talon file defines the grammar for binding gamepad inputs to the actions defined in gamepad.py. For example, it maps the "dpad_left:down" gamepad event to the user.gamepad_button_down("dpad_left") action.
  • gamepad_settings.talon: This file contains a tag that must be enabled to activate the gamepad bindings. By default it is commented out. To enable the gamepad bindings you must uncomment tag(): user.gamepad.

These files work together to enable gamepad support. The gamepad.talon file listens for gamepad events, and when an event occurs, it calls the corresponding action in gamepad.py.

The directory also includes a gamepad tester, consisting of:

  • gamepad_tester.py: This file implements a visual interface for testing gamepad inputs. It displays a window with representations of the gamepad's buttons, triggers, and sticks.
  • gamepad_tester.talon: This file provides a command (gamepad tester) to toggle the visibility of the gamepad tester window.
  • gamepad_tester_open.talon: This file defines the grammar for sending gamepad inputs to the tester when the tester is open.

The gamepad tester allows you to visualize your gamepad inputs and ensure they are being recognized by Talon. To use the gamepad tester:

  1. Say "gamepad tester" to open the tester window.
  2. Press buttons and move sticks on your gamepad.
  3. Observe the changes in the tester UI.
  4. Say "gamepad tester" again to close the tester window.

The README.md file contains a basic description of how to use the gamepad functionality and includes links to demo videos.

docs.title: listening_timeout
docs.text:
The community/plugin/listening_timeout directory contains a single file, listening_timeout.py. This file implements a Talon plugin that automatically disables speech recognition after a period of inactivity.

Here's how it works:

  • Settings: The plugin defines two user settings:

    • listening_timeout_minutes: An integer specifying the number of minutes of inactivity before speech recognition is disabled. A value of -1 disables the timeout.
    • listening_timeout_show_notification: A boolean that determines whether a notification should be displayed when the timeout expires.

  • Timeout Logic:

    • The last_phrase_time variable tracks the time of the last detected speech.
    • The start_timeout_job function schedules a check to see if the timeout has elapsed.
    • The stop_timeout_job cancels the current timer.
    • The calculate_timeout function converts minutes to seconds.
    • The check_timeout function checks if the time since the last phrase exceeds the configured timeout. If the timeout has expired, it calls actions.user.listening_timeout_expired() and cancels the timer. Otherwise, if the timeout is enabled, it starts a new timeout job.
    • The post_phrase function is triggered after each phrase. It updates the last_phrase_time and starts a new timeout job (if enabled and speech is active), or stops the existing job if the timeout is disabled.
    • The actions.speech.enable action is overridden to update last_phrase_time and restart the timeout if speech is enabled.
    • The actions.speech.disable action is overridden to cancel the timeout.

  • Actions: The plugin defines a listening_timeout_expired action which disables speech recognition using actions.speech.disable() and shows a notification if configured.

  • Initialization: The on_ready function is called when Talon starts up. It initializes last_phrase_time, starts the timeout job if speech is already enabled, and registers the post_phrase function to be called on every phrase.
  • Notifications: The plugin uses actions.app.notify to display a notification when speech recognition is disabled due to a timeout.

In summary, this plugin introduces a timeout mechanism for speech recognition in Talon. After a configurable amount of inactivity, speech recognition is automatically disabled, potentially saving resources and reducing accidental activations.

docs.title: macro
docs.text:
The community/plugin/macro directory contains files for creating, saving, and replaying macros using voice commands.

The macro.talon file defines the voice commands for interacting with the macro functionality. These commands include:

  • macro record: Starts recording a new macro.
  • macro stop: Stops recording a macro.
  • macro play [{user.saved_macros}]: Plays back the last recorded macro, or a named saved macro.
  • macro copy [{user.saved_macros}]: Copies the last recorded macro, or a named saved macro, to the clipboard as a Talon command.
  • macro copy as <user.text>: Copies the current macro to the clipboard as a Talon command, using the provided text as a name.
  • macro save as <user.text>: Saves the current macro, using the provided text as a name.
  • macro list: Displays a list of saved macros.
  • macro list close: Closes the saved macros list.

The macro.py file contains the Python implementation of the macro functionality. It includes the following:

  • A list called saved_macros that stores the names of saved macros.
  • A dictionary called macros that stores the actual saved macro commands.
  • A list called macro that stores the current macro being recorded.
  • A boolean called recording that indicates whether a macro is currently being recorded.
  • An imgui GUI for displaying the list of saved macros, which is opened by macro list and closed by macro list close.
  • Actions defined in the @mod.action_class that implement the various macro commands. These actions handle recording, saving, loading, and executing macros, and copying macros to the clipboard as Talon commands.
  • A pre:phrase event handler that appends the most recent spoken command to the macro list if recording is enabled.

In summary, macro.talon provides the voice commands, and macro.py provides the underlying logic for recording, saving, and playing back macros. The voice commands in macro.talon interact with the Python actions in macro.py to implement the macro functionality.

docs.title: media
docs.text:
The community/plugin/media directory contains files for controlling media playback.

The core logic is in media.py. It defines an action play_pause that uses the appropriate key for the current operating system. On Windows, it sends the "play_pause" key, while on other platforms it sends the "play" key.

media.talon defines the voice commands for media control. It maps phrases to actions, such as:

  • "volume up" and "volume down" which are mapped to their respective key presses
  • "set volume <number>" which is mapped to a user.media_set_volume action, assumed to be defined elsewhere
  • "volume mute" which is mapped to the "mute" key
  • "media play next" and "media play previous" mapped to the "next" and "prev" keys, respectively
  • "media play" and "media pause" which both map to the user.play_pause() action defined in media.py

Together these files allow the user to control media playback using voice commands.

docs.title: microphone_selection
docs.text:
The community/plugin/microphone_selection directory contains code for a Talon plugin that allows users to select their active microphone from a list using a GUI.

The core logic is in microphone_selection.py. This file defines the plugin's behavior:

  • It uses the cubeb library to get a list of available microphones, filtering for only those that are enabled.
  • It provides an imgui-based graphical user interface to display and select the microphones. The GUI displays each available microphone with an index number, allowing the user to choose by number.
  • The microphone_select action sets the active microphone using actions.sound.set_microphone.
  • The plugin also provides actions to toggle and hide the GUI.
  • The list of available microphones is dynamically updated when the devices change. This means that if a microphone is enabled, disabled, or unplugged, the list will automatically update.

The microphone_selection.talon file provides the Talon commands that interface with the Python logic:

  • ^microphone show$: Triggers user.microphone_selection_toggle(), which opens the GUI.
  • ^microphone close$: Triggers user.microphone_selection_hide(), which hides the GUI.
  • ^microphone pick <number_small>$: Triggers user.microphone_select(number_small), which selects the microphone at the given index in the list.

In summary, the plugin provides an easy way for users to dynamically select their active microphone, especially useful for users that switch between multiple microphones. The Python code handles the logic and GUI while the Talon code enables the user to interact with it via voice commands.

docs.title: mode_indicator
docs.text:
The mode_indicator directory contains the files for a Talon plugin that displays a graphical indicator showing the current Talon mode. The indicator is a colored circle that changes color based on the active mode (command, dictation, sleep, mixed, or other) and whether the microphone is muted. The plugin allows users to customize the size, position, colors, and transparency of the indicator.

The core logic is in mode_indicator.py. This file defines a Module named mod and a number of settings related to the appearance of the mode indicator, such as mode_indicator_show, mode_indicator_size, mode_indicator_x, mode_indicator_y, mode_indicator_color_alpha, and several color settings. It uses Talon's canvas API to draw the indicator on the screen. It also registers callbacks to update the indicator when the Talon mode changes, or when its settings are modified. The on_draw function takes a SkiaCanvas and draws a colored circle with a gradient and shadow. The update_indicator function handles showing, hiding, and moving the indicator. The on_update_contexts function determines the current Talon mode and updates the indicator accordingly. The poll_microphone function checks if the microphone is muted, and if so, updates the indicator color.

mode_indicator.talon defines the default values for the settings declared in mode_indicator.py. This file also includes comments to explain what each setting does and examples of how to configure the indicator's appearance.

README.md provides an overview of the mode indicator plugin, including a description of its functionality, screenshots of the indicator in different modes, instructions on how to enable it, and a link to a YouTube demo.

docs.title: mouse
docs.text:
This directory, community/plugin/mouse, contains several files that work together to provide advanced mouse control functionality in Talon. The main components include:

  • mouse.py: This file defines core mouse actions and settings. It includes actions for enabling and disabling "zoom mouse", dragging, and copying the mouse position, among other things. It also sets up settings for pop click behavior and cursor hiding. The Actions class defines the following important actions:
    • zoom_close(): Closes an in-progress zoom, usually called when the user intends to cancel a zoom.
    • mouse_wake(): Enables control mouse and zoom mouse and hides the cursor if configured.
    • mouse_drag(button: int): Starts dragging with the specified mouse button.
    • mouse_drag_end(): Releases all held mouse buttons, ending any active drag.
    • mouse_drag_toggle(button: int): Toggles dragging with the specified button, starting a drag if not already dragging, and ending it if dragging with that button.
    • mouse_sleep(): Disables control mouse and zoom mouse and shows the cursor again.
    • copy_mouse_position(): Copies the current mouse position to the clipboard.
    • mouse_move_center_active_window(): Moves the cursor to the center of the currently active window. The UserActions class defines noise_trigger_pop(), which is triggered by a pop noise and conditionally performs a mouse click, but can be configured to cancel drags and scrolls instead.
  • mouse.talon: This Talon file binds voice commands to actions defined in mouse.py and other Talon actions. It defines commands to toggle tracking, perform clicks, start and end drags, scroll, copy the mouse position, and more. Some key commands include:
    • control mouse: Toggles control mouse using tracking.control_toggle().
    • control off: Disables control mouse using user.mouse_sleep().
    • zoom mouse: Toggles zoom mouse using tracking.control_zoom_toggle().
    • touch, righty, and mid click: Simulate mouse clicks with left, right, and middle mouse buttons respectively.
    • <user.modifiers> touch and <user.modifiers> righty: Simulate mouse clicks with modifier keys held down.
    • left drag, right drag: Start dragging using the left or right mouse button.
    • end drag: Ends any active drag.
    • wheel up, wheel down, wheel left, and wheel right: Scroll the mouse wheel in the specified direction.
    • wheel upper, wheel downer, and wheel gaze: Scroll continuously.
    • wheel stop: Stops any continuous scrolling.
    • copy mouse position: Copies the mouse coordinates to the clipboard.
  • mouse_scroll.py: This file provides actions for continuous scrolling, scroll acceleration, and scroll direction. It uses Talon's cron module to achieve smooth continuous scrolling. It includes actions for scrolling up, down, left, and right, as well as continuous scrolling and gaze-based scrolling. It also includes the setting mouse_enable_hiss_scroll, which enables scroll with a hiss noise.
  • mouse_cursor.talon: This Talon file provides commands for showing and hiding the mouse cursor by enabling the user.mouse_cursor_commands_enable tag.
  • mouse_cursor.py: This Python file implements the functionality for hiding and showing the mouse cursor, working differently on Windows and other platforms.

These files work together to provide a comprehensive suite of mouse control features. mouse.py defines core logic and settings, mouse.talon exposes these actions via voice commands, mouse_scroll.py adds advanced scrolling, and mouse_cursor.py and mouse_cursor.talon implement cursor visibility toggling.

docs.title: repeater
docs.text:
The community/plugin/repeater directory contains files that enable repeating commands and phrases.

The file repeater.talon defines Talon commands for repeating other commands. It allows the user to specify a number of repetitions via ordinals or numbers, or repeat the command or phrase once or twice, or any number of times. It uses core.repeat_command to repeat an action. It also uses core.repeat_partial_phrase to repeat the last spoken phrase.

The file pop_twice_to_repeat.py defines a way to repeat the last command by making two "pop" noises in quick succession. When the user makes a "pop" sound, Talon records the time, then when it registers another "pop" it checks how much time has elapsed and if it is within the configured minimum and maximum durations it calls actions.core.repeat_command(). This file also provides a tag user.pop_twice_to_repeat that can be used to enable or disable this behavior, and ensures that the pop trigger will not be enabled when mouse zoom is active to avoid conflicts.

docs.title: screenshot
docs.text:
The community/plugin/screenshot directory contains the code for a Talon plugin that allows users to take screenshots in various ways.

The core functionality is implemented in screenshot.py, which defines several actions that can be invoked by voice commands. The most important actions are:

  • screenshot(screen_number: Optional[int] = None): Takes a screenshot of the entire screen, or a specific screen if screen_number is provided. It saves the image to a folder specified by the screenshot_folder setting.
  • screenshot_window(): Takes a screenshot of the currently active window and saves it to the screenshots folder.
  • screenshot_selection(): Triggers the operating system's native screenshot selection tool.
  • screenshot_selection_clip(): Triggers the operating system's native screenshot selection tool and copies the result to the clipboard.
  • screenshot_settings(): Opens the operating system's native screenshot settings (currently only implemented for macOS).
  • screenshot_clipboard(screen_number: Optional[int] = None): Takes a screenshot of the entire screen, or a specific screen if screen_number is provided, and copies it to the clipboard.
  • screenshot_window_clipboard(): Takes a screenshot of the currently active window and copies it to the clipboard.
  • screenshot_rect(rect: ui.Rect, title: str = "", screen_num: Optional[int] = None): Captures the given rectangle area of a screen. It is used by other actions but can be called by other modules too.

The file also defines helper functions for capturing the screenshot (screen.capture_rect), flashing a rectangle on the screen to indicate the area being captured (flash_rect), determining the file path for the saved screenshot (get_screenshot_path), and getting a screen by its number (get_screen).

The screenshot.py file uses contexts to handle platform specific differences. For example, the screenshot_selection() action uses cmd-shift-4 on macOS, super-shift-s on Windows, and shift-printscr on Linux.

The file screenshot.talon defines the voice commands to trigger the actions defined in screenshot.py. It maps phrases like "grab screen", "grab window", and "grab selection" to the corresponding actions. The <number_small> rule is used to specify a particular screen to capture. A not tag: user.screenshot_disabled is used to disable the commands when the screenshot_disabled tag is active.

docs.title: subtitles
docs.text:
The community/plugin/subtitles directory implements custom subtitles in Talon. The subtitles are displayed on screen based on what was spoken. It provides several settings to customize the appearance and behavior of the subtitles.

The core logic for displaying subtitles is in subtitles.py. This file defines the settings that can be used to customize the subtitles, such as:

  • user.subtitles_show: A boolean to enable or disable the subtitles.
  • user.subtitles_screens: Where to display the subtitles - "all", "main", "cursor", or "focus".
  • user.subtitles_size: The font size of the subtitles in pixels.
  • user.subtitles_color: The color of the subtitles text.
  • user.subtitles_color_outline: The color of the subtitles outline.
  • user.subtitles_timeout_per_char: The amount of time in milliseconds to extend the timeout for each character in the subtitle.
  • user.subtitles_timeout_min: The minimum time in milliseconds for a subtitle to be shown.
  • user.subtitles_timeout_max: The maximum time in milliseconds for a subtitle to be shown.
  • user.subtitles_y: The vertical position of the subtitles as a percentage of the screen height (0 = top, 1 = bottom).

The show_subtitle function is the main entry point for displaying a subtitle. It takes the text to be displayed, clears any existing subtitles, determines which screen to use, and then calls show_text_on_screen to draw the subtitle on a canvas. The on_draw function sets the font size, calculates the position of the text, and draws the text with an optional drop shadow and outline. The calculate_timeout function calculates how long to show the subtitle based on the length of the text and the settings for timeout per character, minimum timeout and maximum timeout.

on_phrase.py handles speech events. The on_pre_phrase function is registered as a listener for speech phrases. When a phrase is detected, it calls show_subtitle to display the spoken text. It also handles cases where the phrase should be skipped, for example if the user is in sleep mode.

subtitles.talon sets the default values for the settings defined in subtitles.py, such as whether the subtitles are shown by default, the color and size of the subtitles, and the timeout values. These settings can be customized by the user to suit their needs.

The README.md provides a brief overview of the functionality of the subtitles plugin, instructions for enabling and disabling the subtitles, and links to additional documentation. It also includes an image of the subtitles.

Together, these files provide a complete implementation for custom subtitles in Talon, offering a high degree of customization and flexibility.

docs.title: symbols
docs.text:
The community/plugin/symbols directory contains Talon scripts for inserting and wrapping text with symbols and delimiters.

The file symbols_deprecated.talon defines deprecated commands for inserting pairs of symbols, and wrapping selected text with symbols. For example, saying "empty string" will insert a pair of single quotes, and saying "quote that" will wrap the selected text with single quotes. These commands use user.deprecate_command which will display a warning in the log when used.

The file symbols.talon provides a more modern way of inserting and wrapping text. It defines commands for inserting common symbols like newlines, dashes, triple quotes, ellipses, and arrows. It also defines generic commands for inserting and wrapping text with any delimiter pair, using the user.delimiter_pair_insert and user.delimiter_pair_wrap_selection actions, which are presumably defined elsewhere in the user's configuration. The intention is that this file will eventually replace the functionality in symbols_deprecated.talon.

docs.title: talon_draft_window
docs.text:
The talon_draft_window directory provides a way to edit text in a dedicated window, with a focus on using voice commands for navigation and manipulation. It is primarily used to make editing text via voice easier, especially for prose.

The core functionality is implemented in the Python file draft_ui.py. This file defines the DraftManager class, which manages the text area window and its styling. It uses the experimental talon.experimental.textarea API. The DraftManager provides methods for showing/hiding the window, setting its content, moving and resizing, and also for selecting text. A key feature is the ability to label words with single-character "anchors" that can be used in voice commands.
