The calculate_text_anchors function is responsible for calculating the position of these labels, ensuring they are centered around the current cursor position.

The file draft_talon_helpers.py sets up the Talon integration. It defines settings for customizing the window's appearance using mod.setting (e.g., theme, label size, label color, and text size). It also registers a callback (_update_draft_style) that will update the DraftManager when these settings are changed. This file also defines a workaround for the character-by-character undo behavior of the experimental text area via the UndoWorkaround class. This workaround enables a history of changes to be logged so that user can undo and redo edits made to text in the draft window. The Actions class in this file makes the draft window's functionality accessible to Talon commands. This includes actions for showing/hiding the window, selecting text using anchors, positioning the caret, getting the text content, resizing the window, and moving it to named screen positions. Lastly, it defines two capture groups draft_anchor and draft_window_position that are used by other Talon files.

The main Talon command files are:

  • draft_global.talon: This file defines global commands (available in command mode) for showing the draft window, including variations for moving and resizing the window using named positions or specific dimensions. It also allows you to open the draft window with the currently selected text.
  • draft_window_open.talon: This file defines commands available when the draft window is open (but not necessarily focused), such as hiding the window and submitting the text back into the original application.
  • draft_window.talon: This file provides commands used when the draft window is in focus. These include commands to replace a single word, position the cursor, select words or ranges of words, delete text, and reformat selected text using formatters. These commands make heavy use of the draft_anchor capture group defined in draft_talon_helpers.py.

The file settings.talon.example shows an example of how to customize the look and feel of the draft window by overriding the default settings.

The README.md file provides a basic introduction, usage example, customization instructions, and details on how to run the included tests.

The file test_draft_ui.py contains unit tests for the calculate_text_anchors function.

The __init__.py file is empty, which makes this directory importable as a Python package for testing.

In summary, the talon_draft_window plugin provides a robust system for editing text via voice commands using an anchor-based system for navigation and selection. It consists of a core UI class, Talon helpers to provide actions, and several Talon files to define the voice commands for editing text in a draft window.

docs.title: talon_helpers
docs.text:
The talon_helpers directory contains a collection of files that provide actions and commands to help with Talon development and debugging.

The most important files in this directory are:

  • talon_helpers.py: This file defines a set of Talon actions that can be used to debug and inspect the Talon environment. These actions include:
    • talon_add_context_clipboard_python and talon_add_context_clipboard: These actions copy context code to the clipboard that can be pasted into a Talon module to activate it for specific applications.
    • talon_sim_phrase: Simulates a spoken phrase and outputs the result to the log.
    • talon_action_find: Finds and logs information about a given Talon action.
    • talon_debug_list: Dumps the contents of a specified Talon list to the console.
    • talon_debug_tags, talon_debug_modes, and talon_debug_scope: Log various aspects of the Talon scope and state.
    • talon_copy_list: Copies the contents of a specified Talon list to the clipboard.
    • talon_debug_setting and talon_debug_all_settings: Log Talon settings.
    • talon_get_active_context: Returns active context information (application name, executable, etc.).
    • talon_get_hostname: Returns the current hostname.
    • talon_get_active_application_info: Returns detailed information about the active application and window.
    • talon_version_info: Returns Talon and OS version information.
    • talon_pretty_print and talon_pretty_format: Provide pretty-printing functionality.
    • talon_debug_app_windows: Pretty prints the windows of a given application.
    • talon_get_active_registry_list: Returns a list from the Talon registry.
  • talon_helpers.talon: This file provides voice commands to invoke the actions defined in talon_helpers.py. It includes commands for:
    • Opening Talon menus and windows (e.g., debug window, log).
    • Copying application context information.
    • Dumping various debug information to the console.
    • Simulating phrases.
    • Creating a new app context using the actions in create_app_context.py.
    • Opening the GitHub issues page for bug reports.
  • create_app_context.py: This file provides actions to create new application context files. It creates a new directory in the community/apps folder, with a .talon file and a .py file, and populates the files with boilerplate context code.

These files work together to provide a set of tools that can help developers create and debug Talon code. The Python files define the actions that can be performed, and the .talon file exposes those actions as voice commands. This allows developers to easily access and use these debugging tools from within Talon.

docs.title: text_navigation
docs.text:
The community/plugin/text_navigation directory provides a powerful set of tools for navigating and manipulating text using regular expressions and natural language commands. It allows users to move the cursor, select text, or perform actions like cutting or copying based on various criteria such as words, phrases, or predefined patterns.

The directory contains the following key files:

  • navigation_action.talon-list: This file defines a list of actions that can be performed on the text. These actions are GO, EXTEND, SELECT, DELETE, CUT, and COPY.

  • before_or_after.talon-list: This file defines the BEFORE and AFTER options used to specify where to position the cursor in relation to the navigation target.

  • text_navigation.py: This is the main Python file containing the core logic for text navigation. It defines the following:

    • text_navigation_max_line_search setting to control the maximum number of lines that the up and down navigation directions will search.
    • navigation_target_name list, which maps names like "word", "small", and "big" to regular expressions.
    • navigation_target capture, which converts user input into regular expressions used in the search.
    • navigation action, which is the core function for navigating through text. It takes the navigation action (GO, EXTEND, etc.), direction (up, down, left, right), the before_or_after position, and a regular expression as input, and then performs the corresponding action.
    • navigation_by_name action, which is a convenience action similar to navigation but it takes a named target like "word" instead of a regular expression.
    • Helper functions such as get_text_left, get_text_right, get_text_up, and get_text_down, which get the text around the cursor.
    • Functions to navigate and extend, such as go_right, go_left, extend_left, and extend_right.
    • Functions to find matches using a regular expression, match_backwards and match_forward.
    • Functions to handle different types of navigation action, such as handle_navigation_action, handle_select, handle_move, and handle_extend.

  • text_navigation.talon: This file defines the Talon commands and grammar for text navigation. It explains how to use the navigate command with a flexible syntax for various navigation actions. The file includes examples and descriptions of the grammar, along with a convenience command for selecting words, small words, and big words.

The files work together as follows:

  1. The user speaks a command using the syntax defined in text_navigation.talon, such as "navigate left second word".
  2. Talon parses the command using the grammar defined in text_navigation.talon, extracting the navigation action, direction, before/after specification, target, and occurrence number.
  3. Talon calls the user.navigation or user.navigation_by_name action defined in text_navigation.py, passing the parsed information.
  4. The navigation or navigation_by_name action uses the helper functions in text_navigation.py to retrieve text, find matches, and perform the requested action (GO, EXTEND, SELECT, etc.) in the specified direction.
  5. The action modifies the text or cursor position in the editor accordingly.
  6. The navigation_action.talon-list and before_or_after.talon-list provide the available options for the commands.

In summary, the text_navigation plugin enables users to navigate and manipulate text efficiently using natural language commands. The combination of Talon grammar, Python logic, and regular expressions provides a versatile and powerful tool for text editing.

docs.title: then
docs.text:
The community/plugin/then directory contains a single file, then.talon. This Talon file introduces a new command, "then", which serves as a hint to explicitly separate commands when chaining.

The primary purpose of then.talon is to disambiguate commands when chaining using speech. For example, a phrase like "post line air" could be interpreted as a single command or a sequence of two commands: "post line" and "air". Using "then" forces the latter interpretation. The command then uses the Talon command skip() which does nothing, but still acts as a barrier between commands.

docs.title: tags
docs.text:
The community/tags directory contains a collection of subdirectories, each defining a set of reusable voice commands and actions for specific types of applications or tasks. These subdirectories use a consistent pattern of .talon files for voice commands and .py files for defining the underlying logic and action interfaces.

Here's a breakdown of the most important subdirectories and how they work:

  • multiple_cursors: This directory provides voice commands for controlling multiple cursors in a text editor or IDE. The multiple_cursors.talon file defines the voice commands, while the multiple_cursors.py file defines the actions that an editor integration can implement.

  • line_commands: This directory provides commands for navigating and manipulating lines of text. The line_commands.talon file defines the voice commands, while line_commands.py implements the underlying actions for line navigation, selection, and manipulation.

  • messaging: This directory provides commands for interacting with multi-channel messaging applications. The messaging.py file defines the tag and the actions that are available when the tag is active. The messaging.talon file binds voice commands to the actions defined in messaging.py.

  • browser: This directory provides cross-platform browser functionality. The browser.py file defines the core browser actions. The browser_mac.py file provides macOS-specific implementations, and the browser.talon file provides the voice commands for these actions.

  • chapters: This directory contains files for implementing chapter navigation. The chapters.py file defines the actions and a chapters tag for activating the commands, while chapters.talon maps voice commands to those actions.

  • command_search: This directory provides a user command for searching and executing other commands by voice. The command_search.talon file defines the voice command, and command_search.py defines the command_search tag and the action called by the voice command.

  • file_manager: This directory provides a generic file manager interface. The file_manager.py file defines the core logic, including settings, actions, and list creation, as well as the GUI. The file_manager.talon file defines the voice commands.

  • pages: This directory defines a tag and actions for navigating pages in documents. The pages.talon file defines the voice commands, and pages.py defines the tag and actions.

  • find_and_replace: This directory provides find and replace functionality. The find_and_replace.py file defines the tag and the underlying actions, while find_and_replace.talon defines the voice commands.

  • terminal: This directory provides a generic terminal interface. The terminal.py file defines the core actions, while unix_shell.py and windows_shell.py implement those actions for their respective platforms. The readline.py provides readline functionality, and the terminal.talon file provides the voice commands. The unix_utilities.talon, unix_utilities.py, and unix_utility.talon-list files provide a way to insert common unix utility commands.

  • emoji: This directory provides a way to insert emoji, emoticons, and kaomoji. The emoji.talon-list, kaomoji.talon-list, and emoticon.talon-list files define the lists of names to text mappings, emoji.talon defines the voice commands, and emoji.py defines the tag and the lists.

  • address: This directory defines commands for applications with address bars. The address.talon file defines the voice commands for copying the address or navigating to an address. The address.py file defines the tag and the associated actions that must be implemented by an application for this functionality to work.

  • splits: This directory provides commands for managing window splits. The splits.talon file defines the voice commands, while the splits.py file defines the tag and the underlying actions.

  • find: This directory provides basic find commands. The find.py file defines the tag and find.talon defines the voice commands.

  • debugger: This directory provides a generic debugger interface. The debugger.talon file defines the voice commands and debugger.py defines the tag, registers, and the underlying actions.

The README.md file at the root of the community/tags directory (README.md) provides a summary of each of these subdirectories.

In general, each subdirectory in community/tags follows a pattern where a .py file defines a tag and actions, and a .talon file binds voice commands to those actions. This pattern promotes reusability and allows for the creation of a consistent set of voice commands across different types of applications. The actions are intended to be implemented by a plugin or other Talon script that is specific to the target application or context.

docs.title: address
docs.text:
The community/tags/address directory defines a tag and associated actions for applications with a mechanism to browse or navigate by address, such as an address bar or Finder's "Go to Folder" functionality.

The key components are:

  • address.talon: This file defines the Talon voice commands for interacting with applications that support the user.address tag. It provides commands to:
    • Navigate to an address using go [to] <user.address>.
    • Copy the current address using address copy | copy path | url copy | copy address | copy url.
    • Focus the address input field using address bar | go address | go url.
  • address.py: This file defines the address tag and the associated actions. It includes:
    • The address tag which can be used by applications to indicate they support address-based navigation.
    • A capture called address, which is intended to capture an address string and must be implemented by a context that desires to use the user.address tag.
    • Action class called Actions that declares functions for focusing the address input field, copying the current address, and navigating to an address. These actions need to be implemented by applications for the functionality to work.

In summary, this directory provides a set of common commands and actions for address-based navigation across applications, allowing users to interact with them through a unified interface. The address.talon file contains the voice commands that will be used by the user, and the address.py file contains the underlying Python code. The actual behavior of these actions is implemented by the specific application that adds this tag.

docs.title: browser
docs.text:
The community/tags/browser directory contains files that implement cross-platform browser functionality for Talon.

The file browser.py defines the core browser actions, including navigation, bookmarking, and tab management. It uses the browser tag to activate the context for these actions. It includes actions for: * address - extracts the URL from the window title. It can handle cases where the URL is the first or last token in the title, or enclosed in brackets. * bookmark, bookmark_tabs, bookmarks, and bookmarks_bar - handle bookmark management with standard keyboard shortcuts. * focus_address, focus_page, and focus_search - are for focusing various browser elements. * go_blank and go_home - for opening new and home pages, respectively. * go - navigates to a given URL. * go_back and go_forward - for navigation through the browser history. * open_private_window - to open a private browsing session. * reload and reload_hard - for refreshing the current page. * show_downloads, show_clear_cache, and show_history - for showing those browser functions. * submit_form - submits a form on a page. * toggle_dev_tools - to toggle the developer tools. Additionally, the file implements tab_jump and tab_final actions, which use ctrl on windows and alt on other platforms by default. It also defines a tab_duplicate action that duplicates a tab in a new tab. The address_focus and address_copy_address actions interact with the address bar. It defines a utility function is_url to validate a URL string. Finally it defines an action browser_open_address_in_new_tab to open a URL in a new tab.

The file browser_mac.py provides macOS-specific browser actions, extending the core actions in browser.py. It uses the same browser tag to activate the context for these actions, but only on macOS. It defines tab_jump and tab_final actions with cmd instead of alt or ctrl on other platforms. It reimplements the address action to use the macOS accessibility API to extract the address of the current tab. This file also includes mac-specific implementations of actions like bookmark, bookmark_tabs, bookmarks, and bookmarks_bar, focus_address, go_blank, go_home, go_back, go_forward, open_private_window, reload, reload_hard, show_downloads, show_clear_cache, show_history, and toggle_dev_tools.

The file browser.talon provides voice commands for the defined browser actions. It uses the browser tag to activate these commands, and also adds the user.address, user.find, and user.navigation tags. It provides voice commands for navigating in the browser, managing bookmarks, and showing various browser functions.

docs.title: chapters
docs.text:
The community/tags/chapters directory contains files for implementing chapter navigation in applications.

The core logic is in chapters.py, which defines a Talon module tag called chapters. This tag is used to activate the custom actions defined in the same file. The actions provide a basic chapter navigation interface: * chapter_current(): Returns the current chapter number. * chapter_next(): Jumps to the next chapter. * chapter_previous(): Jumps to the previous chapter. * chapter_jump(number: int): Jumps to a specific chapter number. * chapter_final(): Jumps to the final chapter.

The chapters.talon file binds voice commands to the actions defined in chapters.py. It maps phrases like "chapter next", "chapter last", "go chapter [number]", and "go chapter final" to the corresponding actions. For example, "chapter next" will execute user.chapter_next(). Note that to use this functionality, a user must implement an action to provide the current chapter number.

docs.title: command_search
docs.text:
The community/tags/command_search directory contains files that implement a user command for searching and executing other commands by voice.

The file command_search.talon defines the user.command_search tag, as well as the voice command please [<user.text>] that triggers the user.command_search() action. The optional <user.text> argument will be passed to the action as a string.

The file command_search.py defines the command_search tag, which is used to enable the voice command. It also defines an action command_search(command: str) that is called by the voice command from command_search.talon. The action currently takes a string and does nothing with it, but it is intended to be extended to perform the command search functionality.

docs.title: debugger
docs.text:
The community/tags/debugger directory contains files that define a generic debugger interface for use with Talon. This allows users to control a debugger using voice commands.

The core functionality is split between two files:

  • debugger.talon: This file defines the voice commands for interacting with a debugger. It uses the user.debugger_* actions defined in debugger.py. The commands are organized into categories such as code execution, breakpoints, register manipulation, navigation, memory inspection and type inspection. It also includes convenience commands like clearing the command line.

  • debugger.py: This file defines the debugger tag, the registers list, and the actions that are invoked by the voice commands in debugger.talon. It also contains a hardcoded list of x86 and x64 registers. The actions themselves do not implement any actual debugging logic; they are intended to be used by other Talon scripts that provide debugger-specific implementations. The registers list is used to allow the user to reference the common registers by name, for example, "get register stack".

Together, these files provide a reusable set of commands for controlling a debugger with Talon. The debugger.talon file provides the voice interface, and the debugger.py file defines the actions that those commands trigger. This allows for flexibility because each debugger can provide its own implementation of the actions without modifying the voice commands.

docs.title: emoji
docs.text:
The community/tags/emoji directory contains files that define a tag and lists that allow the user to easily insert emoji, emoticons, and kaomoji into text.

  • emoji.talon-list defines a list of emoji by their names. For example "happy" is mapped to "😀".
  • emoji.talon defines the user.emoji tag and three commands: emoticon, emoji, and kaomoji. The commands insert the corresponding item from the lists into the current text field.
  • kaomoji.talon-list defines a list of kaomoji by their names. For example, "happy" is mapped to "(* ^ ω ^)".
  • emoji.py defines the user.emoji tag as well as the user.emoticon, user.emoji, and user.kaomoji lists.
  • emoticon.talon-list defines a list of emoticons by their names. For example, "happy" is mapped to ":)".

The emoji.py file defines the tag user.emoji, which is used by the emoji.talon file, as well as the lists which are defined in the *.talon-list files. The lists define the specific mappings of names to text, and emoji.talon provides the command to insert them.

docs.title: file_manager
docs.text:
The community/tags/file_manager directory contains code for a generic file manager interface in Talon. It allows users to interact with file systems using voice commands, regardless of the underlying file manager application.

The core logic resides in file_manager.py. This file defines:

  • Settings: Several settings are available to control the behavior of the file manager, such as limits on the number of files and folders to iterate or display, and whether to show the file and folder pickers automatically.
  • Actions: A variety of actions that can be invoked through Talon commands, including:
    • file_manager_current_path(): Returns the current path of the active file manager.
    • file_manager_open_parent(): Navigates to the parent directory.
    • file_manager_open_directory(path): Opens a specified directory.
    • file_manager_select_directory(path): Selects a specified directory.
    • file_manager_new_folder(name): Creates a new folder.
    • file_manager_show_properties(): Shows the properties of a file or folder.
    • file_manager_terminal_here(): Opens a terminal at the current location.
    • file_manager_open_file(path): Opens a specified file.
    • file_manager_select_file(path): Selects a specified file.
    • file_manager_refresh_title(): Refreshes the window title to match the current directory.
    • file_manager_update_lists(): Forces an update of the file and directory lists.
    • file_manager_toggle_pickers(): Toggles the visibility of the file and folder pickers.
    • file_manager_hide_pickers(): Hides the file and folder pickers.
    • file_manager_get_directory_by_index(index): Returns a directory at the given index for the ImGui display.
    • file_manager_get_file_by_index(index): Returns a file at the given index for the ImGui display.
    • file_manager_next_file_page(): Navigates to the next page of files in the picker.
    • file_manager_previous_file_page(): Navigates to the previous page of files in the picker.
    • file_manager_next_folder_page(): Navigates to the next page of directories in the picker.
    • file_manager_previous_folder_page(): Navigates to the previous page of directories in the picker.
  • List Creation: Helper functions, get_directory_map and get_file_map, to generate lists of directories and files in the current path. These lists are used for voice command recognition.
  • ImGui Interface: Two ImGui windows gui_folders and gui_files are defined to display the available directories and files for selection. They also include buttons to manage the pickers.
  • Event Handling: The win_event_handler function uses Talon's event system to monitor window focus and title changes, triggering an update of the lists when the current path changes.

The file_manager.talon file defines the voice commands that trigger the actions in file_manager.py. It includes commands for:

  • Toggling the visibility of the file and folder pickers.
  • Navigating to the parent directory.
  • Opening and selecting files and directories using their spoken names or indices.
  • Creating new folders.
  • Showing file properties.
  • Opening a terminal in the current directory.
  • Navigating the pages of the file and folder pickers.

In summary, file_manager.py and file_manager.talon work together to provide a generic, voice-driven file management system. file_manager.py contains the logic for interacting with the file system, updating the lists of files and folders, and displaying them in a GUI. file_manager.talon provides a set of voice commands to interact with the logic in file_manager.py. The file manager uses Talon's context system, which allows it to be enabled only when the user.file_manager tag is active.

docs.title: find
docs.text:
The community/tags/find directory contains files that provide generic find commands for Talon.

The file find.py defines a Talon tag named find. This tag can be used to enable the commands defined in find.talon.

The file find.talon defines a series of voice commands that allow users to find and navigate text within an editor. These commands include:

  • hunt this: Opens the editor's find dialog.
  • hunt this pace: Opens the find dialog and pastes the current selection into it.
  • hunt this <user.text>: Opens the find dialog and enters the specified <user.text> as the search term.
  • hunt next: Navigates to the next search result.
  • hunt previous: Navigates to the previous search result.

By enabling the user.find tag (which is defined in find.talon), these commands become available in Talon.

docs.title: find_and_replace
docs.text:
The community/tags/find_and_replace directory contains files that add find and replace functionality to Talon.

The core logic is in find_and_replace.py. This file defines a Talon module named find_and_replace and a tag with the same name. The tag is used to enable the commands in the find_and_replace.talon file. The Python file also defines an Actions class with methods that provide the underlying functionality for find and replace operations. These methods do not implement the operations themselves; they are meant to be implemented by a plugin or other Talon script. They include:

  • find_everywhere(text: str): Finds text across the project.
  • find_toggle_match_by_case(): Toggles case sensitivity for the find operation.
  • find_toggle_match_by_word(): Toggles matching whole words only.
  • find_toggle_match_by_regex(): Toggles matching by regular expression.
  • replace(text: str): Replaces text in the active editor.
  • replace_everywhere(text: str): Replaces text across the entire project.
  • replace_confirm(): Confirms a replacement at the current position.
  • replace_confirm_all(): Confirms all replacements.
  • select_previous_occurrence(text: str): Selects the previous occurrence of the provided text.
  • select_next_occurrence(text: str): Selects the next occurrence of the provided text.

The find_and_replace.talon file defines the voice commands that trigger the actions defined in find_and_replace.py. It uses the user.find_and_replace tag to activate these commands. It includes commands for:

  • Finding text (hunt all, hunt all <user.text>, etc.).
  • Toggling find options (hunt case, hunt word, hunt expression).
  • Replacing text (replace this [<user.text>], replace all, replace <user.text> all).
  • Confirming replacements (replace confirm that, replace confirm all).
  • Performing quick actions such as clear, comment, go, paste, replace, and select with "last" or "next" occurrences of given text or the clipboard text. These commands are based on common commands in JetBrains IDEs.

The files work together as follows: The Python file defines the actions that can be taken to find and replace text. The Talon file provides voice commands that activate those actions, allowing the user to perform find and replace operations through speech. The user.find tag is also included in this script in order to provide basic find functionality.

docs.title: line_commands
docs.text:
The community/tags/line_commands directory contains files that define Talon commands for manipulating lines of text.

The file line_commands.talon defines a tag user.line_commands that enables a variety of line-based editing commands. These commands include:

  • Navigation:

    • lend and bend: Move the cursor to the end and beginning of the current line respectively, these have deprecation warnings that suggest replacing them with the "go line end" and "go line start" commands.
    • go <number>: Jump to the specified line number.
    • go <number> end: Jump to the specified line number and move the cursor to the end of the line.
    • select camel left and select camel right: Extend the selection by camel/subword to the left and right.
    • go camel left and go camel right: Moves the cursor to the left and right by camel case/subword.

  • Manipulation:

    • comment [line] <number>: Toggles comments on the specified line.
    • comment <number> until <number>: Toggles comments on the specified range of lines.
    • clear [line] <number>: Deletes the specified line.
    • clear <number> until <number>: Deletes the specified range of lines.
    • copy [line] <number>: Copies the specified line.
    • copy <number> until <number>: Copies the specified range of lines.
    • cut [line] <number>: Cuts the specified line.
    • cut [line] <number> until <number>: Cuts the specified range of lines.
    • (paste | replace) <number> until <number>: Pastes over the specified range of lines.
    • (select | cell | sell) [line] <number>: Selects the specified line.
    • (select | cell | sell) <number> until <number>: Selects the specified range of lines.
    • tab that: Indents the current line.
    • tab [line] <number>: Indents the specified line.
    • tab <number> until <number>: Indents the specified range of lines.
    • retab that: Unindents the current line.
    • retab [line] <number>: Unindents the specified line.
    • retab <number> until <number>: Unindents the specified range of lines.
    • drag [line] down and drag [line] up: Moves the current line down or up.
    • drag up [line] <number>: Moves the specified line up one line.
    • drag up <number> until <number>: Moves the specified range of lines up one line.
    • drag down [line] <number>: Moves the specified line down one line.
    • drag down <number> until <number>: Moves the specified range of lines down one line.
    • clone [line] <number>: Clones the specified line at the current position.

The file line_commands.py defines the actions used in the line_commands.talon file. It includes: * The line_commands tag definition. * Action extend_until_line(line: int): Extends the selection to the specified line. * Action select_range(line_start: int, line_end: int): Selects a range of lines. This action is used by many of the commands in line_commands.talon that accept a range of lines. * Actions extend_camel_left() and extend_camel_right(): Extend the selection by camel/subword to the left and right. * Actions camel_left() and camel_right(): Move the cursor by camel/subword to the left and right. * Action line_clone(line: int): Clones a line.

These files work together to provide a comprehensive set of commands for navigating and manipulating lines of text within a code editor or other text-based application using Talon voice control. The .talon file defines the voice commands and their associated actions, while the .py file implements those actions using the Talon API.

docs.title: messaging
docs.text:
The community/tags/messaging directory defines a Talon tag for controlling generic multi-channel messaging applications. It includes a Python file (messaging.py) that defines the tag and the actions that are available when the tag is active. It also has a Talon file (messaging.talon) that binds voice commands to those actions.

The messaging.py file defines the messaging tag and the messaging_actions class. The tag is declared with mod.tag("messaging", desc="Tag for generic multi-channel messaging apps"). This allows for the enabling of voice commands when this tag is active. The messaging_actions class defines methods such as messaging_workspace_previous and messaging_channel_next that are intended to be implemented by the specific application in their respective Talon files.

The messaging.talon file binds voice commands to the actions defined in messaging.py. For example, saying "previous workspace" will execute the user.messaging_workspace_previous() action. It provides voice commands for moving between workspaces, channels, and unread messages. It also has commands for opening search, marking messages as read, and uploading files.

docs.title: multiple_cursors
docs.text:
The community/tags/multiple_cursors directory defines a tag and actions for controlling multiple cursors in an editor.

The core functionality is split between two files:

  • multiple_cursors.talon: This file defines the Talon voice commands for manipulating multiple cursors. It uses the user.multiple_cursors tag and maps spoken phrases like "cursor multiple", "cursor up", and "cursor all" to the corresponding actions.
  • multiple_cursors.py: This file defines the multiple_cursors tag and the action class multiple_cursor_actions, which contains the logic for manipulating the multiple cursors. The actions in this file, such as multi_cursor_enable() and multi_cursor_add_above(), are called by the voice commands defined in multiple_cursors.talon. These actions are expected to be implemented by an editor integration.

In short, the .talon file provides the user-facing voice commands, while the .py file defines the tag and action interfaces that an editor plugin can use to implement the functionality.

docs.title: pages
docs.text:
The community/tags/pages directory defines a tag and actions for navigating pages in documents. It includes two files: pages.talon and pages.py.

pages.talon defines the voice commands for page navigation using the user.pages tag, which is defined in pages.py. The following commands are available:

  • page next: Navigates to the next page.
  • page last: Navigates to the previous page.
  • go page <number>: Navigates to the specified page number.
  • go page final: Navigates to the last page.
  • rotate right: Rotates the document 90 degrees to the right.
  • rotate left: Rotates the document 90 degrees to the left.

pages.py defines the user.pages tag, and a set of actions used by the voice commands defined in pages.talon. The actions provided are:

  • page_current: Returns the current page number.
  • page_next: Navigates to the next page by using page_jump.
  • page_previous: Navigates to the previous page by using page_jump.
  • page_jump: Navigates to a specified page number.
  • page_final: Navigates to the final page.
  • page_rotate_right: Rotates the document 90 degrees to the right.
  • page_rotate_left: Rotates the document 90 degrees to the left.

The actions in pages.py are intended to be implemented by a specific application or context. The pages tag and associated voice commands can be used to navigate any document or interface where page navigation is applicable.

docs.title: splits
docs.text:
The community/tags/splits directory provides a set of commands for managing window splits within applications. It includes two primary files: splits.talon and splits.py.

  • splits.talon defines the voice commands for interacting with window splits. It uses the user.splits tag to activate the commands. The commands allow you to perform actions like moving the active window to a different split, splitting a window, flipping a split, maximizing a split, resetting splits, clearing splits, and navigating between splits using a number.

  • splits.py defines the actions that are performed when the voice commands in splits.talon are called. It defines the user.splits tag and implements a class Actions with methods for moving a window to a specific split, splitting the window in various ways, flipping the orientation of the active split, maximizing a split, resetting splits, clearing splits, and navigating between splits using an index.

docs.title: terminal
docs.text:
The community/tags/terminal directory contains files for interacting with terminal applications. It provides a set of actions for common terminal operations like navigating directories, running commands, and manipulating text. It also includes support for common unix utilities and readline shortcuts.

The core functionality is defined in terminal.py. This file defines an action class with methods such as terminal_list_directories, terminal_change_directory, terminal_clear_screen, etc. These methods provide an abstraction layer over terminal commands, allowing different operating systems and shells to implement them differently.

unix_shell.py and windows_shell.py implement these actions for Unix-like and Windows-like shells, respectively. They use the insert and key actions to send appropriate shell commands. For example, terminal_list_directories is implemented as actions.insert("ls"); actions.key("enter") in unix_shell.py and actions.insert("ls"); actions.key("enter") in windows_shell.py. Note that the implementation for listing all directories differs between the two with ls -a for unix and ls -force for windows.

The readline.py file provides implementations for editing text using readline shortcuts like ctrl-a (beginning of line), alt-b (word left), and ctrl-u (delete line). These actions are made available when the user.readline tag is active.

The terminal.talon file defines voice commands for the actions in terminal.py. For example, the command lisa will execute user.terminal_list_directories(), and katie up will execute user.terminal_change_directory(".."). It also includes some common commands like copy paste for copying and pasting.

The files related to unix utilities, unix_utilities.talon, unix_utilities.py, and unix_utility.talon-list provide a way to insert common unix utility commands. The unix_utility.talon-list file defines a list of commands (e.g. grep, awk, ls, cd) that can be invoked with the core {user.unix_utility} command. For example, saying "core grab" will insert "grep ". This functionality is enabled by the user.unix_utilities tag.

In summary, this directory provides a comprehensive set of tools for interacting with terminals via voice, with actions for common terminal tasks and text editing, as well as support for common unix utilities.

docs.title: test
docs.text:
The community/test directory contains files and subdirectories used for testing the Talon Community repository. The most important subdirectory is stubs.

The stubs subdirectory contains stub implementations of the Talon API, allowing tests to run without a full Talon environment. This is crucial for testing the code base in isolation.

The stubs/talon subdirectory is particularly important because it contains the core stubs for Talon's API. The __init__.py file defines classes and global variables that mimic Talon's core components. Key classes include Actions, Module, Context, ImgUI, UI, Settings, Registry, Resource, and App. These classes allow tests to simulate the behavior of Talon's action system, modules, contexts, graphical interfaces, settings, registry, resources, and application. It also provides the test_mode variable, which is set to True to indicate the test environment. The grammar.py file provides a stub for the Phrase class, which is used in Talon's grammar system. The stubs/talon/experimental subdirectory contains type stubs for the experimental Talon API, with textarea.py providing type hints for the textarea API.

The test files in community/test directory use these stubs to test different aspects of the Talon Community code.

  • test_formatters.py tests the text formatting functionality, ensuring that text can be transformed into various formats such as snake case, capitalized words, etc.. It uses the stubbed actions to register test actions that it uses to call the functions being tested.

  • test_dictation.py tests the dictation formatting logic, verifying that spaces and capitalization are applied correctly based on context.

  • test_create_spoken_forms.py tests the functionality that creates the different spoken forms of text used in the command system. It uses a mocked version of core.user_settings.track_csv_list to simulate loading settings from CSV files. This test file also replaces actions.user with a mocked version which it then uses to call functions.

  • test_code_modified_function.py tests the code_modified_function action. This action is used to call code formatting actions based on a set of modifiers like "public", "static", or "private". It registers test actions and uses mocks to ensure it calls the correct code formatting actions given modifiers.

These test files, along with the stubbed Talon API in stubs, allow for isolated testing of the core functionality of the Talon Community repository.

docs.title: stubs
docs.text:
The community/test/stubs directory contains stub implementations of the Talon API for use in testing. These stubs allow tests to run without depending on the full Talon runtime environment. The stubs are made available by pytest's pythonpath configuration as described in the README.

The community/test/stubs/talon subdirectory contains the core stubs. The most important file is __init__.py. This file defines classes that mimic the behavior of Talon's core components, including:

  • Actions: This class implements a way to register and call actions, which is how Talon performs operations based on speech commands or other triggers. It provides methods to register both module-level actions, intended to be persistent and used by modules, and test-level actions, that are used for mocking behavior in tests.
  • Module: This class implements a subset of Talon's Module class, which is used to define Talon modules, including actions, lists, and settings.
  • Context: This class implements a stub of Talon's Context class, which allows actions and lists to be activated only under specific conditions, like when a particular application is in focus.
  • ImgUI and UI: These classes provide a way for tests to use graphical interfaces without depending on a running graphical environment.
  • Settings: A stub of the Talon settings object.
  • Registry: A stub of the Talon registry.
  • Resource: A stub of the Talon resource system.
  • App: A stub of the Talon app variable.

This file also defines several global variables that represent common Talon objects like actions, settings, and the app variable, and also provides the test_mode variable, which is set to True to indicate the test environment.

The grammar.py file provides stubs for the Phrase class, which is used in Talon's grammar system. This stub class allows testing to behave correctly when used with functions that check isinstance(some_input, Phrase).

The experimental subdirectory contains stubs for the experimental Talon API. Currently, only experimental/README.md and the file experimental/textarea.py are included. The textarea.py file provides type hints for the experimental textarea API by assigning None to the following names: TextArea, Span, DarkThemeLabels, and LightThemeLabels.

docs.title: talon
docs.text:
The community/test/stubs/talon directory provides stub implementations of the Talon API for use in testing. These stubs allow tests to run without depending on the full Talon runtime environment.

The most important file in this directory is __init__.py. This file defines classes that mimic the behavior of Talon's core components, including:

  • Actions: This class implements a way to register and call actions, which is how Talon performs operations based on speech commands or other triggers. It provides methods to register both module-level actions, intended to be persistent and used by modules, and test-level actions, that are used for mocking behavior in tests.
  • Module: This class implements a subset of Talon's Module class, which is used to define Talon modules, including actions, lists, and settings.
  • Context: This class implements a stub of Talon's Context class, which allows actions and lists to be activated only under specific conditions, like when a particular application is in focus.
  • ImgUI and UI: These classes provide a way for tests to use graphical interfaces without depending on a running graphical environment.
  • Settings: A stub of the Talon settings object.
  • Registry: A stub of the Talon registry.
  • Resource: A stub of the Talon resource system.
  • App: A stub of the Talon app variable.

This file also defines several global variables that represent common Talon objects like actions, settings, and the app variable, and also provides the test_mode variable, which is set to True to indicate the test environment.

The grammar.py file provides stubs for the Phrase class, which is used in Talon's grammar system. This stub class allows testing to behave correctly when used with functions that check isinstance(some_input, Phrase).

The experimental subdirectory contains stubs for the experimental Talon API. Currently, only experimental/README.md and the file experimental/textarea.py are included. The textarea.py file provides type hints for the experimental textarea API.

docs.title: experimental
docs.text:
The community/test/stubs/talon/experimental directory contains type stubs for experimental Talon features. These stubs allow type checkers to understand the experimental API, enabling more robust development.

The file textarea.py defines type hints for the experimental textarea API. This file provides None assignments for the following:

  • TextArea
  • Span
  • DarkThemeLabels
  • LightThemeLabels

These assignments allow type checkers to understand that the referenced symbols are defined elsewhere, while avoiding a dependency on the Talon runtime during static analysis.
